source.title: Add structured tool-contract playground and inline snippet runner
source.author: Ava Chen
source.release_notes: Adds a new interactive docs playground for authoring structured tool contracts; includes an inline snippet runner and telemetry probes. Backwards-compatible docs-only change.
commits: feat(docs): add playground page and interactive runner
chore: wire telemetry probes and lightweight CI smoke test
feat(examples): include two sample tool contracts and compact README
fix(docs): tweak layout for mobile preview
ci: relax flaky puppeteer timeout and rerun failing job
source.description:
Context and motivation:
This PR introduces a compact, interactive playground in the docs site for authoring and validating structured tool contracts used by RAG agents. Our goal is to lower the onboarding time for integrators and to make it trivial to iterate on tool schemas and example snippets while seeing live telemetry for invocation patterns. The playground targets: engineers prototyping tool contracts, solutions engineers demonstrating integrations, and docs authors writing reproducible examples.

What this change contains (single-string summary):
- New docs page at /playgrounds/structured-tool-contract with an embedded snippet runner that executes sample contracts in a sandboxed iframe and shows structured validation feedback.
- Snippet runner JS (site/static/js/snippet-runner.js) implements a short-lived local shim that validates contract JSON and emits telemetry probes to telemetry/probes/tool-contract-playground-probes.yaml.
- Two example tool contracts in examples/tool-contracts/ with annotated comments and a tiny README showing best practices (edge cases for optional params, param typing, and permission hints).
- CI addition to run a lightweight Puppeteer smoke job that loads the playground and asserts the validation pane renders.

Checklist:
- [x] Docs page with inline examples and schema reference
- [x] Example contracts and README
- [x] Snippet runner with basic sandbox validation and UX affordances
- [x] Telemetry probes to help us instrument sample usage patterns
- [x] CI smoke test to reduce regressions

How to run locally (developer notes):
1) yarn; yarn build:docs; yarn start:docs-local
2) Open http://localhost:3000/playgrounds/structured-tool-contract
3) Use the left panel to paste or load example-tool-contract.json; hit Run to validate and simulate a call.
4) Telemetry events are emitted to the local dev logger (see README section) or to the telemetry dev endpoint when using a configured key.

Key design decisions and rationale:
- We keep validation deterministic and side-effect free: the snippet runner does not call external endpoints by default; it simulates responses according to the contract spec so users can iterate without credentials.
- Telemetry probes are intentionally coarse-grained (invocations, validation_errors, snippet_exec_duration_ms) to avoid PII and to keep event volume low.
- The runner is implemented as a minimal JS shim (no backend) so the docs site remains self-contained and deployable to static hosting.

Testing and CI notes:
- CI initially showed a flaky Puppeteer job (timeout on CI step 'playground-render'), I relaxed the timeout and re-ran; final status: pass.
- Unit tests added for JSON schema validation logic (examples/tool-contracts/validation.test.js).

Review conversation (high-level extract):
- Diego Morales: "Nice UX; can we show an explicit schema diff when the runner auto-fixes an obvious typing issue?"
- Ava Chen (author): "Added a compact 'auto-fix suggestions' panel and a small note that fixes are opt-in; included in latest patch."
- Priya Nair: "Telemetry looks reasonable, please add sampling on the exec_duration metric."
- Ava Chen: "Added 10% sampling and documented it in probes yaml (commented)."
- Luca Bianchi: "CI flake observed; recommends increasing Puppeteer timeout and adding a retry. Approved after fixes."

Merge outcome and follow-ups:
- Merged using squash/merge on 2026-03-07 after approvals from Priya and Luca and successful CI.
- Follow-up work: ENG-4821: wire production telemetry ingestion pipeline to support playground signals; DOC-99: expand the playground with a multi-tool scenario and live mock responses.

Files changed (high-level snippets):
- docs/playgrounds/structured-tool-contract.md: includes the embedded runner iframe and a short walkthrough with code samples. Example snippet: `{"name":"lookup_customer","params":{"customer_id":"string"}}`
- site/static/js/snippet-runner.js: ~220 lines implementing schema validation, simulated response generation, and a small event emitter to surface telemetry.
- examples/tool-contracts/sample-tool-contract.json: two contract examples (sync lookup and async streaming notify).
- telemetry/probes/tool-contract-playground-probes.yaml: defines event names and sampling rules (comments included).

Notes for maintainers:
- This is docs-first and non-breaking. If you want to iterate on UX, update the static JS and test via the local docs server.
- If telemetry sampling is changed, please coordinate with the platform telemetry team to avoid sudden ingest spikes.

If there are any additional reviewers we should ping (security for telemetry, or platform for ingestion), I can open follow-up tasks. Thanks to the reviewers for quick turnarounds and to SRE for helping stabilize the CI job.

source.title: Multi-locale RAG quickstart and i18n evaluation playbook
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.release_notes: Multi-locale RAG quickstart (TS + Python); i18n evaluation playbook and sample locale datasets; docs nav and troubleshooting for non-Latin tokenization/embeddings
source.content:
Title: Multi-locale Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) quickstart and internationalization (i18n) evaluation playbook.

Summary: This PR introduces a focused quickstart and evaluation playbook for teams building RAG experiences that support multiple locales (languages, scripts, and regional variants). It includes two example integrations (TypeScript and Python), a per-locale embedding guidance section, a small set of curated locale test datasets for evaluation, docs site navigation updates, troubleshooting notes for tokenization/encoding edge cases, and a lightweight harness to measure retrieval + generation quality per locale. The goal is to reduce time-to-value for customers who need multilingual RAG with production-grade evaluation and practical guidance on commons issues (e.g., punctuation differences, right-to-left languages, and tokenizer idiosyncrasies).

Motivation & context: We get frequent requests from platform customers and partners about language coverage and correctness for non-English workloads. Existing quickstarts focus on English-first flows. This PR addresses gaps identified in ENG-2143 and recent partner feedback (SUP-452) by providing: (1) example code that demonstrates locale-aware indexing and prompt scaffolding, (2) eval playbooks for measuring per-locale precision and hallucination rates, (3) recipes for embedding model selection and normalization per script, and (4) how-to for fallback routing and translation proxies.

Checklist:
- [x] Add TypeScript quickstart: examples/i18n-quickstart/ts/{app,docs}.
- [x] Add Python quickstart: examples/i18n-quickstart/py/{worker,notebook}.
- [x] Add i18n evaluation playbook: docs/guides/i18n-evals.md.
- [x] Add sample locale datasets (small): data/i18n-samples/{ja,ar,es,ru,hi}.jsonl.
- [x] Update docs site nav and quickstart index.
- [x] Add tests for locale-aware tokenizer smoke checks (CI).
- [x] Add migration notes and troubleshooting doc.

Commits (high level):
- c1a2b3: scaffold i18n quickstart structure and README for TS example.
- d4e5f6: implement Python worker example and notebook showing end-to-end indexing+query.
- a7b8c9: add i18n evaluation playbook and small sample datasets, with expected metrics baseline.
- e0f1g2: docs site navigation and search indexing updated to include i18n guide.
- h3i4j5: add CI smoke tests for tokenizer/encoding and small test harness to run evals in GH Actions.

Files changed (high level):
- examples/i18n-quickstart/ts/README.md (new): step-by-step quickstart for TS, highlights locale-aware prompt templates, and demonstrates embeddings normalization pipeline.
- examples/i18n-quickstart/ts/sample-app.ts (new): minimal server that indexes locale-specific docs and performs RAG queries with per-locale prompt templates.
- examples/i18n-quickstart/py/worker.py (new): background indexer showing language detection, normalizer, and embedding model selection.
- examples/i18n-quickstart/py/notebook.ipynb (new): walkthrough for local experimentation and eval plotting.
- docs/guides/i18n-evals.md (new): playbook describing metrics (locale-precision, subset-ROUGE, hallucination-rate), test composition, and how to interpret regressions.
- docs/quickstarts/index.md (updated): added link to the new quickstart and short blurb on language coverage.
- data/i18n-samples/{ja.jsonl,ar.jsonl,es.jsonl,ru.jsonl,hi.jsonl} (new): small curated retrieval prompts and gold answers per locale for smoke evaluation.
- .github/workflows/i18n-smoke.yml (new): CI job to run lightweight tokenizer/eval checks on PRs touching i18n files.
- tests/tokenizer_smoke_test.py (new): language tokenization sanity checks.
- docs/troubleshooting/i18n.md (new): common pitfalls and how to troubleshoot tokenization mismatches and embedding drift.

Notes on implementation details and choices:
- Embedding normalization: examples recommend normalizing punctuation (NFKC) and mapping full-width/half-width characters for East Asian scripts before indexing. The playbook explains when to apply model-based translation vs. embedding translation.
- Tokenization edge cases: documents include a short matrix highlighting tokenization differences and approximate token cost implications for common languages.
- Locale routing: sample-app.ts includes a small strategy function to route requests to regionally-optimized model variants and to fallback to translation proxies when retrieval quality is low.
- Evaluation harness: docs/guides/i18n-evals.md contains a recommended baseline for acceptance (per-locale precision >= 0.75 on curated set) and instructions to add more locale data to CI for enterprise customers.

Review conversation (condensed):
- Jordan Lee (reviewer): "Nice to see datasets included — can we keep the sample corpora deliberately small to avoid licensing issues? Also add a note about character normalization in the quickstart."
- Priya (author): "Yes — all samples are synthetic or public-domain; I added the normalization section and an explicit note about dataset provenance in the README."
- Mateo Alvarez (reviewer): "The playbook metrics look good, but suggest adding a short script to compute hallucination-rate automatically. Otherwise teams will interpret metrics differently."
- Priya: "Added scripts/eval/hallucination_checker.py and documented how it works and its failure modes."
- Sofia Chen (reviewer): "CI job failed first run due to flaky tokenizer expectation on macOS runners — we should pin the environment."
- Priya: "Refactored the smoke test to avoid platform-specific byte encoding assumptions and added GH Actions matrix to pin python 3.10 linux runner."
- Jordan Lee: "Approved after the CI rerun. Good to merge with squash."

CI and validation: The PR triggered a new GH Actions job (i18n-smoke) which initially failed due to platform encoding assumptions; after the fix all checks passed. The final CI status is: pass (unit + smoke + lint). A subset of the eval scripts are intentionally light so they run quickly in PRs; the full playbook recommends running the larger suite in nightly pipelines.

Backward compatibility and migration: This change is additive (docs and examples only). No runtime or API surface changes. Marking as non-breaking.

Testing notes: Included a few deterministic smoke tests and an example notebook with reproducible plots showing per-locale retrieval precision and a notebook section for manual qualitative checks. The sample datasets are intentionally small (~50 examples per locale) and are meant as templates for teams to expand.

Merge outcome: Squash and merge to main on 2026-03-02 after approvals. Release notes and docs redirects have been queued for the next docs site build.

Follow-ups (suggested):
- Add end-to-end performance notes for multilingual tokenization and cost implications (ENG follow-up ticket suggested).
- Expand datasets for additional locales (Vietnamese, Thai) and add RTL visual test harnesses for UI teams.
- Consider a small partner-facing workshop to validate recommendations on real-world corpora.

If anything needs to be reverted quickly: the PR is docs/examples only; revert would remove quickstart and playbook from the site and example manifests.

Contact: Priya Natarajan (author) for follow-up; Mateo Alvarez for eval playbook questions.

title: Multi-locale RAG quickstart and i18n evaluation playbook

source.title: Add compact authoring checkpoints and telemetry-guided RAG templates
source.author: Maya Thompson
source.description: Motivation: customers asked for smaller, repeatable authoring artifacts that capture stable checkpoints for RAG authoring workflows and enable telemetry-driven regressions during prompt/index changes. This PR adds a compact 'checkpoint' template format, new telemetry-guided assertion examples, and an embedded authoring devcontainer/watch-mode recipe so engineers can iterate locally against seeded indexes. What this includes: 1) new compact checkpoint schema and examples (YAML+JSON) that capture prompt seeds, index snapshot pointers, and golden-response assertions; 2) a telemetry-guided assertions guide that maps telemetry signals (token-latency, rerank-quality, recall-window) to test thresholds and suggested remediation; 3) two new runnable samples: typescript/mini-agent and python/authoring-worker that demonstrate checkpoint-driven test runs and automatic rollback hooks; 4) a minimal devcontainer and a lightweight file-watcher that automatically runs the checkpoint harness on file changes; 5) an authoring VS Code extension manifest (samples only) that surfaces checkpoint failures inline and links to telemetry dashboards. Checklist: - [x] Add checkpoint schema and examples - [x] Add step-by-step guide for telemetry assertions - [x] Add TS and Python samples with README how-to - [x] Add devcontainer + watch-mode recipe - [x] Add tests for the harness smoke flow - [x] Docs lint and build passed How to validate: follow the README in docs/rag-authoring/checkpoints to run the devcontainer, create a checkpoint, and run `python harness.py --checkpoint ./samples/checkpoints/minimal.yaml`. Related work: follows ENG-8945 (authoring stability) and references the RAG telemetry taxonomy doc. Migration notes: existing RAG tutorials are unaffected; checkpoint templates are opt-in. This PR is documentation and samples only; no runtime changes to production serving are included.
source.commits: 4 commits: (1) add checkpoint schema and examples; (2) telemetry-guided assertions doc + thresholds; (3) add TS/Python sample harness and readmes; (4) devcontainer and watch-mode script; commit messages include small fixes to examples and CI metadata.
source.review_thread:
Ethan Park (reviewer): 'Nice direction, appreciate the opt-in behavior. Can you add a short section about index compatibility and recommended index snapshot retention policies?'
Maya Thompson (author): 'Added a section in telemetry-guided-assertions.md that recommends keeping the index snapshot for 30 days for fast rollbacks, and 90 days for regulated archives. Also added a short table of compatibility expectations.'
Miguel Alvarez (reviewer): 'The typescript sample imports a now-deprecated SDK call. Please replace with the new streaming API and add a test harness to the TS CI job.'
Maya Thompson: 'Replaced the call with streaming-compatible stub and added a smoke test in ci/doc-lint.yml that runs the TS sample in Node 18. Kept the sample intentionally minimal to avoid pulling core SDK runtime deps.'
Sara Liu (reviewer, final): 'Docs look good; linter clean. Approving pending CI pass.'
CI: initial doc-lint job failed on linkcheck -> fixed broken link in README -> re-run -> pass.
source.merge_outcome: Merged by squash via web UI. Commit message: 'docs: authoring checkpoints + telemetry assertions (ENG-8945)'. Backported: none. No prod runtime changes. Merge blocked briefly by CI linkcheck failure which was corrected in a follow-up commit.
source.release_notes: Introduce opt-in compact authoring checkpoints for RAG workflows, plus telemetry-guided assertion recipes and local devcontainer/watch-mode for faster iteration. Samples added: python harness and typescript mini-agent. No breaking changes.
source.notes_for_ops: This change only affects docs and examples; no operational rollout required. Telemetry thresholds in the docs are recommendations and should be tuned against production signals before automation is enabled.
source.testing_instructions: See examples/rag-harness/python/README.md and examples/rag-harness/ts/README.md. Quick verify: start devcontainer, run ./tools/watchers/checkpoint-watcher.sh on the samples folder, then modify samples/checkpoints/minimal.yaml and observe harness smoke tests passing.

source.title: Add RAG synthetic corpus authoring lab and docs
source.author: Ava Chen
source.description:
Motivation: teams building Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) workflows often lack reproducible, small-scale corpora and a compact authoring loop to iterate on indexing/chunking/labeling. This PR introduces a focused "authoring lab" in the docs site: a guided walkthrough that teaches how to (1) synthesize representative corpora from heterogeneous sources, (2) apply chunking and metadata strategies, (3) run local smoke-evals against a typed prompt set, and (4) surface telemetry to detect dataset drift. The authoring lab contains conceptual guidance, a step-by-step tutorial, runnable examples (Python and TypeScript), and a tiny browser-hosted runner for manual experimentation.

What changed (high level): added a new docs page at /docs/rag/authoring/synthetic-corpora-lab with a compact tutorial, two language examples under docs/rag/authoring/examples, a portable JS runner in site/static/playgrounds to demo sampler behavior in the browser, an image diagram, and a CI smoke workflow to exercise the examples on PRs. The doc includes a short checklist for productionizing synthetic corpora (legal/PII review, provenance tags, sampling seeds). The tutorial is lightweight and intended to be copy-paste friendly for product engineers and platform teams.

Checklist: - [x] Draft tutorial text and diagrams - [x] Add Python example (notebook + small script) - [x] Add TypeScript example (ts + run script) - [x] Add browser runner demo - [x] Add CI smoke pipeline to run basic examples - [x] Add telemetry probes guide and sample metrics queries - [x] Run lint and docs build locally

Testing and CI: the new CI job (ci/smoke/synth-corpus-smoke.yml) runs a small matrix: python example smoke test, ts build + node run, and a docs build to ensure the new page compiles. CI passed in the last run. I validated the python notebook outputs locally and exercised the browser-runner in Chrome. The smoke tests are intentionally very small (a few synthetic documents) so they are cheap and fast.

Docs UX notes: the page is authored as a hands-on lab with collapsible code blocks and small executable snippets. Code samples are written to be copy-paste runnable with minimal dependencies (requests/openai-client placeholders, local LightIndexer shim). The page also references the existing "RAG authoring checklist" and links back to the retrieval tuning guide.

Backward compatibility / breaking changes: none. This is documentation and examples only; there are no runtime or API changes.

Related: ENG-4921 (improve retrieval authoring flows), DOC-1285 (docs lab initiative), SUP-3210 (customer request for reproducible corpora guidance).
commits: a1f3c9d: initial lab outline and tutorial scaffold
b7e2f11: add python synth_corpus example and notebook (examples/python/synth_corpus.py, notebook.ipynb)
c3d4e22: add typescript example and run script (examples/ts/synth-corpora.ts)
d5f6b33: add browser playground runner and image assets (site/static/playgrounds/synth-runner.js, assets/images/rag-synth-diagram.svg)
e7a8c44: add ci smoke workflow and docs sidebar entry, fix typos and expand troubleshooting notes
review_comments: Markus Lavoie (2026-02-21): Nice tutorial — can we add a short section that explains tradeoffs between overlapping chunks vs fixed-size chunks for reranking workloads? Suggested: one paragraph + a small example config.
Ava Chen (2026-02-21): Thanks Markus — added a 'chunking tradeoffs' subsection with config snippet and an example of when overlap helps (QA) vs when it hurts (latency-sensitive rerank).

Priya Nair (2026-02-21): CI looked green but the TS example uses an npm package that isn't pinned. Can you pin to a patch version in package.json or add a note? Also, add a quick troubleshooting note about Node 18 vs 20 behavior.
Ava Chen (2026-02-21): Pinned the dependency and added troubleshooting note. Verified on Node 18 and 20 in CI matrix.
Diego Morales (2026-02-22): telemetry section is great — one nit: show an example metric query for token distribution change over time (Grafana/PromQL style) so platform teams can copy it.
Ava Chen (2026-02-22): Added a PromQL snippet and a short explanation of what to watch (median token length, unique id cardinality).
source.release_notes: Adds a new RAG authoring lab (synthetic corpora) with runnable Python/TypeScript examples, a browser playground runner, CI smoke tests, and a short telemetry guide to detect dataset drift.

title: Add RAG synthetic corpus authoring lab and docs
preview:
Motivation: teams building Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) workflows often lack reproducible, small-scale corpora and a compact authoring loop to iterate on indexing/chunking/labeling. This PR introduces a focused "authoring lab" in the docs site: a guided walkthrough that teaches how to (1) synthesize representative corpora from heterogeneous sources, (2) apply chunking and metadata strategies, (3) run local smoke-evals against a typed prompt set, and (4) surface telemetry to detect dataset drift. The authoring lab contains conceptual guidance, a step-by-step tutorial, runnable examples (Python and TypeScript), and a tiny browser-hosted runner for manual experimentation.

What changed (high level): added a new docs page at /docs/rag/authoring/synthetic-corpora-lab with a compact tutorial, two language examples under docs/rag/authoring/examples, a portable JS runner in site/static/playgrounds to demo sampler behavior in the browser, an image diagram, and a CI smoke workflow to exercise the examples on PRs. The doc includes a short checklist for productionizing synthetic corpora (legal/PII review, provenance tags, sampling seeds). The tutorial is lightweight and intended to be copy-paste friendly for product engineers and platform teams.

Checklist: - [x] Draft tutorial text and diagrams - [x] Add Python example (notebook + small script) - [x] Add TypeScript example (ts + run script) - [x] Add browser runner demo - [x] Add CI smoke pipeline to run basic examples - [x] Add telemetry probes guide and sample metrics queries - [x] Run lint and docs build locally

Testing and CI: the new CI job (ci/smoke/synth-corpus-smoke.yml) runs a small matrix: python example smoke test, ts build + node run, and a docs build to ensure the new page compiles. CI passed in the last run. I validated the python notebook outputs locally and exercised the browser-runner in Chrome. The smoke tests are intentionally very small (a few synthetic documents) so they are cheap and fast.

Docs UX notes: the page is authored as a hands-on lab with collapsible code blocks and small executable snippets. Code samples are written to be copy-paste runnable with minimal dependencies (requests/openai-client placeholders, local LightIndexer shim). The page also references the existing "RAG authoring checklist" and links back to the retrieval tuning guide.

Backward compatibility / breaking changes: none. This is documentation and examples only; there are no runtime or API changes.

Related: ENG-4921 (improve retrieval authoring flows), DOC-1285 (docs lab initiative), SUP-3210 (customer request for reproducible corpora guidance).

source.title: Document Optimize quality gates, results interpretation, and override workflow
source.author: Elliot Price
source.description: This PR adds first-pass internal/external documentation for Optimize “quality guardrails gating” to ensure cost-saving configuration recommendations (batching, caching, quantization profiles) cannot be applied to production traffic without passing quality evaluation gates (or a time-bound, auditable override). The docs target both self-serve users (what you’ll see in Console, how to interpret pass/fail) and enterprise/security reviewers (audit trail, RBAC, override controls). Sections included: (1) Overview: what quality gates are and what changes they apply to (batching policy updates, prefix/KV caching settings, quantization profile switches). (2) Gate lifecycle: Proposed → Gates pending/running → Passed/Failed → Apply allowed/blocked → Rollout canary coupling. (3) Results interpretation: metrics shown, baseline selection, how to read deltas, common failure reasons (sample size, flakiness, task success regression, rubric regression). (4) Overrides: who can approve, how to request, required evidence, expiration, and how it appears in audit logs; includes warning copy for UI parity. (5) Operational guidance: what to do when a gate fails (rerun vs investigate), and pointers to runbooks and dashboards. (6) Known limitations for limited rollout (hosted API first; Dedicated defaults next; Private workflow manual) and expected timeline. This PR does not change product behavior; it documents the behavior implemented in redwood + eval-harness + observability-pack PRs referenced below.
commits: docs(optimize): add overview page for quality gates and supported change types
docs(console): document gate status UI states and failure reason strings
docs(policy): add override request/approval flow and expiration semantics
docs/security): add audit log events + RBAC notes for overrides
docs(runbooks): link gate failure runbook + flake quarantine guidance
chore: add glossary and terminology alignment (gate run, baseline, canary)
review_conversation: Aditya Rao (Product): Can we be explicit that gates are mandatory for one-click apply AND auto-apply? Also call out which changes are smoke-gated vs full-gated in MVP. Elliot Price (Author): Updated Overview + FAQ: clarified enforcement at apply boundary, and added a table mapping config types to smoke/full gates for MVP (batching/cache: smoke then full; quantization: full gates required).

Jonas Weber (Evals): Please avoid implying gates guarantee ‘no regressions’—we should say ‘reduces risk’ and mention variance/min sample sizes. Also add note about rerun policy when flakiness is detected. Elliot Price (Author): Adjusted language across pages to ‘risk-reducing’ and added ‘Statistical notes’ callout with min sample sizes + confidence handling; added rerun + quarantine notes with links to flake workflow.

Allison Grant (SRE/Observability): The alerting guidance should emphasize avoiding paging on single failures; point to the dashboard + flake rate metric. Also include a ‘gate execution latency’ expectation so customers don’t assume instant. Elliot Price (Author): Updated Observability page: added recommended alert thresholds (rate-based) and a section on gate execution latency expectations and what drives it (GPU availability, suite size).

Marcus Lin (Security): Override docs must mention audit trail fields (who/when/why/expiration) and RBAC role naming. Also explicitly state overrides are time-bound and require justification. Elliot Price (Author): Added Security/Audit Logs page with event names + fields; updated Override page with RBAC role names (Optimize Admin / Override Approver) and mandatory justification + expiration semantics.

Kenji Nakamura (Design): The failure-state copy in docs should match Console wording (‘Blocked by quality gate’, ‘Override requested’, ‘Override granted (expires …)’). Elliot Price (Author): Synced copy with latest Console strings and included screenshots placeholders + exact labels in the Console page.

Dylan Brooks (Console Eng): Please note that artifact links can be restricted by org policies; include fallback behavior if an artifact is not accessible. Elliot Price (Author): Added note in Reading Results: artifact access depends on org settings; if restricted, Console shows artifact ids and support contact path.
notable_links: Related implementation PRs: redwood/pr-19531 (block apply on failure), redwood/pr-19537 (override flow + audit events), redwood/pr-19570 (artifact links in Console API), redwood/pr-19593 (metrics/tracing), observability-pack/pr-883 (dashboards/alerts), eval-harness/pr-418 (flake score + rerun policy)
source.release_notes: Docs: Added customer-facing and internal documentation for Optimize quality gates, including how gates work, how to interpret pass/fail results, override request/approval process (time-bound, auditable), and links to observability/runbooks. No product behavior changes in this PR.
source.merge_outcome: Merged to main via squash on 2026-02-18 after review approvals; follow-up requested to add a dedicated ‘Dedicated/Private’ section when those workflows GA.

source.title: Console: Usage Insights docs (route/model breakdown, cost drivers, exports, troubleshooting)
source.author: Wei Chen
source.body:
## Summary
Adds the initial help-center / docs-site page for **In-console Usage Insights (Beta)**. The doc explains how the route+model breakdown is computed, defines the key metrics (tokens in/out, cache, retries/fallbacks, streaming overhead), and includes a troubleshooting section based on early beta/support feedback. This is intended to ship with the Console beta rollout and will be linked from the Usage Insights pages.

## What’s included
- New doc page: **Console → Usage → Usage Insights (Beta)**
- Definitions and attribution semantics (aligned with platform ADR/API contract)
- Guidance on interpreting cost drivers and “What changed?” deltas
- Export (CSV) overview + permissions/audit expectations
- Troubleshooting / known issues (billing reconciliation, missing route tags, time range timeouts)
- Screenshot placeholders with callouts (to be replaced once final UI polish lands)

## Notes on terminology
We use **Route** in the Console UI and docs to mean the canonicalized request path + method as recorded by the gateway (e.g., `POST /v1/chat/completions`). Where customers expect “endpoint,” we call out that Route ≈ Endpoint and explain normalization (path params templated).

## Security/RBAC
Docs explicitly call out that cost visibility and exporting are gated by workspace settings and role permissions. Also notes that export/download events are audit-logged (field set referenced in INT-9140).

## Screenshot placeholders
Added `TODO(screenshots)` blocks with suggested capture specs:
- Workspace selector + time range
- Breakdown table (Route × Model) with sorting
- Drilldown view with context length histogram and cache hit rate
- Top Cost Drivers card(s)
- Export flow modal + permission denied state

## Related work
- Console UI: redwood/pr-23141
- Export endpoints + signed downloads: redwood/pr-23148
- RBAC gating: redwood/pr-23160
- Data freshness banner: redwood/pr-23170

## Checklist
- [x] Align metric definitions with Console tooltips (DevEx feedback)
- [x] Include billing reconciliation caveats and expected tolerances
- [x] Add troubleshooting entries for known beta issues (SUP-24811 / SUP-24827 / SUP-24863)
- [x] Security review callout for audit logs + export permissions
- [x] Mark as Beta + document limitations

## Open items (follow-ups)
- Replace placeholders with final screenshots after UI copy freeze (target: before beta announcement post)
- Add a short “How to export via API” snippet once examples repo script is public (redwood-examples/pr-622)
- Expand section on model aliases/version bucketing once ENG-3823 fully lands (avoid duplicate-row confusion)
commits: docs(console): add Usage Insights overview + navigation entry
docs(console): define tokens in/out, cache hit rate, retries, fallbacks, streaming overhead
docs(console): add exports + permissions + audit logging section
docs(console): add troubleshooting/known issues and support links
docs(style): tighten terminology (route vs endpoint) and add glossary anchors
docs: address review feedback (reconciliation tolerance wording, time zone note)
notable_snippets:
Glossary excerpt (usage-insights-glossary.mdx):
- Tokens in: Number of input tokens processed for a request, including system + user messages and any tool/function call arguments.
- Tokens out: Number of tokens generated in the response. For streaming responses, tokens out are counted as they are emitted.
- Cache hit rate: The fraction of requests (or tokens, depending on view) served from prefix/KV cache. Cache misses often correlate with longer prompts or low prompt reuse.
- Retry: An additional attempt for a request due to transient errors/timeouts. Retries can increase cost even if the client sees only one response.
- Fallback: When capacity or policy triggers a compatible model variant. Cost is attributed to the model that actually produced tokens.

Troubleshooting excerpt (usage-insights.mdx):
Q: The Usage Insights total doesn’t match my invoice.
A: For Beta, totals should reconcile within tolerance once data is fully ingested. Differences can come from aggregation lag, late-arriving events, and rounding at different layers. If drift persists beyond the documented tolerance for >24h, contact support and reference SUP-24811.

Q: I don’t see route breakdown for OpenAI-compatible endpoints.
A: Ensure your client is using the Redwood gateway routes and that the route normalization mapping is enabled for your workspace. Some OpenAI-compat paths may appear under a normalized route until the mapping backfill completes (SUP-24827).
review_conversation: Elliot Price (review): Can we add a simple, explicit definition of Route (method + canonical path) right near the top? Also please include a note about time zone handling (Console uses workspace time zone; exports are UTC).
Wei Chen (author): Yep. Added a "What is a Route?" section above the first table and a short time zone note in Filters + Export sections.

Alex Martinez (review): Good overall. Two nits from DevEx feedback: 1) Avoid calling it "endpoint grouping"—just say route normalization. 2) Add a one-liner that Route ≈ Endpoint for people coming from other dashboards.
Wei Chen (author): Updated phrasing + added the Route ≈ Endpoint callout under Terminology.

Marissa Cole (review): Please be careful on the "What changed?" section: we should not imply causality. Can we phrase drivers as "correlates" and make it clear it’s heuristic-based? Also add a small note that "Top cost drivers" may be limited when data is partial/lagging.
Wei Chen (author): Adjusted copy to "signals" / "correlates" and added a partial-data caveat + link to freshness banner explanation.

Marcus Lin (review - security): Export section needs a stronger statement about permissions + audit logging. Mention that download links are signed and time-limited; include that exports may be disabled for restricted workspaces. Also link INT-9140 requirements (without exposing internals).

Wei Chen (author): Done: added "Security and permissions" subsection, included signed URL TTL language (non-specific), and noted restricted workspace behavior. Kept it customer-facing (no internal ticket details).
Dylan Brooks (review): Can we add a troubleshooting entry for "Insights page times out on 30d" with recommended mitigation (shorter ranges) and explanation that perf is improving?
Wei Chen (author): Added a Q/A referencing SUP-24863, plus a note about long ranges and exports as an alternative.
Elliot Price (approval): Approved with minor copy tweaks. Please ensure all TODO(screenshots) are consistent and searchable.
Wei Chen (author): Standardized TODO markers and added a small checklist in the doc header for screenshot replacement.
source.release_notes: Adds the Beta help-center doc for Console Usage Insights, including metric definitions, export guidance, RBAC/audit notes, and troubleshooting for common reconciliation and missing-route issues.

source.title: Docs: audit logging event types + export destinations (SIEM/S3) with config examples
source.author: Kira Thompson
source.description:
Motivation: Sales/SEs have been repeatedly asked for a crisp, copy/paste-ready explanation of Redwood audit logging (what events exist, what fields are included, how to export, and what limitations/retention knobs exist by deployment mode). The existing docs were too high-level and didn’t include enough concrete configuration examples, which created drift between RFP language and what customers can actually implement.

What’s in this PR: (1) A new “Audit log event taxonomy” section with stable event names and a minimal field contract (so customers can build detections/ingestions without relying on internal-only fields). (2) An “Export destinations” section covering supported patterns used in the field today: pull via API, push/ship to customer-owned S3-compatible object storage, and SIEM ingestion via customer-managed forwarder (Splunk/Elastic/etc.). (3) Practical configuration examples (curl + SDK snippets) and a troubleshooting appendix that Solutions Engineering can point to during security reviews.

Scope notes / non-goals: This is documentation-only. It intentionally does not promise roadmap (e.g., ‘native’ direct integrations to specific SIEM vendors). Where behavior differs between Hosted, Dedicated, and Private, the docs call this out explicitly and link to the Security/Compliance enablement materials for evidence request process.

Customer-facing highlights: - Clear list of audit event types: auth/admin actions, API key lifecycle, org/user management, model deployment/rollout actions, data retention setting changes, and export configuration changes. - Export overview: API-based export (cursor pagination) and object storage export (customer bucket; customer-owned keys; Redwood writes). - Examples for common workflows: export last 24h to S3, consume via Athena/Glue, and forward into SIEM via a customer-owned collector.

Screenshots/diagrams: No screenshots. Includes a small ASCII data-flow diagram for export paths.

Related: ENG-13988 (add docs/examples for audit log export integrations referenced by enablement kit) and PM-4821 (Enterprise Security Enablement Kit refresh).
commits: docs(audit-logging): add event taxonomy and field contract
docs(audit-logging): add export destinations section (API + S3/object storage) with data flow diagram
docs(audit-logging): add configuration examples and troubleshooting guidance
docs: tighten wording around retention and deployment-mode caveats; add glossary links
changed_areas: mkdocs.yml / nav (added audit logging + export pages to Security section)
illustrative_snippets: Object storage export config (curl): curl -X POST -H "Authorization: Bearer $REDWOOD_TOKEN" -H "Content-Type: application/json" https://api.redwoodinference.com/v1/audit-exports -d '{"destination":{"type":"s3_compatible","bucket":"customer-audit-logs","region":"us-east-1","prefix":"redwood/","endpoint":"https://s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com"},"format":"jsonl","delivery":{"mode":"hourly"}}'
source.review_conversation:
Elliot Price (review): The structure is good, but we need to be careful about implying we have vendor-native SIEM integrations. Please reword the Splunk/Elastic mentions to “customer-managed forwarder pattern” and add a short “what we don’t provide” box.

Kira Thompson (author): Updated language to avoid “native integration” wording; added a “Non-goals / limitations” callout on the export page and moved Splunk/Elastic to examples under customer-managed forwarding.

Marcus Lin (security): In the event taxonomy, avoid listing fields we can’t guarantee are present across deployments. We should define a minimal contract and then say “additional fields may appear.” Also add an explicit note that request/response payloads are not included in audit logs by default.

Kira Thompson (author): Done. Added “Minimal field contract” section + “additional fields may appear” sentence. Added note: audit logs capture control-plane + access events and do not include model inputs/outputs by default.

Naomi Feldman (Product Security PM): Please align the retention wording with the enablement kit language. The docs should say retention depends on plan/deployment and that customers can configure retention where available, without stating a universal number.

Kira Thompson (author): Updated retention section to be deployment-mode specific and removed any fixed durations. Added link to the customer-facing retention/deletion overview page for canonical wording.

Markus Klein (SE): The examples are solid; can we add a short troubleshooting section for the two issues we see most: (1) customer bucket policy denies PutObject, (2) clock skew / filter windows causing perceived “missing events” when paging?

Kira Thompson (author): Added troubleshooting bullets + a recommended approach for paging by cursor and widening windows when validating.

Elliot Price (final): Looks shippable. Nav placement is good. Please add a one-line note in the API page that endpoints require org admin role (or equivalent) and link to RBAC doc.

Kira Thompson (author): Added RBAC note + link. Ready for final.

Approvals: Elliot Price approved. Marcus Lin approved. Naomi Feldman approved. CI green; squashing and merging.
source.release_notes: Docs update only: expanded audit logging documentation, added export destinations overview, and provided configuration/troubleshooting examples for enterprise security reviews.

title: Docs: audit logging event types + export destinations (SIEM/S3) with config examples
preview:
Motivation: Sales/SEs have been repeatedly asked for a crisp, copy/paste-ready explanation of Redwood audit logging (what events exist, what fields are included, how to export, and what limitations/retention knobs exist by deployment mode). The existing docs were too high-level and didn’t include enough concrete configuration examples, which created drift between RFP language and what customers can actually implement.

What’s in this PR: (1) A new “Audit log event taxonomy” section with stable event names and a minimal field contract (so customers can build detections/ingestions without relying on internal-only fields). (2) An “Export destinations” section covering supported patterns used in the field today: pull via API, push/ship to customer-owned S3-compatible object storage, and SIEM ingestion via customer-managed forwarder (Splunk/Elastic/etc.). (3) Practical configuration examples (curl + SDK snippets) and a troubleshooting appendix that Solutions Engineering can point to during security reviews.

Scope notes / non-goals: This is documentation-only. It intentionally does not promise roadmap (e.g., ‘native’ direct integrations to specific SIEM vendors). Where behavior differs between Hosted, Dedicated, and Private, the docs call this out explicitly and link to the Security/Compliance enablement materials for evidence request process.

Customer-facing highlights: - Clear list of audit event types: auth/admin actions, API key lifecycle, org/user management, model deployment/rollout actions, data retention setting changes, and export configuration changes. - Export overview: API-based export (cursor pagination) and object storage export (customer bucket; customer-owned keys; Redwood writes). - Examples for common workflows: export last 24h to S3, consume via Athena/Glue, and forward into SIEM via a customer-owned collector.

Screenshots/diagrams: No screenshots. Includes a small ASCII data-flow diagram for export paths.

Related: ENG-13988 (add docs/examples for audit log export integrations referenced by enablement kit) and PM-4821 (Enterprise Security Enablement Kit refresh).

source.title: Document tiered SLOs + error budget policy (Hosted vs Dedicated) and escalation expectations
source.author: Elliot Price
source.body: Summary: This PR updates internal and external documentation to reflect Redwood’s tiered reliability commitments for Hosted (shared) vs Dedicated (reserved capacity) and the associated error budget policy. It explains (a) canonical SLIs and SLO targets at a high level, (b) how to interpret error budget status in Console and what it means operationally, and (c) escalation expectations and what data is required for support/SRE triage. Motivation: We’ve had repeated customer escalations around latency spikes and 5xx/stream disconnects during burst traffic, plus confusion about what counts against SLOs (client aborts, invalid requests, rate-limits, fallback/retries). This PR consolidates and standardizes wording across docs site pages used by CS/SE and customers. Scope: Hosted + Dedicated only; Private is mentioned as future work and explicitly out-of-scope for enforcement in this phase. Contents included in this PR: Tiered SLO overview page with definitions (SLI vs SLO vs SLA), tier scope, and in-scope endpoints (chat/completions, embeddings, streaming). Error budget explainer page: reporting window, what “budget remaining” means, burn-rate alerting concept (multi-window), and common misunderstandings (e.g., a short spike can be OK if it doesn’t materially burn budget). Streaming reliability section: what counts as a streaming failure, how we distinguish server disconnects vs client aborts, and how retries/fallback are accounted for. Escalation guide: for Hosted vs Dedicated, expected response path, when an event becomes an incident, and the required intake fields (timestamps, region, model, request IDs, and whether it was streaming). Console guidance: where to find the SLO dashboards, how to filter by tier/region, and how to interpret annotations (deploys/incidents). Non-goals: This PR does not change SLO targets themselves, does not publish contractual SLA language, and does not add new product UI—only docs updates and alignment. Notes on terminology: We intentionally use “SLOs (internal)” vs “SLAs (contractual)” and call out that exclusions may differ between internal measurement and contract language. Where possible, we reference the source-of-truth internal policy pages and keep the external pages focused on customer comprehension. Checklist: [x] Hosted vs Dedicated definitions reviewed by PM Hosted + PM Dedicated; [x] Streaming failure accounting reviewed by SRE; [x] CS escalation path reviewed by CS leadership; [x] Links to runbooks/status comms policy added for internal readers; [x] Added examples and ‘what to send Support’ snippet for common cases (latency spikes, elevated 5xx, stream disconnects).
commits: docs(reliability): add tiered SLO overview and glossary
docs(reliability): add error budget status + burn-rate explainer
docs(streaming): clarify disconnect taxonomy (server vs client abort)
docs(support): add SLO escalation expectations + intake requirements
docs(console): document SLO dashboard filters and annotations
chore: fix links, add redirects for prior reliability pages
notable_snippets: From site/docs/reliability/streaming-reliability.mdx: "We classify streaming as unsuccessful when the server terminates the stream with an error (5xx) or the connection drops before a terminal event is sent. Client-initiated aborts (client closes connection) do not count against SLOs when reliably detected via gateway abort signals."

From site/docs/reliability/error-budgets/index.mdx: "Error budget is the allowed unreliability over the rolling window. A transient spike may page on burn-rate, but only sustained burn consumes budget materially. When budget is exhausted for a tier/region, non-emergency releases may be frozen until mitigations restore budget trajectory."

From site/docs/support/escalations/_intake-template.mdx: "Required: UTC timestamps, region, tier (Hosted/Dedicated), endpoint (chat/completions/embeddings), model, streaming yes/no, 3–5 request IDs, and whether retries/fallback were enabled."
review_conversation: Aisha Bello (SRE) 2026-02-13: The streaming section is close, but please be explicit about what we do with 499s and client disconnects. We should avoid implying we always detect client aborts perfectly—word it as 'when reliably detected' and point to request IDs for ambiguous cases.

Elliot Price (author) 2026-02-13: Good call. Updated wording to 'when reliably detected via gateway abort signals' and added a note that ambiguous disconnects should be escalated with request IDs so SRE can classify using traces.

Nikhil Sharma (PM Hosted) 2026-02-14: For Hosted, can we avoid listing hard numbers for latency SLOs here? I’d prefer the external doc to describe measurement and expectations, and link to the Console dashboard, otherwise we’ll have to keep this perfectly in sync with internal targets.

Elliot Price (author) 2026-02-14: Adjusted: removed explicit percentile targets from external page; kept them in internal-only docs (linked) and described 'tiered latency percentiles' at a conceptual level. External page now focuses on how to interpret dashboard + what we do during incidents.

Lauren Bishop (PM Dedicated) 2026-02-14: Dedicated customers will ask 'is fallback allowed' and 'does fallback count as success'. We need a crisp statement: fallback may occur to maintain availability, but latency may be impacted; SLI accounting is based on what the customer sees at the API boundary.

Elliot Price (author) 2026-02-15: Added a 'Routing/fallback and SLI accounting' section: success/latency are measured end-to-end at the API boundary; internal retries/fallback do not get special treatment, and the customer-visible outcome is what counts.

Jasmine Liu (VP CS) 2026-02-15: Escalation expectations look good, but add a clear split: Dedicated escalations route to Enterprise on-call and can trigger tier-specific incident comms; Hosted uses standard support SLAs unless there’s broad impact.
Elliot Price (author) 2026-02-15: Added tier-specific routing language and a short table: 'Who to contact / what happens next'. Also linked internal status/comms guidelines for CS/SE.

Sean Gallagher (Dir SRE) 2026-02-16: Please add a paragraph about burn-rate alerts vs incidents. Customers will see 'budget is burning' in conversation and assume outage. We need a definition: burn-rate = early warning; incident = declared based on impact thresholds and duration.

Elliot Price (author) 2026-02-16: Added 'Burn-rate alerts are internal early-warning signals' section + example scenarios. Also added wording to avoid overpromising comms for brief burns that auto-mitigate.
Markus Klein (Solutions Engineering) 2026-02-16: Minor: ensure we have a single canonical link we can send to customers asking 'how do you measure reliability'. Right now there are three pages.
Elliot Price (author) 2026-02-16: Added a short 'Start here' block on tiered SLO landing page and ensured the support/escalation page references it as the canonical entry point.
source.merge_outcome: Merged via squash into main by Elliot Price
release_notes: Docs: Added tiered SLO + error budget overview (Hosted vs Dedicated)
Docs: Clarified streaming disconnect accounting and client abort handling
Docs: Published escalation expectations and required intake fields for SLO-related issues
Docs: Documented how to interpret SLO dashboards and error budget status

source.title: Add escalation playbook + SLA policy links to Support docs nav
source.author: Elliot Price
source.description: Context: As part of the Customer Success & Support escalation playbook refresh and known-issues sprint, Support asked that the internal docs site make the key references discoverable from the Support landing + left-nav (instead of buried in Confluence search). This PR updates navigation + cross-links to (1) Escalation Playbook (paths/contacts, severity matrix, bridge conventions, exec escalation), (2) SLA policy by customer tier, and (3) customer comms templates; it also adds a short “How to use these docs during an escalation” blurb and ensures link text matches the published Confluence titles. Scope: navigation + internal doc pages only; no customer-sensitive details, no new policy content (policy source of truth remains Confluence). Notes: Links use the existing internal Confluence link helper so they resolve correctly in preview/prod; link targets intentionally point at the canonical Confluence pages rather than duplicating content in the docs site. Follow-ups: INT-333 will add Jira automation to prompt linking the index; ENG-4826 will ship the public known-issues landing separately (PR 2841).

docs(support): add SLA + escalation quicklinks to landing
docs(copy): align link titles + add ‘when to escalate’ callout
chore(ci): tighten link-check allowlist for confluence redirects

content/support/index.mdx (add ‘Escalation & SLAs’ quicklinks block + short usage note)

content/support/customer-comms-templates.mdx (new page linking to templates + guidance on what not to promise)
content/support/jira-triage.mdx (add inline links to severity matrix + SLA timers doc)

README-internal.md (add note: keep escalation contacts in Confluence, not docs repo)
content/_partials/sensitivity-note.mdx (new reusable callout: avoid customer-specific data)
content/support/_meta.json (ensure pages show under Support section)
source.implementation_notes: Navigation: Adds a top-level “Escalations & SLAs” group under Support, adjacent to Triage/Intake. Adds a dedicated internal hub page to reduce link sprawl and to keep Confluence as the system of record. Copy: Uses consistent naming: Support Priority (P0–P4) and Incident Severity (Sev0–Sev3) and links to the mapping doc; includes a reminder that Support should not bypass #incidents criteria and should follow bridge conventions. Sensitivity: Explicit callout that the docs site should not include direct phone numbers, personal emails, or customer-identifying incident details; those stay in Confluence with appropriate permissions.

source.title: SDK docs: add streaming troubleshooting (timeouts, reconnect, idempotency) + cross-links to known issues
source.author: Wei Chen
source.description:
## Summary
Adds a dedicated streaming troubleshooting section to the SDK docs and links it consistently to the new Known Issues hub articles. The goal is to reduce Support back-and-forth by giving customers copy/paste-ready guidance for the most common streaming failure modes (timeouts, reconnect, truncated streams) and by standardizing our language around safe retries and idempotency.

This PR is part of the Customer Success & Support escalation playbook refresh / known-issues sprint work. It is intentionally customer-safe (no account names, no internal dashboards).

## What’s included
- New docs page: “Troubleshooting streaming” with:
  - Common symptoms + likely causes (proxy idle timeouts, client read timeouts, network path resets)
  - Recommended client timeout defaults (connect vs read) and keepalive guidance
  - Reconnect flow for SSE/streaming responses (retry with jittered backoff)
  - Safe retry rules + idempotency guidance (what is safe to retry and what isn’t)
  - Clear “When to escalate to Support” criteria + required info (request IDs, region, model, SDK version)
- Cross-links to Known Issues:
  - Known issue: Streaming timeouts and reconnect (workarounds)
  - Known issues index landing page
- Minor edits to Python/TS/Go streaming guides to point to the troubleshooting page (instead of repeating partial guidance).

## Rationale
Support tickets SUP-1842 / SUP-1854 (and multiple repeats) consistently stall on:
- Unclear separation of customer network/proxy timeouts vs Redwood-side disconnects
- Inconsistent retry advice (some customers retry mid-stream unsafely)
- Missing “what to send Support” context (request IDs, regions, model/version, whether HTTP/2 is in play)

This doc consolidates the guidance and keeps the workaround details in the Known Issues pages so Support can update mitigations without chasing SDK guide copies.

## Not included / follow-ups
- No changes to server behavior; see ENG-4822 (improve streaming disconnect error details) for runtime/gateway improvements.
- No customer-specific mitigations beyond what’s already published as a Known Issue.

## Screenshots
- N/A (text-only docs).

## Implementation notes
- The troubleshooting content lives under SDK docs so it’s discoverable from language-specific guides.
- The “When to escalate” section aligns with the playbook language (request IDs, time window, error strings, impacted region/model).

### Example snippet (docs)
"If you see `stream reset` / truncated output mid-generation: (1) retry the request from the beginning (do not attempt to resume partial tokens), (2) use exponential backoff with jitter, and (3) include the `x-request-id` from the failed attempt when contacting Support. If failures persist for >10 minutes in a single region or affect multiple regions, escalate per the Known Issue guidance."

## Checklist
- [x] Links verified (no relative-link rot)
- [x] Terminology aligned: “stream reset / truncated stream / disconnect”
- [x] Support-safe (no internal tooling references)
- [x] Reviewed by Support/SRE for accuracy on retry + escalation criteria

Related:
- PR #2841 (Known issues hub + initial workaround articles)
- PR #2842 (Support docs nav updates + escalation/SLA links)
commits: docs(sdk): add troubleshooting page for streaming timeouts/reconnect/idempotency
docs(sdk): link python/ts/go streaming guides to troubleshooting section
docs: align escalation wording + add required diagnostics list
docs: fix broken anchors and unify terminology (disconnect vs reset)
conversation:
Elliot Price (Docs): Please add a short callout that links to the Known Issues index, not just the streaming article. Also, avoid stating exact internal SLO numbers here—keep it symptom/impact-based.

Wei Chen (Author): Updated to include both links in the header and removed the SLO mention. “When to escalate” now references symptoms/time window and required context.

Owen Phillips (Support): The retry guidance needs to be explicit that retries should restart the request (don’t attempt to resume a partial stream). Also add required fields: region + model + SDK version + request IDs.

Wei Chen (Author): Added a “Safe retries” subsection with “restart request only” language and a bullet list of required info for Support.

Rafael Mendes (SRE): Two tweaks: (1) prefer ‘jittered exponential backoff’ wording; (2) make it clear that disabling HTTP/2 is a temporary workaround and only recommended if it matches the Known Issue article.

Wei Chen (Author): Incorporated both. The HTTP/2 mention is now a link-out to the Known Issue page with a ‘temporary workaround’ label.

Kira Thompson (Writer): Can we standardize terms: “stream reset” and “truncated stream” appear—add them to a small glossary at the top.

Wei Chen (Author): Added a short “Symptoms glossary” block.

Elliot Price (Docs): Approved.

Owen Phillips (Support): Approved.

Rafael Mendes (SRE): Approved.
release_notes: Docs: Added SDK troubleshooting guidance for streaming timeouts/reconnect/idempotency; linked to Known Issues hub for current workarounds.

Related:
- PR #2841 (Known issues hub + initial workaround articles)
- PR #2842 (Support docs nav updates + escalation/SLA links)
text:
## Summary
Adds a dedicated streaming troubleshooting section to the SDK docs and links it consistently to the new Known Issues hub articles. The goal is to reduce Support back-and-forth by giving customers copy/paste-ready guidance for the most common streaming failure modes (timeouts, reconnect, truncated streams) and by standardizing our language around safe retries and idempotency.

This PR is part of the Customer Success & Support escalation playbook refresh / known-issues sprint work. It is intentionally customer-safe (no account names, no internal dashboards).

## What’s included
- New docs page: “Troubleshooting streaming” with:
  - Common symptoms + likely causes (proxy idle timeouts, client read timeouts, network path resets)
  - Recommended client timeout defaults (connect vs read) and keepalive guidance
  - Reconnect flow for SSE/streaming responses (retry with jittered backoff)
  - Safe retry rules + idempotency guidance (what is safe to retry and what isn’t)
  - Clear “When to escalate to Support” criteria + required info (request IDs, region, model, SDK version)
- Cross-links to Known Issues:
  - Known issue: Streaming timeouts and reconnect (workarounds)
  - Known issues index landing page
- Minor edits to Python/TS/Go streaming guides to point to the troubleshooting page (instead of repeating partial guidance).

## Rationale
Support tickets SUP-1842 / SUP-1854 (and multiple repeats) consistently stall on:
- Unclear separation of customer network/proxy timeouts vs Redwood-side disconnects
- Inconsistent retry advice (some customers retry mid-stream unsafely)
- Missing “what to send Support” context (request IDs, regions, model/version, whether HTTP/2 is in play)

This doc consolidates the guidance and keeps the workaround details in the Known Issues pages so Support can update mitigations without chasing SDK guide copies.

## Not included / follow-ups
- No changes to server behavior; see ENG-4822 (improve streaming disconnect error details) for runtime/gateway improvements.
- No customer-specific mitigations beyond what’s already published as a Known Issue.

## Screenshots
- N/A (text-only docs).

## Implementation notes
- The troubleshooting content lives under SDK docs so it’s discoverable from language-specific guides.
- The “When to escalate” section aligns with the playbook language (request IDs, time window, error strings, impacted region/model).

### Example snippet (docs)
"If you see `stream reset` / truncated output mid-generation: (1) retry the request from the beginning (do not attempt to resume partial tokens), (2) use exponential backoff with jitter, and (3) include the `x-request-id` from the failed attempt when contacting Support. If failures persist for >10 minutes in a single region or affect multiple regions, escalate per the Known Issue guidance."

## Checklist
- [x] Links verified (no relative-link rot)
- [x] Terminology aligned: “stream reset / truncated stream / disconnect”
- [x] Support-safe (no internal tooling references)
- [x] Reviewed by Support/SRE for accuracy on retry + escalation criteria

Related:
- PR #2841 (Known issues hub + initial workaround articles)
- PR #2842 (Support docs nav updates + escalation/SLA links)

source.title: Internal docs: Cost Model API usage, field semantics, and troubleshooting
source.author: Elliot Price
source.description:
This PR updates the internal docs site to reflect Cost Model API v1 as the canonical source for cost primitives used by Console and Optimize. It adds concrete request/response examples, defines field semantics (esp. prefill vs decode, cache attribution, batch efficiency, and overhead allocation), and includes troubleshooting guidance pulled from early integrations and support escalations.

Motivation
- We’ve had multiple definitions of “cost” across telemetry, billing hooks, Console, and Optimize prototypes. Teams need a single, versioned set of docs that matches the schema package + service behavior.
- Customer-facing escalations (SUP-31844, SUP-31877) showed that missing/ambiguous semantics (overhead vs request-attributable; optimistic what-if assumptions) cause confusion and mis-triage.

What’s included
- New internal docs page: “Cost Model API (v1) – Usage & Semantics”
  - Endpoint overview: /v1/cost/estimate, /v1/cost/breakdown, /v1/cost/what-if
  - Version negotiation guidance (client sends supported_schema_versions; server responds with selected_schema_version)
  - Request bounding guidance for Console hot paths (time ranges, grouping cardinality, max dimensions)
- Field definitions aligned with ADR-022 taxonomy
  - Explicit definitions for prefill_token_count vs decode_token_count
  - cache_attributed_{prefill,decode}_tokens vs cache_hit_rate; how to interpret partial hits
  - batch_efficiency_ratio and queue_time_ms: what they represent and what they do NOT
  - overhead_allocation_policy_id + overhead_usd: how overhead is allocated and why invoice totals may differ
- What-if guardrails for Optimize
  - Allowed parameter ranges (batch size bounds, cache warm-rate bounds, quantization profile constraints)
  - Confidence interval fields and when to surface “low confidence” in the UI
- Troubleshooting section
  - “Console breakdown doesn’t match invoice” decision tree (most common causes: reporting window, overhead policy, telemetry lag, feature-flag fallback)
  - “What-if savings too optimistic” checklist (cache warm-rate assumptions, workload summary mismatch, quant profile incompatibility)
  - Common error codes and mitigations (SCHEMA_VERSION_UNSUPPORTED, REQUEST_BOUNDS_EXCEEDED, TELEMETRY_LAGGING)

Notes
- Docs intentionally avoid duplicating the schema reference (generated from the schema package). This PR links to the canonical schema docs and adds human explanations + examples.
- All examples use fake org/project IDs and redacted model IDs.

Follow-ups
- Once Console feature flag rollout passes 50% traffic, add a short “Console integration notes” appendix with real-world latency numbers and caching recommendations (tracked in ENG-4825).

References
- ADR-021 Standardize cost model interfaces
- ADR-022 Cost breakdown taxonomy
- Cost Model API v1 design spec (internal Confluence)
- Support: SUP-31844, SUP-31877
commits: docs(cost-model): add v1 overview + endpoint examples
docs(cost-model): define cost breakdown taxonomy & field semantics
docs(cost-model): add troubleshooting guide + common error codes
docs(nav): link cost-model docs from Platform APIs index
chore: fix lint + linkcheck failures
ci_checks: linkcheck: pass (rerun after fixing two broken Confluence permalinks)
review_conversation: Ethan Park (requested changes): The estimate example currently implies overhead is always present per-request. Can we clarify that overhead may be returned as 0 for some modes/policies and that it’s policy-driven (overhead_allocation_policy_id)?
Elliot Price (author): Updated semantics section + examples to show overhead_usd can be 0. Added a short explanation of overhead allocation policy IDs and linked to the finance alignment doc.

Chloe Martin: For Console, we need explicit guidance on bounding group_by dimensions (otherwise dashboard queries will explode). Can we add a table of recommended max cardinalities and a note about caching results per (org_id, project_id, model_id, day)?

Elliot Price (author): Added “Console hot path guidance” section with recommended bounds and a note to prefer server-side aggregation endpoints. Also added a callout that client-side grouping is discouraged for >O(1k) series.

Aditya Rao (requested changes): The what-if docs should be explicit that cache_warm_rate is not in v1 inputs (we’ve been asked for it but it’s deferred). The text currently mentions it as if it’s supported.

Elliot Price (author): Fixed. Moved cache_warm_rate into “v2 candidates / not supported in v1” and updated the troubleshooting checklist to reference the current supported fields (workload_summary + cache_hit_rate assumptions).

Allison Grant: Please include the exact error code strings and whether they are retryable. Also add a brief note on determinism (same inputs => same outputs) and which fields can vary due to telemetry lag.
Elliot Price (author): Added an “Errors & retryability” snippet + determinism section. Documented TELEMETRY_LAGGING as non-retryable for immediate retries; advises backing off or widening the window.

Laura Bennett: The reconciliation section should explicitly state that invoice is system-of-record and that cost model outputs are for standardized attribution/visibility, not RevRec. Also add a note about monthly close windows.
Elliot Price (author): Added a finance callout: invoice is system-of-record; docs now describe reporting windows and why month-close adjustments can shift totals.
