source.title: Docs: Shadow Traffic Quality Metrics quickstart (enablement, interpretation, retention warnings)
source.author: Elliot Price
source.release_notes: Adds public quickstart for Shadow Traffic quality metrics (beta): how to enable shadowing on a route/rollout, what metrics mean, common pitfalls, and retention/redaction defaults + knobs.
source.description: This PR adds a docs quickstart for Shadow Traffic quality metrics (beta) aimed at ML/platform engineers rolling out model/version/config changes safely without impacting end-user responses. It covers: how shadowing works (mirrored requests, control response returned), how to enable via Console and API, recommended sampling rates and minimum sample sizes, how to interpret deltas + confidence intervals, scoring lag expectations, and a dedicated Safety/Privacy section (retention defaults, redaction behavior, access controls, and audit logging). This content is aligned with the v1 metric bundle (refusal rate, format adherence, instruction-following rubric score, and basic safety/refusal signals) and includes warnings around cost, cardinality, and streaming edge-cases. References: PM-1842 PRD, ENG-5419 docs epic, SUP-28451 customer request, and internal SRE guardrails guidance.
commits: docs(shadow-traffic): add quickstart page and navigation entry
docs(shadow-traffic): add Console walkthrough + API examples
docs(quality-metrics): explain deltas, CI bands, and sample size guidance
docs(security): add retention/redaction defaults + Dedicated/Private notes
docs(troubleshooting): add scoring lag + missing data playbook and links
checks: Link checker: PASS (rerun once after fixing relative anchors)
conversation: Marissa Cole (Product): Can we explicitly state that shadow results are advisory-only at beta and do not auto-rollback by default? Also add a note that quality regressions should be investigated alongside latency/cost deltas, not in isolation.

Elliot Price (Author): Added a banner in the intro: “Beta: advisory signals by default” and a callout in the 'Using quality gates' section noting that automation depends on rollout policy configuration and is off unless enabled. Also added a short paragraph in 'Interpreting deltas' about triangulating with latency/cost/error metrics.

Sean Gallagher (SRE): Please add explicit guardrails: shadow path must be fail-open, has strict timeouts, and should not page on shadow target errors unless they threaten control path (we've seen confusion during dogfood). Also add troubleshooting steps for disabling quickly (kill switch / policy off).

Elliot Price (Author): Added 'Operational guardrails' section with fail-open semantics, recommended shadow timeout, and a note on alerting/paging policy. Added troubleshooting steps and a 'Disable shadowing' snippet in both Console and API sections with references to runbooks (internal link labeled as “for Redwood oncall”).

Marcus Lin (Security): Retention/redaction section needs to be more precise. Avoid implying we store full prompts by default. We should say “redacted artifacts” and point to configurable knobs; also explicitly state that prompt/response bodies may be truncated or hashed depending on policy and deployment type. Add an access control note (RBAC + audit logs).

Elliot Price (Author): Updated wording to “shadow artifacts (redacted and truncated by default)” and added a table describing defaults + knobs. Added RBAC + audit log notes and a reminder to use field allowlists/redaction rules for regulated workloads.

Jonas Weber (Evals): Please include rubric version pinning in the quickstart; otherwise users will compare experiments across rubric changes and blame the model. Also add a warning that model-graded metrics can drift; recommend pinning scorer model/rubric per experiment run.

Elliot Price (Author): Added 'Rubric versioning' section: every experiment run stores rubric_version and scorer_model_id; recommend pinning for longitudinal comparisons and rerunning control baseline if rubric changes. Also added a caveat about grader drift and calibration.

Allison Grant (Observability): For the metrics examples, avoid high-cardinality breakdowns (prompt_id, customer_id). Use segment examples like route, model, region, request_type, and coarse “tenant_tier”. Also add mention of scoring lag dashboard and how to interpret missing points.
Elliot Price (Author): Updated examples to use cardinality-safe dimensions and added a 'Scoring lag' note with expected windows and what gaps mean (ingestion vs scoring).

Monica Patel (SDK/API): API examples should match the Console backend contract naming. Use /v1/shadow-experiments endpoints and include pagination in “List experiment runs”. Also please keep SDK mention scoped to availability (beta: Python+TS, Go coming soon).
Elliot Price (Author): Updated curl examples to /v1/shadow-experiments/* and added list pagination (limit, cursor). Added a brief SDK availability callout: Python/TS in beta; Go planned.
source.body:
Title: Shadow Traffic Quality Metrics (Beta) — Quickstart

What you’ll do in this guide
In this quickstart you’ll enable Shadow Traffic on a production route and compare “control” vs “shadow” variants using quality metrics—without changing what your users see. Shadow Traffic mirrors a sampled subset of real requests to one or more candidate variants (e.g., new model version, quantization profile, routing policy, or prompt template), scores the shadow outputs asynchronously, and surfaces quality deltas alongside latency/cost/error metrics.

Key properties (read this first)
- No user impact: The user always receives the control response. Shadowing is fail-open: if the shadow path fails, the control path continues unaffected.
- Asynchronous quality scoring: Quality metrics may lag behind request volume (ingestion + scoring queue). Plan for delayed dashboards.
- Beta behavior: Quality signals are advisory by default. Automatic rollback/quality gating depends on your rollout policy configuration and is off unless explicitly enabled.

Prerequisites
- You have access to the Redwood Console (or API) with permissions to manage routes/rollouts and view quality dashboards.
- Your route is already instrumented in Console (latency/cost/errors visible).
- You understand what you’re testing (model version vs quantization vs prompt template). Pick one variable per experiment when possible.

Concepts
- Control: The variant that serves user-visible responses.
- Shadow target: One or more variants that receive mirrored requests for evaluation only.
- Sampling rate: The fraction of production requests mirrored to the shadow target (e.g., 0.5%–5%).
- Deterministic bucketing: The same “type” of request is consistently assigned to the same shadow condition, reducing variance when comparing windows.
- Run: A continuous time window of shadowing under a fixed policy + rubric configuration.

Step 1 — Create a shadow experiment (Console)
1) Go to Console → Rollouts → Shadow Experiments → Create
2) Select a route (or an existing canary/A-B rollout object)
3) Choose your shadow target(s)
   - Example targets: model=rw/llama-3.1-70b-instruct@v2, quantization=int8-profile-b, or routing-policy=cost-optimized
4) Set sampling
   - Recommended defaults:
     - Start: 1% sampling
     - Increase: 2–5% once you confirm overhead and scoring lag are stable
   - If your traffic is low, use higher sampling to reach statistical power faster.
5) Set timeouts + fail-open behavior
   - Shadow timeout should be strict. If the shadow request times out, it’s recorded as shadow_error and the user response is unaffected.
6) Save

Step 2 — Enable shadowing via API (optional)
If you prefer automation (CI/CD or rollout pipelines), you can create and enable a shadow experiment via API.

Create experiment
curl -X POST https://api.redwoodinference.com/v1/shadow-experiments \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $REDWOOD_API_KEY" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"route_id":"rt_7w2k...","name":"llama70b-v2-shadow","sampling_rate":0.01,"shadow_targets":[{"type":"model","model_id":"rw/llama-3.1-70b-instruct@v2"}],"shadow_timeout_ms":2500,"fail_open":true,"rubric_version":"qm_v1.0","scorer_model_id":"rw/grader-13b@2026-02-01"}'

List runs (paginated)
curl -X GET "https://api.redwoodinference.com/v1/shadow-experiments/shx_123/runs?limit=50&cursor=..." \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $REDWOOD_API_KEY"

Disable shadowing quickly (kill switch)
If you need to stop shadowing due to overhead or an incident, disable the experiment (control traffic is unaffected either way):
curl -X POST https://api.redwoodinference.com/v1/shadow-experiments/shx_123:disable \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $REDWOOD_API_KEY"

Step 3 — Understand the default v1 quality metric bundle
Shadow Traffic ships with a default metric bundle (v1). Metrics are computed on shadow outputs and compared to control outputs for the same mirrored requests (paired comparison).

Metrics included in v1 (typical)
- Refusal rate: Fraction of responses classified as refusal / non-assistance.
- Format adherence: Whether the response matches the expected format (e.g., valid JSON schema, required keys, tool-call structure, or regex checks).
- Instruction-following rubric score: A model-graded rubric (0–5) measuring compliance with user instructions.
- Safety/refusal signals: Basic flags indicating safety policy triggers and refusal behaviors.

Important caveats
- Model-graded metrics can drift if the scorer model or rubric changes. Pin rubric_version and scorer_model_id per experiment run.
- Heuristic checks (format/regex) are sensitive to prompt templates and can overcount failures if your format spec is ambiguous.

Step 4 — Interpret quality deltas (and confidence intervals)
Console → Shadow Experiments → (select experiment) → Results

You’ll see, per metric:
- Control value vs Shadow value
- Delta (Shadow - Control)
- Confidence interval (CI band)
- Sample size (N)

How to read this
- Look for deltas that are both practically meaningful and statistically stable. A small delta with a wide CI often means you need more samples.
- Prefer paired comparisons and keep the experiment configuration stable. Changing sampling, rubric, or target mid-run will complicate interpretation.
- Triangulate with latency/cost/error metrics. A “better” rubric score at the cost of large latency regressions may still be a net negative.

Recommended minimums (rule-of-thumb)
- For binary rates (refusal/format pass): aim for at least hundreds to thousands of paired samples before making a decision.
- For rubric scores: expect higher variance; you may need more samples or longer windows.

Scoring lag: why your dashboard may look “empty”
Shadow scoring is asynchronous. It’s normal to see request volume immediately but quality aggregates appear later. Use the “Scoring lag” panel to confirm whether you’re waiting on ingestion or scoring.

Step 5 — Drill into examples safely
Use “Examples” to inspect paired control vs shadow outputs for debugging regressions.

Security + privacy tips
- Do not rely on example views for long-term storage. Shadow artifacts are retained for a short window by default (see Retention & Redaction below).
- Avoid adding high-cardinality identifiers (prompt_id, user_id, email) as tags/labels. Prefer coarse segments (route, model, region, request_type, tenant_tier).

Retention & redaction (warnings and defaults)
Shadow Traffic can capture shadow artifacts (redacted and truncated by default) to support scoring and debugging.

Defaults (may vary by deployment / contract)
- Hosted API: short retention by default (typically 72 hours) for redacted artifacts; aggregates may be retained longer.
- Dedicated: configurable; common default is 7 days for redacted artifacts, longer for aggregates depending on plan.
- Private (VPC/on-prem): configurable and may default to minimal retention; storage location and export controls depend on your deployment.

What “redacted/truncated by default” means
- Sensitive fields may be removed, hashed, or replaced before storage.
- Prompt/response bodies may be truncated to limit exposure and cost.
- You can configure allowlists/redaction rules to keep only the fields necessary for scoring.

Access controls + auditability
- Shadow experiment creation/edits require route/rollout management permissions.
- Viewing examples may be restricted to elevated roles depending on tenant policy.
- Actions are recorded in audit logs (create/update/enable/disable), which can be exported for compliance workflows.

Cost and overhead guidance
- Shadow requests increase inference load on the shadow target. Start small (1%) and scale only after verifying capacity.
- Scoring workers also consume compute. If scoring lag grows, reduce sampling or metrics bundle size.
- Guardrails are designed to protect the user path. If you observe control-path latency impact, treat it as a P0 and disable shadowing.

Streaming requests (known constraints)
Shadowing streaming requests can capture partial outputs depending on timeout and stop conditions. If your use case relies on full-stream fidelity, verify behavior in a low sampling run first and use the Examples view to confirm.

Troubleshooting
Problem: “No quality deltas are showing up”
- Check scoring lag dashboard and pipeline health indicators.
- Confirm your sampling rate is non-zero and the experiment is enabled.
- Verify you’re not filtering by a segment with too few samples.

Problem: “Quality deltas look unstable / flip direction day-to-day”
- Increase sample size (or sampling rate) and keep the run configuration stable.
- Pin rubric_version and scorer_model_id; avoid comparing across rubric changes.
- Ensure deterministic bucketing is enabled so request populations are consistent.

Problem: “Cardinality alerts or observability cost spike”
- Remove high-cardinality tags from experiment metadata.
- Use coarse segments and avoid emitting per-user identifiers.

Next steps
- Attach shadow experiment results to a canary rollout in Console for a unified timeline view.
- For prompt-set regression testing (offline), use eval-harness with pinned rubrics and compare against a known baseline.
- If you need stricter retention controls (regulated workloads), contact your Redwood account team to configure Dedicated/Private defaults and exports.

Links
- Rollouts overview
- Quality deltas and confidence intervals
- Shadow Traffic retention & redaction
- Shadow Traffic troubleshooting

(Internal notes for Redwood oncall: see runbook link in the SRE folder; not part of public docs.)

  -d '{"route_id":"rt_7w2k...","name":"llama70b-v2-shadow","sampling_rate":0.01,"shadow_targets":[{"type":"model","model_id":"rw/llama-3.1-70b-instruct@v2"}],"shadow_timeout_ms":2500,"fail_open":true,"rubric_version":"qm_v1.0","scorer_model_id":"rw/grader-13b@2026-02-01"}'

List runs (paginated)
curl -X GET "https://api.redwoodinference.com/v1/shadow-experiments/shx_123/runs?limit=50&cursor=..." \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $REDWOOD_API_KEY"

Disable shadowing quickly (kill switch)
If you need to stop shadowing due to overhead or an incident, disable the experiment (control traffic is unaffected either way):
curl -X POST https://api.redwoodinference.com/v1/shadow-experiments/shx_123:disable \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $REDWOOD_API_KEY"

Step 3 — Understand the default v1 quality metric bundle
Shadow Traffic ships with a default metric bundle (v1). Metrics are computed on shadow outputs and compared to control outputs for the same mirrored requests (paired comparison).

Metrics included in v1 (typical)
- Refusal rate: Fraction of responses classified as refusal / non-assistance.
- Format adherence: Whether the response matches the expected format (e.g., valid JSON schema, required keys, tool-call structure, or regex checks).
- Instruction-following rubric score: A model-graded rubric (0–5) measuring compliance with user instructions.
- Safety/refusal signals: Basic flags indicating safety policy triggers and refusal behaviors.

Important caveats
- Model-graded metrics can drift if the scorer model or rubric changes. Pin rubric_version and scorer_model_id per experiment run.
- Heuristic checks (format/regex) are sensitive to prompt templates and can overcount failures if your format spec is ambiguous.

Step 4 — Interpret quality deltas (and confidence intervals)
Console → Shadow Experiments → (select experiment) → Results

You’ll see, per metric:
- Control value vs Shadow value
- Delta (Shadow - Control)
- Confidence interval (CI band)
- Sample size (N)

How to read this
- Look for deltas that are both practically meaningful and statistically stable. A small delta with a wide CI often means you need more samples.
- Prefer paired comparisons and keep the experiment configuration stable. Changing sampling, rubric, or target mid-run will complicate interpretation.
- Triangulate with latency/cost/error metrics. A “better” rubric score at the cost of large latency regressions may still be a net negative.

Recommended minimums (rule-of-thumb)
- For binary rates (refusal/format pass): aim for at least hundreds to thousands of paired samples before making a decision.
- For rubric scores: expect higher variance; you may need more samples or longer windows.

Scoring lag: why your dashboard may look “empty”
Shadow scoring is asynchronous. It’s normal to see request volume immediately but quality aggregates appear later. Use the “Scoring lag” panel to confirm whether you’re waiting on ingestion or scoring.

Step 5 — Drill into examples safely
Use “Examples” to inspect paired control vs shadow outputs for debugging regressions.

Security + privacy tips
- Do not rely on example views for long-term storage. Shadow artifacts are retained for a short window by default (see Retention & Redaction below).
- Avoid adding high-cardinality identifiers (prompt_id, user_id, email) as tags/labels. Prefer coarse segments (route, model, region, request_type, tenant_tier).

Retention & redaction (warnings and defaults)
Shadow Traffic can capture shadow artifacts (redacted and truncated by default) to support scoring and debugging.

Defaults (may vary by deployment / contract)
- Hosted API: short retention by default (typically 72 hours) for redacted artifacts; aggregates may be retained longer.
- Dedicated: configurable; common default is 7 days for redacted artifacts, longer for aggregates depending on plan.
- Private (VPC/on-prem): configurable and may default to minimal retention; storage location and export controls depend on your deployment.

What “redacted/truncated by default” means
- Sensitive fields may be removed, hashed, or replaced before storage.
- Prompt/response bodies may be truncated to limit exposure and cost.
- You can configure allowlists/redaction rules to keep only the fields necessary for scoring.

Access controls + auditability
- Shadow experiment creation/edits require route/rollout management permissions.
- Viewing examples may be restricted to elevated roles depending on tenant policy.
- Actions are recorded in audit logs (create/update/enable/disable), which can be exported for compliance workflows.

Cost and overhead guidance
- Shadow requests increase inference load on the shadow target. Start small (1%) and scale only after verifying capacity.
- Scoring workers also consume compute. If scoring lag grows, reduce sampling or metrics bundle size.
- Guardrails are designed to protect the user path. If you observe control-path latency impact, treat it as a P0 and disable shadowing.

Streaming requests (known constraints)
Shadowing streaming requests can capture partial outputs depending on timeout and stop conditions. If your use case relies on full-stream fidelity, verify behavior in a low sampling run first and use the Examples view to confirm.

Troubleshooting
Problem: “No quality deltas are showing up”
- Check scoring lag dashboard and pipeline health indicators.
- Confirm your sampling rate is non-zero and the experiment is enabled.
- Verify you’re not filtering by a segment with too few samples.

Problem: “Quality deltas look unstable / flip direction day-to-day”
- Increase sample size (or sampling rate) and keep the run configuration stable.
- Pin rubric_version and scorer_model_id; avoid comparing across rubric changes.
- Ensure deterministic bucketing is enabled so request populations are consistent.

Problem: “Cardinality alerts or observability cost spike”
- Remove high-cardinality tags from experiment metadata.
- Use coarse segments and avoid emitting per-user identifiers.

Next steps
- Attach shadow experiment results to a canary rollout in Console for a unified timeline view.
- For prompt-set regression testing (offline), use eval-harness with pinned rubrics and compare against a known baseline.
- If you need stricter retention controls (regulated workloads), contact your Redwood account team to configure Dedicated/Private defaults and exports.

Links
- Rollouts overview
- Quality deltas and confidence intervals
- Shadow Traffic retention & redaction
- Shadow Traffic troubleshooting

text:
Title: Shadow Traffic Quality Metrics (Beta) — Quickstart

What you’ll do in this guide
In this quickstart you’ll enable Shadow Traffic on a production route and compare “control” vs “shadow” variants using quality metrics—without changing what your users see. Shadow Traffic mirrors a sampled subset of real requests to one or more candidate variants (e.g., new model version, quantization profile, routing policy, or prompt template), scores the shadow outputs asynchronously, and surfaces quality deltas alongside latency/cost/error metrics.

Key properties (read this first)
- No user impact: The user always receives the control response. Shadowing is fail-open: if the shadow path fails, the control path continues unaffected.
- Asynchronous quality scoring: Quality metrics may lag behind request volume (ingestion + scoring queue). Plan for delayed dashboards.
- Beta behavior: Quality signals are advisory by default. Automatic rollback/quality gating depends on your rollout policy configuration and is off unless explicitly enabled.

Prerequisites
- You have access to the Redwood Console (or API) with permissions to manage routes/rollouts and view quality dashboards.
- Your route is already instrumented in Console (latency/cost/errors visible).
- You understand what you’re testing (model version vs quantization vs prompt template). Pick one variable per experiment when possible.

Concepts
- Control: The variant that serves user-visible responses.
- Shadow target: One or more variants that receive mirrored requests for evaluation only.
- Sampling rate: The fraction of production requests mirrored to the shadow target (e.g., 0.5%–5%).
- Deterministic bucketing: The same “type” of request is consistently assigned to the same shadow condition, reducing variance when comparing windows.
- Run: A continuous time window of shadowing under a fixed policy + rubric configuration.

Step 1 — Create a shadow experiment (Console)
1) Go to Console → Rollouts → Shadow Experiments → Create
2) Select a route (or an existing canary/A-B rollout object)
3) Choose your shadow target(s)
   - Example targets: model=rw/llama-3.1-70b-instruct@v2, quantization=int8-profile-b, or routing-policy=cost-optimized
4) Set sampling
   - Recommended defaults:
     - Start: 1% sampling
     - Increase: 2–5% once you confirm overhead and scoring lag are stable
   - If your traffic is low, use higher sampling to reach statistical power faster.
5) Set timeouts + fail-open behavior
   - Shadow timeout should be strict. If the shadow request times out, it’s recorded as shadow_error and the user response is unaffected.
6) Save

Step 2 — Enable shadowing via API (optional)
If you prefer automation (CI/CD or rollout pipelines), you can create and enable a shadow experiment via API.

Create experiment
curl -X POST https://api.redwoodinference.com/v1/shadow-experiments \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $REDWOOD_API_KEY" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"route_id":"rt_7w2k...","name":"llama70b-v2-shadow","sampling_rate":0.01,"shadow_targets":[{"type":"model","model_id":"rw/llama-3.1-70b-instruct@v2"}],"shadow_timeout_ms":2500,"fail_open":true,"rubric_version":"qm_v1.0","scorer_model_id":"rw/grader-13b@2026-02-01"}'

List runs (paginated)
curl -X GET "https://api.redwoodinference.com/v1/shadow-experiments/shx_123/runs?limit=50&cursor=..." \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $REDWOOD_API_KEY"

Disable shadowing quickly (kill switch)
If you need to stop shadowing due to overhead or an incident, disable the experiment (control traffic is unaffected either way):
curl -X POST https://api.redwoodinference.com/v1/shadow-experiments/shx_123:disable \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $REDWOOD_API_KEY"

Step 3 — Understand the default v1 quality metric bundle
Shadow Traffic ships with a default metric bundle (v1). Metrics are computed on shadow outputs and compared to control outputs for the same mirrored requests (paired comparison).

Metrics included in v1 (typical)
- Refusal rate: Fraction of responses classified as refusal / non-assistance.
- Format adherence: Whether the response matches the expected format (e.g., valid JSON schema, required keys, tool-call structure, or regex checks).
- Instruction-following rubric score: A model-graded rubric (0–5) measuring compliance with user instructions.
- Safety/refusal signals: Basic flags indicating safety policy triggers and refusal behaviors.

Important caveats
- Model-graded metrics can drift if the scorer model or rubric changes. Pin rubric_version and scorer_model_id per experiment run.
- Heuristic checks (format/regex) are sensitive to prompt templates and can overcount failures if your format spec is ambiguous.

Step 4 — Interpret quality deltas (and confidence intervals)
Console → Shadow Experiments → (select experiment) → Results

You’ll see, per metric:
- Control value vs Shadow value
- Delta (Shadow - Control)
- Confidence interval (CI band)
- Sample size (N)

How to read this
- Look for deltas that are both practically meaningful and statistically stable. A small delta with a wide CI often means you need more samples.
- Prefer paired comparisons and keep the experiment configuration stable. Changing sampling, rubric, or target mid-run will complicate interpretation.
- Triangulate with latency/cost/error metrics. A “better” rubric score at the cost of large latency regressions may still be a net negative.

Recommended minimums (rule-of-thumb)
- For binary rates (refusal/format pass): aim for at least hundreds to thousands of paired samples before making a decision.
- For rubric scores: expect higher variance; you may need more samples or longer windows.

Scoring lag: why your dashboard may look “empty”
Shadow scoring is asynchronous. It’s normal to see request volume immediately but quality aggregates appear later. Use the “Scoring lag” panel to confirm whether you’re waiting on ingestion or scoring.

Step 5 — Drill into examples safely
Use “Examples” to inspect paired control vs shadow outputs for debugging regressions.

Security + privacy tips
- Do not rely on example views for long-term storage. Shadow artifacts are retained for a short window by default (see Retention & Redaction below).
- Avoid adding high-cardinality identifiers (prompt_id, user_id, email) as tags/labels. Prefer coarse segments (route, model, region, request_type, tenant_tier).

Retention & redaction (warnings and defaults)
Shadow Traffic can capture shadow artifacts (redacted and truncated by default) to support scoring and debugging.

Defaults (may vary by deployment / contract)
- Hosted API: short retention by default (typically 72 hours) for redacted artifacts; aggregates may be retained longer.
- Dedicated: configurable; common default is 7 days for redacted artifacts, longer for aggregates depending on plan.
- Private (VPC/on-prem): configurable and may default to minimal retention; storage location and export controls depend on your deployment.

What “redacted/truncated by default” means
- Sensitive fields may be removed, hashed, or replaced before storage.
- Prompt/response bodies may be truncated to limit exposure and cost.
- You can configure allowlists/redaction rules to keep only the fields necessary for scoring.

Access controls + auditability
- Shadow experiment creation/edits require route/rollout management permissions.
- Viewing examples may be restricted to elevated roles depending on tenant policy.
- Actions are recorded in audit logs (create/update/enable/disable), which can be exported for compliance workflows.

Cost and overhead guidance
- Shadow requests increase inference load on the shadow target. Start small (1%) and scale only after verifying capacity.
- Scoring workers also consume compute. If scoring lag grows, reduce sampling or metrics bundle size.
- Guardrails are designed to protect the user path. If you observe control-path latency impact, treat it as a P0 and disable shadowing.

Streaming requests (known constraints)
Shadowing streaming requests can capture partial outputs depending on timeout and stop conditions. If your use case relies on full-stream fidelity, verify behavior in a low sampling run first and use the Examples view to confirm.

Troubleshooting
Problem: “No quality deltas are showing up”
- Check scoring lag dashboard and pipeline health indicators.
- Confirm your sampling rate is non-zero and the experiment is enabled.
- Verify you’re not filtering by a segment with too few samples.

Problem: “Quality deltas look unstable / flip direction day-to-day”
- Increase sample size (or sampling rate) and keep the run configuration stable.
- Pin rubric_version and scorer_model_id; avoid comparing across rubric changes.
- Ensure deterministic bucketing is enabled so request populations are consistent.

Problem: “Cardinality alerts or observability cost spike”
- Remove high-cardinality tags from experiment metadata.
- Use coarse segments and avoid emitting per-user identifiers.

Next steps
- Attach shadow experiment results to a canary rollout in Console for a unified timeline view.
- For prompt-set regression testing (offline), use eval-harness with pinned rubrics and compare against a known baseline.
- If you need stricter retention controls (regulated workloads), contact your Redwood account team to configure Dedicated/Private defaults and exports.

Links
- Rollouts overview
- Quality deltas and confidence intervals
- Shadow Traffic retention & redaction
- Shadow Traffic troubleshooting

source.title: Publish marketplace security overview + buyer FAQ (AWS/GCP)
source.author: Elliot Price
source.release_notes:
- New public docs pages: Marketplace Security Overview + Marketplace Buyer FAQ
- Wording aligned to Security/GRC guidance (no over-claims; clear shared responsibility)
- Links to gated evidence request flow (SOC 2 report, pen test summary) for procurement
source.description:
Goal: publish marketplace-friendly, customer-facing security docs that AWS/GCP reviewers and enterprise buyers can use without opening a ticket, while keeping claims accurate and consistent with our SOC 2/ISO-aligned controls. This PR adds (1) a Marketplace Security Overview page with a simple system/data-flow narrative and clear shared responsibility boundaries, and (2) a buyer-focused FAQ that answers common procurement/security questions (encryption, audit logs, retention, residency, incident response, support SLAs, and how to request evidence).

Key points / decisions (per Security & GRC review): we avoid absolute language like "we never store prompts" and instead document our default handling + configurable controls; we call out that some security evidence (SOC 2 Type II report, pen test summary) is available under NDA / upon request; we do not claim certifications we do not hold; we state encryption practices at a level appropriate for public docs (in transit + at rest, KMS options for Private) without disclosing sensitive implementation details.

Content added:
- /security/marketplace/overview: scope, deployment modes (Hosted/Dedicated/Private) and how security responsibilities differ, high-level data flow, encryption overview, identity/access, logging/audit, retention/residency, subprocessors pointers, and evidence request path.
- /security/marketplace/faq: short Q&A for procurement-led buyers, including marketplace subscription->entitlement->org provisioning expectations and who to contact when entitlement propagation is delayed.

Follow-ups (out of scope here): add diagrams once Design finalizes the screenshot/diagram pack (DES-913), and align FAQ wording with the final marketplace listing FAQ blocks once PMM locks copy for AWS/GCP submission.
commits: docs(security): add marketplace security overview skeleton and nav entry
docs(marketplace): add buyer-focused security FAQ + procurement questions
docs: tighten wording per GRC review (claims, evidence access, definitions)
docs: add redirects + update internal cross-links from onboarding pages
chore: fix markdown lint issues and broken links in security section
changed_files: site/docs/marketplace/faq.mdx (update: link to security FAQ + entitlement delays question)
site/docs/security/data-handling.mdx (update: clarify defaults vs configurable retention)
site/static/robots.txt (update: allow indexing of new security pages)

source.review_conversation:
Alex Martinez (Docs/DevEx) — 2026-02-07: "Structure looks good. Two nits: (1) can we move the evidence request CTA higher on the overview page; buyers will scan for SOC2 quickly. (2) Please add a short note about entitlement delays + retry guidance, since Support has been seeing that."

Elliot Price (author) — 2026-02-07: "Done. Added an evidence request callout under 'Compliance posture' and a short section under FAQ + a link to onboarding troubleshooting."

Olga Petrov (GRC) — 2026-02-08: "Please remove 'SOC 2 certified' language. We should say 'SOC 2 Type II report available under NDA' and 'we maintain a SOC 2-aligned control environment'. Also: avoid stating specific log retention periods unless we can guarantee them across SKUs. Recommend wording: 'retention is configurable; defaults described in docs; contract terms may apply'."

Elliot Price — 2026-02-08: "Acknowledged. Updated posture section + replaced fixed-day retention mentions with defaults + 'configurable' language. Added a 'Definitions' note distinguishing control plane vs customer content."

Marcus Lin (Security Eng) — 2026-02-09: "Encryption section: don't imply customer-managed keys for Hosted. That feature is Private-only today. Suggest: 'Hosted/Dedicated: Redwood-managed keys in cloud KMS; Private: customer-managed KMS/HSM integration options'. Also avoid naming specific KMS key rotation cadence publicly."

Elliot Price — 2026-02-09: "Updated encryption section accordingly and removed rotation specifics. Added a short table mapping deployment mode -> key management options."

Dr. Aisha Rahman (Security sponsor) — 2026-02-10: "Overall good. One more: Incident response language should not promise a specific notification window publicly. Use 'per contract/SLA' and point to Support/CS path. Also remove reference to 'ISO 27001 certification' unless Legal confirms."

Elliot Price — 2026-02-10: "Updated IR section to 'per contract/SLA' and removed ISO certification mention. Left 'ISO-aligned controls' phrasing consistent with prior guidance."

Naomi Feldman (Security/Compliance PM) — 2026-02-11: "Approved after the above changes. Please ensure the marketplace onboarding docs link to these pages (to reduce duplicate questionnaires)."

Elliot Price — 2026-02-11: "Added cross-links from marketplace onboarding FAQ and security landing page."

Soojin Lee (PMM) — 2026-02-11: "Thanks. The buyer FAQ aligns with the listing copy direction. Please avoid competitor callouts on these pages."

Elliot Price — 2026-02-11: "No competitor language included. Ready to merge."

source.title: Regional failover operator guide: automation behavior, runbook entry points, and pitfalls
source.author: Elliot Price
source.release_notes:
- Added operator-facing guide for regional failover automation (Hosted API + Dedicated) covering triggers, guardrails, rollback semantics, approvals, and verification steps.
- Added quick links to dashboards, runbooks, and incident-bot/audit events.
- Added troubleshooting section for common pitfalls observed in rehearsals/game day (signal noise, oscillation prevention, streaming reconnect expectations, and DNS TTL misconceptions).
source.description:
## Summary
This PR adds a new operator-facing guide for Redwood’s regional failover automation work (Reliability Program). The goal is to make the system understandable and operable for on-call: what the automation will do, what it will not do, where to start during an event, and how to validate outcomes.

The guide is intentionally written as an “operator manual” rather than a design doc: it prioritizes decision points, runbook entry points, and fast checks over implementation details.

## Why
During rehearsals and Game Day 1 we saw consistent confusion around:
- Which signals are authoritative vs corroborating (reachability vs 5xx burn vs tail latency vs capacity)
- When the controller is in dry-run vs execute mode and how to interpret recommendations
- Rollback and hold-down semantics (avoiding oscillation) and what on-call can safely override
- Where to look for the audit trail / incident timeline annotations (INT-1149)
- What “failover complete” means for Hosted vs Dedicated (including pool drain status)

This doc consolidates the above into a single entry point that links out to the existing runbooks and dashboards.

## What’s included
- New doc: `Regional failover automation: Operator guide`
  - Audience: SRE on-call, routing/control plane on-call, and incident commanders
  - Scope: Hosted API + Dedicated multi-region routing; *does not* cover Private bespoke automation
- A “Start here during an event” section with:
  - Triage flow: verify impairment → check region health summary → confirm corroborating signals → check controller mode → approve/monitor execution → validate and record
  - Direct links to runbooks (Hosted failover, controller runbook, GPU drain/evacuation)
- A “How automation decides” section:
  - Plain-language explanation of the trigger hierarchy and dampening/hold-down timers
  - What constitutes a region-level event vs partial degradation (ties to multi-region SLO definition)
- Guardrails and safety limits:
  - Per-tenant flip rate limits, blast radius constraints, and approvals in production
  - Rollback rules and anti-flap expectations (what on-call should *not* do)
- Observability + auditability:
  - What dashboards to open and what “good” looks like during failover
  - How to find controller audit events and incident-bot summaries
- Common pitfalls (from rehearsal notes and support questions):
  - False positives from partial network reachability loss
  - Latency spikes caused by client retries/reconnect patterns (esp. streaming)
  - Capacity headroom constraints in the target region and why failover can be intentionally gated
  - DNS TTL misconceptions (steering is not instant; caching + provider behavior)

## What’s not included
- No new product promises or SLA language (PM-512 tracks customer-facing UX/copy separately)
- No step-by-step duplication of existing runbooks; the guide links out to them and clarifies when to use each

## Docs structure changes
- Adds a Reliability/Operations section entry for “Regional failover automation”
- Adds cross-links and consistent terminology (region impairment, failover, rollback, hold-down, dry-run)

## Validation
- Ran docs build locally
- Link check (internal anchors + external Confluence/GitHub references)
- Confirmed the “Start here” flow matches current on-call ownership boundaries

## Follow-ups
- Once the failover controller CLI ships broadly, update the guide with stable command names and example outputs.
- After Game Day 2, fold in any additional pitfalls and update the success-criteria checklist.

Refs: ENG-2411 (umbrella), ENG-2423 (runbook refresh), PM-512 (customer-facing UX expectations), INT-1149 (timeline annotations), SUP-25405 (SLO definition question).

docs: add rollback/hold-down explanation and do-not-do list
docs: add observability + audit events + incident-bot references
docs: address common pitfalls (signal noise, capacity gating, DNS TTL, streaming reconnect)
chore: fix nav ordering and normalize terminology

chore: fix broken anchors and outdated links
source.conversation:
Tomáš Novák (review) — 2026-02-04: The ‘Start here’ section is great, but can we make the decision flow more explicit? On-call needs a crisp “if reachability is red, treat as authoritative” statement. Also please add a callout that manual policy flips without hold-down awareness can cause oscillation.

Elliot Price (author) — 2026-02-04: Added an explicit priority statement in the trigger hierarchy section and a highlighted “Do not flip repeatedly” callout with the rationale.

Miles Thornton (review) — 2026-02-05: A few details to tighten:
1) The rollback section should differentiate “rollback” vs “revert to primary after hold-down” (operators have been mixing these).
2) Add a note that capacity gating can intentionally block failover even with reachability impairment, and how to interpret that (not a bug).
3) Please avoid implying DNS TTL is the only steering mechanism.

Elliot Price (author) — 2026-02-06: Updated rollback terminology; added a dedicated subsection “Why failover can be gated” including capacity/headroom and per-tenant safety limits; adjusted DNS language to “DNS + steering layers; TTL affects propagation, not instantaneous reroute.”

Allison Grant (review) — 2026-02-06: Observability section: can we add a minimal panel list for what to open first (reachability probes, 5xx burn, p95/p99 latency burn, capacity headroom, policy flips, drain progress)? This matches the dashboard spec and what we asked for in rehearsal.

Elliot Price (author) — 2026-02-07: Added ‘first 5 minutes’ panel checklist and an ‘expected during failover’ note to reduce misreads.

Devon Price (review) — 2026-02-07: Can you add a reference to incident-bot summaries and where audit events land, plus the guidance about what to paste into the incident timeline? This addresses INT-1149.

Elliot Price (author) — 2026-02-07: Added an “Auditability & incident timeline” section with copy/paste fields: event_id, reason, affected routes/tiers, start/stop timestamps, and dashboard links.

Rafael Mendes (approval) — 2026-02-10: Looks aligned with current controller behavior and on-call expectations. Approving.

Tomáš Novák (approval) — 2026-02-10: Approved after last edits. Please keep the “pitfalls” section updated post-Game Day 2.

Merge — 2026-02-10: Squash merged by Elliot Price.

title: Regional failover operator guide: automation behavior, runbook entry points, and pitfalls
preview:
## Summary
This PR adds a new operator-facing guide for Redwood’s regional failover automation work (Reliability Program). The goal is to make the system understandable and operable for on-call: what the automation will do, what it will not do, where to start during an event, and how to validate outcomes.

The guide is intentionally written as an “operator manual” rather than a design doc: it prioritizes decision points, runbook entry points, and fast checks over implementation details.

## Why
During rehearsals and Game Day 1 we saw consistent confusion around:
- Which signals are authoritative vs corroborating (reachability vs 5xx burn vs tail latency vs capacity)
- When the controller is in dry-run vs execute mode and how to interpret recommendations
- Rollback and hold-down semantics (avoiding oscillation) and what on-call can safely override
- Where to look for the audit trail / incident timeline annotations (INT-1149)
- What “failover complete” means for Hosted vs Dedicated (including pool drain status)

This doc consolidates the above into a single entry point that links out to the existing runbooks and dashboards.

## What’s included
- New doc: `Regional failover automation: Operator guide`
  - Audience: SRE on-call, routing/control plane on-call, and incident commanders
  - Scope: Hosted API + Dedicated multi-region routing; *does not* cover Private bespoke automation
- A “Start here during an event” section with:
  - Triage flow: verify impairment → check region health summary → confirm corroborating signals → check controller mode → approve/monitor execution → validate and record
  - Direct links to runbooks (Hosted failover, controller runbook, GPU drain/evacuation)
- A “How automation decides” section:
  - Plain-language explanation of the trigger hierarchy and dampening/hold-down timers
  - What constitutes a region-level event vs partial degradation (ties to multi-region SLO definition)
- Guardrails and safety limits:
  - Per-tenant flip rate limits, blast radius constraints, and approvals in production
  - Rollback rules and anti-flap expectations (what on-call should *not* do)
- Observability + auditability:
  - What dashboards to open and what “good” looks like during failover
  - How to find controller audit events and incident-bot summaries
- Common pitfalls (from rehearsal notes and support questions):
  - False positives from partial network reachability loss
  - Latency spikes caused by client retries/reconnect patterns (esp. streaming)
  - Capacity headroom constraints in the target region and why failover can be intentionally gated
  - DNS TTL misconceptions (steering is not instant; caching + provider behavior)

## What’s not included
- No new product promises or SLA language (PM-512 tracks customer-facing UX/copy separately)
- No step-by-step duplication of existing runbooks; the guide links out to them and clarifies when to use each

## Docs structure changes
- Adds a Reliability/Operations section entry for “Regional failover automation”
- Adds cross-links and consistent terminology (region impairment, failover, rollback, hold-down, dry-run)

## Validation
- Ran docs build locally
- Link check (internal anchors + external Confluence/GitHub references)
- Confirmed the “Start here” flow matches current on-call ownership boundaries

## Follow-ups
- Once the failover controller CLI ships broadly, update the guide with stable command names and example outputs.
- After Game Day 2, fold in any additional pitfalls and update the success-criteria checklist.

Refs: ENG-2411 (umbrella), ENG-2423 (runbook refresh), PM-512 (customer-facing UX expectations), INT-1149 (timeline annotations), SUP-25405 (SLO definition question).

source.title: Add AWS/GCP Marketplace onboarding pages + entitlement troubleshooting
source.author: Wei Chen
source.description:
Summary: This PR adds first-class AWS and GCP Marketplace onboarding docs to reduce time-to-first-token for marketplace buyers and to unblock partner submission requirements. It introduces a Marketplace onboarding landing page, provider-specific step-by-step guides (Subscribe → Entitlement → Provision org → Create API key → First request), and an entitlement troubleshooting page with concrete error states and recommended next actions. It also links to the Marketplace Security Documentation Pack and clarifies which security artifacts are public vs available on request.

Motivation / context: Marketplace buyers are procurement-led and frequently get stuck during entitlement propagation and org linking. Partner feedback requested clearer customer-facing steps and explicit guidance for common failure modes (e.g., existing orgs, multi-subscription, delayed entitlement sync). This doc set is intended to pair with the console marketplace entrypoint (ENG-22112) and entitlement verification endpoint work (ENG-22110) by documenting the same error codes/UX copy.

What’s included: (1) New docs IA for Marketplace onboarding under /docs/marketplace/ with a landing page that routes customers by provider and deployment type (Hosted/Dedicated/Private). (2) AWS Marketplace guide covering subscription, private offers vs public offers, entitlement timing expectations, how to find product code / account identifiers needed for support, and first-call examples. (3) GCP Marketplace guide covering subscription, billing account prerequisites, procurement approvals, and first-call examples. (4) Troubleshooting: a dedicated Entitlements & provisioning troubleshooting page with a table mapping common errors (ENTITLEMENT_NOT_FOUND, ENTITLEMENT_PENDING, ORG_ALREADY_LINKED, SUBSCRIPTION_MISMATCH, REGION_NOT_AVAILABLE) to remediation steps, including when to wait vs when to contact support and what data to provide. (5) Security: links to the Marketplace Security Documentation Pack index and a short ‘Security & compliance for marketplace buyers’ section with safe wording (no over-claims), plus notes on audit logs and retention controls.

Out of scope: Pricing details beyond high-level SKU descriptions (handled in the pricing/packaging pages update PR). No changes to console UX or backend entitlement logic.

Notes for partner submission: Final URLs introduced in this PR are stable and intended to be used in AWS/GCP marketplace listing fields. If redirects/vanity URLs are needed for older links, we should track separately (see INT-771).

QA: Ran local docs build, link checker, and spell/markdown lint. Verified nav placement and breadcrumbs. Confirmed that security links do not expose gated evidence directly and instead route to the security pack overview with ‘contact us’/‘on request’ language for reports.

Screenshots: Not included in this PR; screenshots will land once Design finalizes the capture set (DES-913).
commits: docs(marketplace): add marketplace onboarding landing page and nav entry
docs(aws): add AWS Marketplace onboarding guide + first request example
docs(gcp): add GCP Marketplace onboarding guide + billing prerequisites
docs(troubleshooting): add entitlement/provisioning troubleshooting matrix
docs(security): link marketplace security pack + tighten wording per GRC review
chore: add redirects + update internal link references
source.review_conversation:
Elliot Price (Docs): Requested tightening the information architecture to keep Marketplace docs under a single /marketplace prefix and avoid duplicating content across AWS/GCP pages. Suggested extracting shared sections (definitions, first request, org linking) into partials to reduce drift. Author update: extracted shared partials and replaced duplicated blocks.

Naomi Feldman (Security/Compliance): Asked to avoid implying “SOC 2 certified” language and to ensure claims are aligned with the security packet wording (e.g., ‘SOC 2 Type II report available under NDA/on request’). Requested clarifying shared responsibility and not listing specific retention periods as hard guarantees without referencing configuration. Author update: replaced language with approved phrasing, added shared responsibility callout, and moved retention specifics to a ‘see security packet’ link.

Marcus Lin (Security Eng): Pointed out that entitlement troubleshooting should not instruct customers to paste raw tokens/logs into support tickets. Requested guidance to share request IDs and timestamps instead. Author update: updated support escalation section to request request_id, org_id, cloud account ID, subscription ID, and timestamps; added explicit ‘do not share prompts/keys’ note.

Ben Carter (Growth): Requested explicit ‘How long does provisioning take?’ expectations and a prominent ‘What to do if you’re stuck’ box above the fold to reduce drop-offs. Author update: added entitlement timing partial and a troubleshooting CTA box on both AWS/GCP pages.

Alex Martinez (DevEx): Requested linking this doc set from the Quickstart entrypoint and ensuring the first request examples use the marketplace-safe base URL and include environment variables. Author update: updated snippets and added a shared marketplace links snippet for consistency.

Final review: All reviewers approved after wording and IA changes; CI green; merged via squash.
source.release_notes: Docs: Added AWS/GCP Marketplace onboarding guides, entitlement troubleshooting, and links to the Marketplace Security Documentation Pack to reduce marketplace buyer friction.

source.title: Docs: Safe apply & rollback for Optimization Configs (Hosted/Dedicated)
source.author: Kira Thompson
source.description: Summary: Adds a customer/admin-facing guide for versioned Optimization Configs, safe apply (validate → canary → promote), and one-click rollback for Hosted and Dedicated. Includes UX screenshots callouts (alt text only), API examples, and operational caveats (partial rollouts, cooldowns, metrics gating, and what rollback guarantees do/don’t mean). Motivation: Repeated customer asks for reversible optimization changes (batching/caching/quant profiles) and clear change-management/audit story (refs: SUP-18467, SUP-18421). This doc is intended to be linked from Console tooltips and the Optimize docs nav. Contents: 1) Concepts: config versions, scopes (tenant/model/region), active vs promoted version, and “known-good” baseline. 2) Safe apply workflow: validate, staged rollout with canary %, gating on SLOs (5xx + latency) and quantization quality signals, pause/resume semantics. 3) Rollback workflow: one-click rollback in Console + API endpoint, what happens in the serving fleet, expected propagation time, and post-rollback verification checklist. 4) Caveats/FAQs: partial rollout states, concurrent changes, cooldown windows, audit logs & RBAC permissions, and Dedicated-specific notes. Out of scope: Private deployment instructions (separate guide tracked elsewhere); automated rollback without human confirmation (early access). Screens/UX: Uses stable anchor links and avoids hardcoding UI strings that are still changing; includes short snippets for the confirmation modal copy and blast-radius summary. Validation: ran docs build locally; verified API examples with staging endpoints and ensured headers/fields align with current control plane naming (config_version, rollout_id).
commits: docs(optimize): add safe apply + rollback guide (Hosted/Dedicated)
docs(api): add example curl for rollback + history endpoints
docs(console): add notes on canary gating panels and annotations
docs(security): clarify audit log fields + RBAC permissions required
chore: fix broken links + move guide into Optimize nav group
chore: style pass (frontmatter, headings, callouts)
ci_checks: Link Checker: PASS (1 warning acknowledged: external vendor status page intermittent)
conversation: Paula Mendes (PM): Can we make sure we call out that auto-rollback isn’t generally available at GA? We’ve had customers assume it’ll “self-heal.” Also please add a short section about what we mean by “blast radius” in the confirmation modal.

Kira Thompson (author): Added an explicit “Not automatic by default” callout in Rollback and expanded the blast radius definition in the Rollback confirmation section (scope = tenant/model/region + canary%). Also added an FAQ entry: “Will Redwood auto-rollback my config?”

Chloe Martin (Console): The UI is using “Optimization config version” consistently, not “opt config.” Can you align the terminology and avoid quoting exact button text that might change before Limited GA?

Kira Thompson (author): Updated to “Optimization Config” throughout and switched UI references to descriptive language + stable anchor links. Removed hardcoded button labels; kept one snippet for the type-to-confirm pattern but noted it may vary by workspace policy.

Sean Gallagher (SRE): Good start. Please add propagation expectations and a concrete verification checklist. On rollback, on-call will look for: p95 returning to baseline, error rate normalizing, and rollout status returning to “Completed (Rolled back).” Also note that rollback may temporarily invalidate caches and cause a small latency blip.

Kira Thompson (author): Added “What happens after you click Roll back” with an expected propagation window (typically <5 minutes) and a verification checklist include. Added a caveat about cache invalidation causing transient latency changes and recommended monitoring windows (10–15 minutes) before declaring success.

Marcus Lin (Security): Can you be precise about RBAC names/permissions? Also, for audit logs, we need to mention required fields that customers can key on (actor, request_id, scope, from_version/to_version, rollout_id). Avoid claiming we log prompt/content.

Kira Thompson (author): Updated RBAC section to reference the “optimization change operator” role and the specific permission categories (create/validate/apply/promote/rollback). Added audit log field list and explicitly stated we do not include request payload contents in these audit events.

Arjun Desai (Applied ML): For quantization changes: please include that quality gating may run evals asynchronously and can block promotion even when latency/error is fine. Also call out that rollback returns to the prior version but does not “restore” previously generated outputs.

Kira Thompson (author): Added quantization-specific gating section and clarified that rollback affects serving behavior going forward and does not retroactively modify outputs. Also noted that quality gates can be noisy and rollouts can be paused pending additional eval runs.
Chloe Martin (Console) - Approval: Approved. Terminology alignment looks good and the UI references are future-proof.
Sean Gallagher (SRE) - Approval: Approved. Checklist + cache invalidation caveat are exactly what on-call needs.
Marcus Lin (Security) - Approval: Approved with nits addressed. Thanks for the explicit statement about audit event contents.
Paula Mendes (PM) - Approval: Approved. Matches GA messaging and sets expectations.
release_notes: New documentation page: “Safe apply and rollback for Optimization Configs (Hosted/Dedicated)”
Added API examples for config version history and rollback endpoints
Clarified RBAC + audit log expectations for enterprise change-management reviews
Documented known caveats: partial rollouts, propagation time, and cache invalidation effects

source.title: Add Enterprise Security overview page (public) + SOC 2 request link stubs
source.author: Elliot Price
source.description: Context: Enterprise deals are increasingly gated on security/compliance review. We need a public, linkable Enterprise Security overview page that mirrors approved internal FAQ wording without leaking restricted evidence or promising roadmap items. This PR adds a new docs page with deployment-mode-specific notes (Hosted / Dedicated / Private), clarifies our customer data handling language, and includes stubs for requesting gated artifacts (e.g., SOC 2 Type II report) via the evidence request process. Scope/Non-goals: This is documentation-only and should not imply new product functionality; any features described must already exist. The page intentionally avoids: (a) claiming ISO 27001 certification, (b) stating absolute data residency guarantees without mode/region caveats, (c) offering pen-test reports publicly, and (d) describing future roadmap for audit logging/SCIM beyond current behavior. What’s included: (1) /enterprise/security overview page with sections: security program overview, data protection, encryption, access controls, audit logging, vulnerability management, incident response, shared responsibility, and deployment mode notes; (2) canonical phrasing for “no training on customer data” consistent with current FAQ; (3) “Request SOC 2 report” link stub that routes to gated evidence request instructions; (4) small nav + metadata updates so Sales/SE can share a stable URL during RFPs.
commit_summary: docs: add enterprise security overview page scaffold + nav entry
docs: align data handling + retention wording with Security/GRC guidance
docs: add SOC 2 request CTA + evidence request link stubs
chore: run link check fixes and unify terminology (Hosted/Dedicated/Private)
changed_areas: site content: new enterprise security page
shared snippets: security terminology + CTA link targets
link checking: fix anchors and external link formatting
files_changed: README.md (updated: contributor note on security wording + review owners)
notable_snippets: Frontmatter excerpt (content/enterprise/security.md): title: "Enterprise Security"; description: "Security and compliance overview for Redwood Inference deployments."; sidebar_label: "Security"
CTA excerpt: "SOC 2 Type II report (under NDA): Request access via our evidence request process." (links to /security/evidence-requests)
Shared responsibility note excerpt: "Controls vary by deployment mode; Private deployments shift additional responsibilities to the customer’s environment and change control plane boundaries."
source.testing: CI: docs-build (Docusaurus) ✅; lint ✅; link-check ✅; spellcheck ✅. Manual: verified nav renders on local preview; verified anchors for access-controls/audit-logging sections; confirmed evidence request page is present and does not expose restricted artifacts.
source.rollout_notes: Post-merge: Sales Enablement to update Confluence index to point to /enterprise/security as the public-facing overview. Any customer request for SOC 2 report should be routed through the evidence request workflow (NDA + ticket).
review_conversation: Marcus Lin (Security Eng): Please avoid implying blanket data residency guarantees. The current draft says “data stays in-region” without mode caveats. Suggest: “Data processing location depends on selected deployment mode and region configuration; Private deployments allow customer-controlled networking boundaries.”

Elliot Price (author): Updated the Data Residency & Regional Availability section to add mode-specific language and removed the absolute phrasing. Also added a short “How to talk about residency” callout that points to the evidence request process for details.

Olga Petrov (GRC): SOC 2 wording: please say “SOC 2 Type II report available under NDA” and do not mention audit period dates publicly. Also remove “certified” language anywhere near ISO/27001—use “aligned with” / “mapped to common frameworks” only.

Elliot Price (author): Adjusted compliance section accordingly: “SOC 2 Type II report available under NDA” and removed references to audit period. Replaced “ISO certified” phrasing (none intended, but I found “ISO-ready” in one sentence) with “aligned to common controls and customer requirements.”

Camila Reyes (Product Marketing): Tone/structure looks good. Minor: prefer “Redwood Hosted API” vs “Hosted” on first mention, then use “Hosted” consistently. Also add a short opening paragraph explaining why there are different deployment modes.
Elliot Price (author): Applied naming conventions and added an opening paragraph + quick comparison table bullets for Hosted/Dedicated/Private (kept intentionally high level, no pricing/packaging).

Naomi Feldman (Product Security PM): Access controls section: ensure SCIM is described as supported only where it actually is. Current line reads like it’s universal across deployments; add a note that availability may vary and to confirm with SE for Private/on-prem environments.
Elliot Price (author): Added “Availability varies by deployment mode and customer configuration” note and linked to the “contact us / SE” guidance instead of making a blanket promise.
Wei Chen (Docs): Link checker found a broken anchor on /security/evidence-requests. Can we ensure the stub page has stable IDs for SOC2/subprocessors/DPA requests?
Elliot Price (author): Fixed anchor IDs and added explicit headings for SOC 2 / Subprocessors / DPA so we can link consistently from emails/RFPs.
source.release_notes: Docs: Added a public Enterprise Security overview page with approved security/compliance wording and a SOC 2 evidence request call-to-action (gated under NDA).

title: Add Enterprise Security overview page (public) + SOC 2 request link stubs
preview: Context: Enterprise deals are increasingly gated on security/compliance review. We need a public, linkable Enterprise Security overview page that mirrors approved internal FAQ wording without leaking restricted evidence or promising roadmap items. This PR adds a new docs page with deployment-mode-specific notes (Hosted / Dedicated / Private), clarifies our customer data handling language, and includes stubs for requesting gated artifacts (e.g., SOC 2 Type II report) via the evidence request process. Scope/Non-goals: This is documentation-only and should not imply new product functionality; any features described must already exist. The page intentionally avoids: (a) claiming ISO 27001 certification, (b) stating absolute data residency guarantees without mode/region caveats, (c) offering pen-test reports publicly, and (d) describing future roadmap for audit logging/SCIM beyond current behavior. What’s included: (1) /enterprise/security overview page with sections: security program overview, data protection, encryption, access controls, audit logging, vulnerability management, incident response, shared responsibility, and deployment mode notes; (2) canonical phrasing for “no training on customer data” consistent with current FAQ; (3) “Request SOC 2 report” link stub that routes to gated evidence request instructions; (4) small nav + metadata updates so Sales/SE can share a stable URL during RFPs.

source.title: Private offline bundles: add FAQ + error code reference for signature verification failures
source.author: Kira Thompson
source.description: Context: As part of signed offline bundles for Redwood Private, Support/SEs are seeing repeat questions and inconsistent interpretations of installer verification errors during air-gapped installs (signature invalid, hash mismatch after transfer, unknown signing key after rotation, and time skew causing not-yet-valid). This PR adds a customer-facing FAQ plus a single canonical error-code reference page, with remediation steps that match installer behavior (preflight fails closed; break-glass is explicitly logged and policy-gated). Scope: (1) New FAQ section for "Signed offline bundles" with common operational questions; (2) Error code reference (RWPB-VERIFY-###) mapping to root causes + next steps; (3) Troubleshooting table optimized for copy/paste into support tickets; (4) Notes on key rotation/trust store updates and time source constraints in air-gapped environments; (5) Clear guidance on when to escalate and what logs/outputs to attach (bundle inspect output, manifest SHA256, installer version). Non-goals: No changes to installer behavior; no commitment to customer-signed bundles beyond "planned" language. Screenshots intentionally omitted to reduce staleness; examples are CLI-output blocks. Docs IA: These pages live under Private installation → Offline bundles, and cross-link the main installation guide from PR #1208. Checklist: - [x] Align error names/codes with installer output and Support taxonomy - [x] Include remediation steps for top 10 known failures from pilot + SUP tickets - [x] Clarify break-glass semantics and required audit artifacts - [x] Link to key rotation doc (trust store update) - [x] Linkcheck + Vale pass
source.commits: Commits (squashed): 1) docs(private): add offline bundle FAQ skeleton + cross-links 2) docs(private): add verification error codes page (RWPB-VERIFY-###) 3) docs(private): add troubleshooting decision table + support evidence checklist 4) docs(private): clarify time skew handling and validity window guidance 5) docs: terminology cleanup (bundle/manifest/signature) + minor nav tweaks
source.changed_files: Changed files (high level): - site/docs/private/installation/offline-bundles/faq.md (new) - site/docs/private/installation/offline-bundles/verification-errors.md (new) - site/docs/private/installation/offline-bundles/_category_.json (nav tweaks) - site/docs/private/installation/offline-bundles/index.md (cross-links to FAQ/errors) - site/docs/private/installation/air-gapped-installation.md (links to error reference + evidence checklist) - site/docs/private/installation/offline-bundles/troubleshooting.md (new quick table; used by Support/SE)
source.ci_checks: CI checks: ✅ docs-build (Docusaurus) ✅ linkcheck ✅ lint/format ✅ vale (style) ✅ spellcheck
source.review_conversation: Elliot Price (Docs lead): "Thanks for consolidating this. Two requests: (1) please avoid promising we 'support' break-glass for all cases—call out it's policy gated and may require Redwood approval; (2) add a short 'collect this for Support' box at the top of the error page so folks don't scroll." Kira Thompson (author): "Done. Updated wording to 'available in supported installer versions; may be disabled by policy' and added a sticky-style callout with evidence checklist (installer version, bundle build ID, inspect output, manifest hash)." Ethan Park (Installer lead): "Can you confirm codes match what the installer prints? In latest builds we standardized on RWPB-VERIFY-0xx with short reason strings. Also include the time-skew hint we added (NTP not available) and the optional diagnostic time override flag name." Kira Thompson: "Pulled the exact strings from the installer docs + ENG-4823 notes. Added time-skew section with 'not-yet-valid' explanation and the diagnostic override flag as 'diagnostics.timeOverride' (explicitly marked 'for debugging only')." Dev Patel (Support readiness): "Please add a direct mapping to known SUP tickets and make sure the 'hash mismatch after transfer' remediation mentions unzip tooling/line endings and verifying checksums pre/post transfer." Kira Thompson: "Added a remediation subsection for RWPB-VERIFY-012 with the common causes: unzip tool rewriting files, CRLF conversion, partial copy. Included the recommended 'redwood-private-installer bundle inspect --verify' command run before and after transfer." Marcus Lin (Security Eng): "A couple security nits: for 'unknown key' do not suggest bypassing as first step; emphasize updating trust store and verifying provenance. Also, in 'signature invalid' section, avoid implying 'corrupt bundle' only—explicitly call out possible tampering and treat as incident until proven otherwise." Kira Thompson: "Acknowledged. Reordered steps: trust store update + obtain bundle from official channel, then re-verify. Added 'treat as potential security incident' callout with escalation guidance and what to preserve (original media, hashes, timestamps)." Fatima Noor (Solutions): "FAQ: add Q about 'Can we verify without installing?' and 'What if our environment can't access a time source?'" Kira Thompson: "Added both. Verification without install points to preflight and bundle inspect. Time source Q links to the air-gapped time guidance and suggests aligning clocks prior to verification." Approvals: Elliot ✅, Ethan ✅, Dev ✅, Marcus ✅, Fatima ✅
release_notes: Docs: Added FAQ and error-code reference for signed offline bundle verification failures (Private air-gapped installs).
Docs: Added troubleshooting/evidence checklist for Support cases involving signature verification.
source.body:
Summary
This PR adds a Private docs FAQ and a dedicated error code reference for offline bundle signature verification failures. The goal is to reduce time-to-triage for air-gapped installs by making installer failures actionable: what happened, why it happens, what to do next, and what to collect for Support.

What’s new
1) FAQ: Signed offline bundles
- What is being signed?
- Can we verify without installing?
- What does "fail closed" mean?
- How do key rotations work in air-gapped environments?
- What time sources are required?
- Can we use our own signing root? (Answer: not in initial GA; roadmap note only)

2) Error code reference: RWPB-VERIFY-###
The installer and preflight tooling surface structured error codes. This doc defines each code, likely causes, and remediation steps.

Collect this for Support (include in ticket)
- Installer version (e.g., redwood-private-installer --version)
- Bundle build ID (from bundle inspect output)
- Full output of: redwood-private-installer bundle inspect <path> --verify
- sha256 of the bundle tarball before/after transfer (if applicable)
- Any local verification audit events (if enabled)

Selected error codes (examples)
- RWPB-VERIFY-001: Signature missing
  Cause: Bundle does not include expected detached signature file (e.g., manifest.sig) or signature path mismatches bundle layout.
  Fix: Re-download bundle; ensure bundle was extracted without dropping files; validate expected layout.

- RWPB-VERIFY-004: Signature invalid
  Cause: Manifest/signature mismatch (tampering, corruption, or wrong signature for the manifest).
  Fix: Treat as potential security incident until proven otherwise. Stop install. Preserve original media. Obtain a fresh bundle from official channel. Re-run bundle inspect --verify. Escalate to Support/Security if repeated.

- RWPB-VERIFY-012: Manifest hash mismatch
  Cause: Artifact referenced in manifest does not match SHA256 in manifest (common after offline transfer/extraction).
  Fix: Compare sha256 before vs after transfer; re-transfer with verified tooling; avoid re-packaging; confirm tar extraction does not normalize line endings or permissions.

- RWPB-VERIFY-021: Unknown signing key
  Cause: Bundle signed by a key not present in local trust store (often after key rotation).
  Fix: Update trust store per key rotation doc; re-verify. Do not bypass as first step.

- RWPB-VERIFY-031: Not-yet-valid / time skew
  Cause: Local system time is behind signature validity start time (common in air-gapped environments without NTP).
  Fix: Correct system time using approved internal process. For diagnostics only, use the documented time override flag to validate whether skew is the root cause.

- RWPB-VERIFY-041: Downgrade prevented
  Cause: Attempting to install a bundle with a signed version lower than what is recorded in local install state.
  Fix: Use supported rollback procedure; do not delete state unless explicitly directed by Support with change control.

Break-glass (last resort)
This PR documents the existence of a break-glass verification override flag for supported installer versions. The docs emphasize: (1) it may be disabled by policy, (2) it emits explicit warnings/audit markers, and (3) it should only be used with Redwood approval for time-bounded emergency remediation.

Why now
- Pilot feedback and SUP tickets show recurring failure modes (hash mismatch after transfer; unknown key post-rotation; time skew causing not-yet-valid).
- Support needed a single page to link in responses instead of ad-hoc guidance.

Links
- Related install guide PR: redwood-docs#1208
- Linear: ENG-4850 (docs/runbooks), ENG-4823 (installer verification), ENG-4826 (inspect CLI)
- Support tickets referenced: SUP-31844, SUP-31892, SUP-31910, SUP-31977

Notes
- Wording intentionally avoids hard commitments for customer-signed bundles; points to roadmap language only.
- Error codes are documented as stable identifiers; exact text may vary slightly across installer versions.
