source.title: Private offline bundles: key rotation + trust store update guide (air-gapped)
source.author: Kira Thompson
source.description: Summary: Adds customer-facing documentation for signing key rotation and trust store updates for Redwood Private offline bundles in air-gapped environments. This explains (1) how rotations are announced and staged (overlap window with multiple trusted keys), (2) how customers update trust roots offline (via a trust store update package or manual key file update, depending on deployment), and (3) what Support expects when troubleshooting unknown-key and post-rotation verification failures. Motivation: Pilot customers asked for a single canonical reference that answers “what changes on rotation?”, “what do I need to copy into the air-gap?”, and “how do I prove my installer trusts the new key before attempting an upgrade?”. This PR aligns language with installer behavior (fail-closed on unknown key; break-glass requires explicit policy gating and produces audit markers) and with Release Eng’s rotation cadence guidance. Scope: Docs only (no product changes). References: Linear ENG-4825 (trust store / rotation), ENG-4850 (docs + runbooks), PM-894 (GA criteria). Also addresses recurring Support case patterns (SUP-31910: unknown signing key after rotation). Content highlights: - Defines terms: signing key, trust store, key set ID, overlap window, “active signing key” vs “trusted verification key set”. - Rotation lifecycle: announce -> publish trust store update -> overlap window -> switch signing key -> retire old key after minimum window. - Air-gapped update flow: verify checksum/signature of the trust update package out-of-band, transfer via approved media, apply update to installer trust store, verify with `redwood-private-installer bundle inspect --verify` before running upgrade. - Failure handling: clear guidance for error `RWPB-VERIFY-UNK-KEY` (unknown key) and `RWPB-VERIFY-NOTYETVALID` (time skew); includes what logs and commands to collect. - Support expectations: attach `bundle inspect` output, trust store version, installer version, and support bundle (includes verification metadata) where permitted. Review notes: Includes a small FAQ and a one-page runbook excerpt for SE/Support (kept within the Private docs section).
commits: docs(private): add key rotation + trust store concepts and lifecycle
docs(private): add air-gapped trust store update steps + examples
docs(private): add troubleshooting section for unknown key / time skew
docs: link trust store updates from offline bundle install + FAQ
chore(docs): terminology and formatting pass (align with installer flags)
changed_files: docs/private/offline-bundles/installation-air-gapped.md (updated: add preflight verify + trust store link)
docs/private/offline-bundles/faq.md (updated: new entries for rotation + unknown key)
docs/private/offline-bundles/troubleshooting.md (updated: error codes + what to collect for Support)
docs/private/_partials/cli-snippets.md (updated: add `trust show` and `trust update` examples)
docs/private/release-notes/2026-01.md (updated: note about required trust store update during rotation window)
docs/_redirects (updated: redirect old “signing keys” stub to new page)
source.conversation:
Elliot Price (Docs Lead) [2026-01-15 10:02]: This is the missing piece for pilots. Two requests: (1) make the rotation lifecycle diagram/text explicit about overlap window (customers will ask “how long do I have?”), and (2) add a short callout: Redwood never asks customers to disable verification as part of normal rotation.

Kira Thompson (Author) [2026-01-15 11:18]: Added an “Overlap window” subsection with minimum/typical durations and clarified that the installer may ship with multiple trusted keys. Also added a callout under “What you should NOT do” re: disabling verification.

Connor O'Brien (Release Eng) [2026-01-15 13:40]: Please avoid committing exact rotation dates; we should document cadence + how we communicate, but not hardcode. Also, the trust update artifact naming should match what Release will publish (we’ve been using `redwood-private-truststore-<keyset-id>.tgz`).

Kira Thompson (Author) [2026-01-15 14:05]: Updated to cadence-based wording and changed the example artifact names to `redwood-private-truststore-KEYSET_2026_01.tgz` with a note that keyset ID is printed by `trust show` and included in release notes.

Marcus Lin (Security Eng Lead) [2026-01-16 09:22]: Security nits: - In the “Manual trust root update” section, be explicit that customers must source the public key/trust package from Redwood’s authenticated distribution channel (even if they then move it offline). - Add a note about protecting the transferred trust package and recording custody (common control ask).

Kira Thompson (Author) [2026-01-16 10:11]: Added guidance on authenticated download + custody logging (optional template bullets). Also added a reminder to keep the trust package and checksum file together, and to avoid modifying line endings.

Dev Patel (Support Readiness) [2026-01-16 12:03]: Can we include the minimum set of artifacts Support needs when someone reports `unknown signing key`? In SUP-31910 they sent a screenshot only and it took days. Suggest a copy/paste block with commands.

Kira Thompson (Author) [2026-01-16 12:28]: Added “Support bundle checklist” with commands: `bundle inspect --verify`, `trust show`, `version`, and log paths + note about redaction policy.

Fatima Noor (Solutions Lead) [2026-01-17 08:47]: One more: mention that some air-gapped environments don’t have reliable time and can hit not-yet-valid issues. Even if product fixes are ongoing, document the recommended check (compare system time to release note timestamp; use installer diagnostic override only when directed by Support).

Kira Thompson (Author) [2026-01-17 09:20]: Added a “Time skew” troubleshooting section and explicitly says the diagnostic time override is for Support-directed debugging only.

Connor O'Brien (Release Eng) [2026-01-18 16:31]: Looks good now. Please add a small link from release notes to the new page (customers read those first during upgrades).

Kira Thompson (Author) [2026-01-18 16:45]: Done.

Marcus Lin (Security Eng Lead) [2026-01-18 17:10]: Approved after latest edits.

Elliot Price (Docs Lead) [2026-01-18 17:22]: Approved. Merge when CI is green.

source.title: Private: Air-gapped installation guide for signed offline bundles
source.author: Kira Thompson
source.release_notes: - Adds a complete customer-facing guide for installing Redwood Private in air-gapped environments using signed offline bundles, including verification, troubleshooting, and key rotation/trust store updates.
source.description:
Motivation: Air-gapped/private customers need a single, end-to-end guide that explains (1) what a signed offline bundle is, (2) how to verify provenance/integrity offline, (3) how installation differs from connected installs, and (4) what to do when verification fails (incl. time skew and key rotation). This PR adds a full guide targeted at Redwood Private operators, plus a concise troubleshooting section and references for Support/SE.

Scope: Documentation only (no product behavior changes). The guide aligns with the installer changes landing in redwood-private-installer (inspect + preflight verify + trust store + break-glass) and the release pipeline guidance from Release Eng.

What’s included:
- New doc: "Air-gapped installation (signed offline bundles)" with prerequisites, bundle transport, verification, install flow, and upgrade notes.
- Step-by-step commands using the installer CLI (inspect/verify) and preflight.
- Troubleshooting section with common error cases: hash mismatch after transfer, unknown signing key after rotation, not-yet-valid due to time skew, and downgrade prevention.
- Operator guidance: what gets logged locally (verification audit events), where to find logs, and how to generate a support bundle.
- Key rotation section: how to update the trust store in an air-gapped env (what file to place, how to validate it), and how to confirm the active signing key.
- Explicit break-glass callout: policy-gated override is documented as last resort with required audit annotations and follow-up actions.

Notable doc decisions:
- Uses a "fail closed" stance in examples: installs should stop on signature/manifest mismatch.
- Places verification before any cluster mutations; includes a suggested two-person check for regulated customers.
- Avoids implying customer-signed bundles are supported today; includes a short "Roadmap" note referencing future work.

Screenshots: none (customers asked for copy/paste CLI first). Added example outputs as fenced blocks.

Requested review focus:
- Security: language around trust roots, key rotation, and break-glass policy wording.
- Release Eng: version pinning and "required installer version" callouts.
- Support/SE: troubleshooting steps map to what we can realistically ask customers to do in air-gapped environments.

References:
- Linear: ENG-4850 (docs/runbooks), PM-894 (GA criteria)
- Related PRs: redwood-private-installer/pr-24844, pr-24888, pr-24961, pr-24979; redwood-docs/pr-1221 (FAQ + error codes), pr-1236 (key rotation)
- Support learnings: SUP-31892 (hash mismatch after transfer), SUP-31910 (unknown key after rotation)

source.conversation:
Elliot Price (Docs lead): Can we ensure the guide is explicit about which installer versions are required? Folks will copy/paste commands that may not exist in older installers.
Kira Thompson (author): Added a callout box at the top: "Requires installer >= 2.18.0" and included a note to run `redwood-private-installer version` before proceeding. Also added a note that `bundle inspect` was introduced in 2.18.0.

Marcus Lin (Security Eng): Two asks: (1) avoid wording that implies cert-based signatures; we’re using a signed manifest with a detached signature. (2) Break-glass section should state approval requirements and that audit events MUST be collected.
Kira Thompson: Updated terminology to "manifest + detached signature" throughout; removed "certificate" references. Break-glass section now includes: approval requirement (Security + Release Eng), mandatory audit annotation, and explicit follow-up to restore normal verification (update trust store / re-download bundle).

Connor O'Brien (Release Eng): Please add one line about where to obtain the public trust roots for air-gapped environments and how they’re distributed with releases.
Kira Thompson: Added: trust roots ship with the installer by default; for rotations we provide a `trust-roots.json` (and signature) alongside the offline bundle, and customers must update the installer trust store directory.

Dev Patel (Support readiness): Troubleshooting needs to mention the SUP-31892 failure mode: archive tool changed line endings / extracted with "smart" text mode and corrupted manifest. We should tell them not to edit manifest and to use binary-safe transfer.
Kira Thompson: Added a "Transport integrity" subsection: binary-safe copy, avoid "text mode" tools, verify bundle tarball checksum before extraction, and re-run `bundle inspect --verify` after transfer.

Fatima Noor (Solutions): +1. Also include a short checklist we can paste into customer emails.
Kira Thompson: Added a "Quick checklist" at the top of the guide and a condensed "Copy/paste" block in the partials file.

Ethan Park (Installer lead): The guide says `preflight --bundle ...` but current flag is `--offline-bundle`. Please align with latest CLI.
Kira Thompson: Fixed to `preflight --offline-bundle <path>` and updated all snippets. Verified against installer docs in pr-25028.

Approval:
- Elliot Price: Approved
- Marcus Lin: Approved (after wording tweaks)
- Connor O'Brien: Approved
- Dev Patel: Approved

Merge: Squash merged to main; will be referenced in Private 2026.01 release notes and cross-linked by pr-1221 FAQ.

source.title: Document incident taxonomy fields in Ops guide
source.author: Elliot Price
source.description:
Summary
Adds an Ops Guide section documenting the required incident taxonomy fields (incident_type / incident_subtype / resolved_owner), how to choose a classification during a live incident, and how Customer Success / Support should interpret taxonomy-driven dashboards.

Context
As part of the Reliability Program (“Incident taxonomy + ownership mapping”), we’re making incident_type a required field in incident-bot and piping it through dashboards/alerts. This PR documents the conventions so on-call classification is consistent and reporting is usable.

What’s included
- New Ops Guide page: “Incident taxonomy fields (required)”.
- A short decision flow for classification (optimize for on-call speed + low ambiguity).
- Guidance on multi-cause incidents: primary incident_type vs contributing systems.
- How the owner is resolved (incident-bot → control plane mapping), and what to do when it’s wrong.
- Dashboard interpretation for CS/Support: what “Unknown/Unclassified” means, how to avoid misreading volume charts, and how to communicate incident type externally.

Key definitions documented
- incident_type: Primary category for reliability reporting + ownership.
- incident_subtype: Narrower bucket used for routing/runbooks where needed.
- resolved_owner: Primary owning team for mitigation + follow-ups (auto-resolved; can be overridden with justification).
- contributing_systems: Freeform list (avoid using this as a substitute for incident_type).

Classification decision flow (high-level)
1) Is this primarily an auth/permissions failure? → Auth/RBAC.
2) Are customers seeing elevated 5xx / timeouts / overload signals? → Availability/Overload.
3) Are p95/p99 latencies elevated without a corresponding 5xx spike? → Latency regression.
4) Are streams resetting / disconnecting / partial responses? → Streaming disconnects.
5) Is traffic being rerouted unexpectedly or fallback behavior is incorrect? → Routing/Fallback misbehavior.
6) Is the service healthy but constrained by fleet/GPU capacity or provisioning lag? → Capacity/Fleet shortage.
7) Are control plane operations failing (policy updates, config writes, rollout controls)? → Control plane outage/degradation.
8) Are we blind (missing metrics/traces/logs) such that we can’t validate impact? → Telemetry blind spot.
9) Private-specific: connectivity/config break (DNS/TLS/VPC routing) → Private deployment connectivity/config.

If multiple apply: pick the type that best describes the customer-visible symptom driving the incident declaration; record other factors under contributing_systems and in the timeline. If truly unclear during mitigation, set incident_type=Unknown and update within 60 minutes (or before resolve), per incident process policy.

CS/Support guidance (dashboards + comms)
- incident_type is intended for operational grouping, not a customer-facing root cause statement. When communicating externally, prefer: “We’re investigating an elevated error rate (Availability) impacting requests in <region>” vs overly specific internal causes.
- “Unknown/Unclassified” should trend down over time; it’s a process metric. A spike often indicates training gaps or tooling regression (e.g., incident-bot modal missing field).
- Compare incident volume by type only within the same tier/time window; Dedicated and Private have different baselines and escalation mechanics.
- Owner in dashboards is for internal accountability; CS should not promise timelines based solely on owner assignment. Use published remediation SLAs and the IC/SRE updates.

Links
- Confluence: Incident taxonomy v1 definitions (SRE)
- Confluence: Ownership mapping + remediation SLA matrix
- ADR-0142: Incident taxonomy as required incident field
- Dashboard spec: Incident taxonomy dashboards (Observability)

Rollout note
This documentation aligns with the incident-bot enforcement change (ENG-4822) and the dashboard rollout (ENG-4828). Please keep this page in sync with taxonomy v1.1 changes; do not add new incident_type values in docs without SRE sign-off.

Checklist
- [x] Terminology consistent with taxonomy v1
- [x] Includes examples for common confusion cases (capacity vs routing, streaming vs client)
- [x] CS/Support read-only interpretation guidance reviewed
- [x] Mentions cardinality constraints (avoid inventing new tags)

Screenshots
Included in the doc: incident-bot modal (required fields) + dashboard filter examples (incident_type, owner, tier).
commits: docs(ops): add incident taxonomy fields page and examples
docs(ops): add classification decision flow + multi-cause guidance
docs(cs): add dashboard interpretation + comms do/don’t section
chore: fix broken links and normalize taxonomy terms
docs: add notes on unknown/unclassified and update cadence
review_conversation: Allison Grant (review): Please explicitly call out cardinality constraints: no freeform incident_type values and no subtype proliferation. Also clarify what “Unknown” means vs “Unclassified”.

Elliot Price (author): Added a “Cardinality + stability” callout box and separated “Unknown” (temporary during mitigation) from “Unclassified” (missing/invalid field). Also added a note that subtypes are allowlisted and owned by SRE/Platform.
Dev Patel (review): For CS, we need stronger language that incident_type is not a customer RCA. Also include one example of wording for Dedicated customers during overload/429s.

Elliot Price (author): Added a “Customer comms” section with safe phrasing and a Dedicated-specific example for throttling/overload. Also noted that 429s can be quota enforcement vs capacity, and that CS should confirm with IC before messaging details.

Rafael Mendes (review): Decision flow looks good. Add an explicit rule for capacity vs routing: if fallback is behaving correctly but capacity is constrained, pick Capacity/Fleet shortage; if fallback is incorrect/unexpected, pick Routing/Fallback misbehavior. Also add the 60-minute expectation to update Unknown.
Elliot Price (author): Done. Added the rule + an example scenario. Also added the timing guidance and linked the incident classification policy page.

Aisha Bello (review): In the dashboards section, note that SLA breach alerts are by severity/tier and not strictly by incident_type. Avoid suggesting CS can infer SLA compliance from incident volume charts.
Elliot Price (author): Updated dashboards guidance: clarified SLA views and added a warning about interpreting volume charts as performance/SLA metrics.
Rafael Mendes (approval): Approved. Good enough to ship with v1 rollout; we’ll iterate for v1.1 once we have a week of data.
Dev Patel (approval): Approved with wording changes; thank you for adding the Dedicated example.
Allison Grant (approval): Approved. Thanks for addressing cardinality and Unknown/Unclassified semantics.
source.release_notes: Internal-only docs update: adds Ops Guide documentation for incident taxonomy fields and dashboard interpretation. No customer-facing changes.

source.title: Document 429 reason codes, headers, and retry guidance for hot-route capacity protection
source.author: Wei Chen
source.release_notes:
- Clarifies 429 semantics (quota vs rate limit vs overload shedding)
- Documents reason codes and headers used by Redwood Hosted API
- Adds recommended retry/backoff patterns for streaming and non-streaming clients
- Explains enterprise priority behavior during overload and how to request temporary exceptions
commits: docs: add 429 reason codes and standard headers to errors reference
docs: add handling-429s guide with retry/backoff recommendations
docs: document enterprise priority + capacity protection behavior and caveats
docs: clarify streaming termination semantics when requests are shed
changelog: note upcoming behavior change for 429 payload/headers
tooling: validate documented reason codes match schema list
ci: link check + spelling fixes
source.body:
## Summary
This PR updates Redwood docs to reflect the new tier-aware rate limiting / quota enforcement and overload protection behavior being rolled out for hot enterprise routes. It documents:
- The standardized 429 error payload shape used across Hosted API endpoints
- The set of 429 reason codes and how to interpret them (quota vs rate limit vs overload shedding)
- Response headers clients should read (including Retry-After) and recommended retry/backoff strategies
- How enterprise priority works under fleet pressure (what is and isn’t guaranteed)

This is intended to reduce customer confusion during rollout and give Support consistent language to reference.

## Why
We’ve historically overloaded multiple concepts into “429 Too Many Requests” with inconsistent messages across routes and SDKs. With hot-route capacity protection enabled, customers will see more predictable throttling for best-effort traffic, and enterprise traffic will be protected by priority admission control. We need clear docs so developers can:
- Distinguish between quota exhaustion and overload shedding
- Implement correct backoff and avoid synchronized retries
- Understand what “enterprise priority” means (and what it does not mean)

Related incidents/tickets prompting clarity:
- SUP-18421 (enterprise reported a 429 spike after priority routing rollout; unclear which limits applied)
- SUP-18462 (streaming clients saw inconsistent termination semantics on shed)

## What changed
### 1) Standardized 429 payload + reason codes
Added a dedicated section to `docs/api-reference/errors.md` and a new guide at `docs/hosted-api/handling-429s.md`.

Documented reason codes (current set):
- `quota_exceeded` — Account/org/project quota depleted for the current window.
- `rate_limited` — Request rate exceeded configured limit (short window protection).
- `overload_shed` — Request rejected due to fleet pressure / admission control to protect SLOs.
- `tier_capacity_protection` — Request rejected because best-effort capacity was constrained and enterprise budgets were reserved.
- `route_budget_exhausted` — Protected route budget depleted for the tier in the current control interval.

Notes included in docs:
- Not all codes apply to all endpoints.
- Codes are stable identifiers; human-readable messages may change.
- Customers should branch on `code` and/or headers, not string-match messages.

### 2) Response headers + semantics
Documented headers returned on 429 responses:
- `Retry-After`: integer seconds when a bounded retry window is known (best effort; may be omitted)
- `x-redwood-request-id`: request correlation ID for Support/SRE
- `x-redwood-limit-scope`: `org` | `project` | `api_key` (where applicable)
- `x-redwood-rate-limit-policy`: policy identifier (low cardinality; useful for escalation)
- `x-redwood-overload-mode`: `none` | `admission_control` | `shedding` (only present for overload-related 429s)

Docs explicitly call out that customers may receive 429 without `Retry-After` during fast-changing overload, and should apply client-side exponential backoff with jitter.

### 3) Retry/backoff guidance (streaming + non-streaming)
Added guidance with concrete examples:
- Non-streaming: exponential backoff w/ decorrelated jitter, honor `Retry-After` when present.
- Streaming: treat an early-terminated stream with a final 429-equivalent error as retryable only when the reason code indicates rate limiting / overload (not quota exhaustion). The docs include a warning about duplicate side effects for tool-calling workflows.

### 4) Enterprise priority explanation
New page: `docs/hosted-api/enterprise-priority-and-capacity-protection.md`.
It explains:
- Enterprise traffic is assigned a higher priority class on protected routes and should remain within published SLOs during moderate overload conditions.
- Best-effort traffic is throttled earlier and more predictably to preserve fleet health.
- Priority does not mean unlimited capacity; severe regional incidents can still cause 429/5xx.
- How to request temporary exceptions (burst allowances) and what information Support will ask for.

## Examples included in docs
### Example 429 response (quota)
```http
HTTP/1.1 429 Too Many Requests
Content-Type: application/json
Retry-After: 60
x-redwood-request-id: req_01JFK9Q2QY7X1N8B1M6P4A6R9Z
x-redwood-limit-scope: project
x-redwood-rate-limit-policy: hosted-default-quota-v2

{
  "error": {
    "type": "rate_limit_error",
    "code": "quota_exceeded",
    "message": "Quota exceeded for this project. Please retry after the window resets or request a quota increase."
  }
}
```

### Example 429 response (overload shedding)
```http
HTTP/1.1 429 Too Many Requests
Content-Type: application/json
x-redwood-request-id: req_01JFK9T1S8Z7QWQ5C3QXH4D9AD
x-redwood-overload-mode: shedding
x-redwood-rate-limit-policy: hot-route-protection-v1

{
  "error": {
    "type": "rate_limit_error",
    "code": "overload_shed",
    "message": "Request rejected due to temporary capacity pressure. Please retry with backoff."
  }
}
```

## Validation
- Ran docs build locally (`pnpm docs:build`) and link-check.
- Added a small script guard (`scripts/validate-error-codes.ts`) to ensure the reason code list stays in sync with the canonical list in docs (reduces drift during rollout).

## Rollout / timing notes
This PR is safe to merge before GA; it uses wording aligned with the current canary behavior and avoids promising specific numeric limits. The changelog entry frames this as “clarified 429 semantics and new reason codes/headers” without implying a billing change.

## Follow-ups (not in this PR)
- Add SDK-specific retry snippets (Python/TS/Go) once SDK teams land their corresponding helpers.
- Add a Console screenshot once internal UI hooks for viewing tier limits are stable.

Co-authored-by: Dev Patel (Support copy edits)
Reviewed-by: Elliot Price (Docs), Amara Diallo (Gateway semantics), Rafael Mendes (SRE), Nikhil Sharma (Product)
review_conversation: Elliot Price: The page structure looks good. Please tighten language around "enterprise guarantees"—avoid wording that implies zero 429s. Also add a note that Retry-After may be omitted under overload.
Wei Chen: Updated wording to "priority admission" and added explicit caveat section + Retry-After note. Also added a short table mapping reason codes to retryability.

Amara Diallo: Confirm headers: gateway returns x-redwood-rate-limit-policy and x-redwood-overload-mode only on overload paths; quota path includes Retry-After when window is known. Please reflect that overload may omit Retry-After.
Wei Chen: Adjusted header docs to note conditional presence; removed any implication that Retry-After is always provided.
Dev Patel: Support needs a short "What do I ask the customer for?" checklist (request-id, timestamps, route, reason code, policy header). Can you add that to handling-429s?
Wei Chen: Added Support checklist section and linked to escalation guidance in the enterprise priority page.
Rafael Mendes: Make sure we don’t encourage immediate retries on overload. Suggest adding a minimum delay + jitter even if Retry-After is small/missing.
Wei Chen: Added recommended floor (250ms) and jitter guidance; clarified to cap max delay and to stop after N attempts.
Nikhil Sharma: Please add one paragraph clarifying that priority is tied to account tier and protected routes, not to model choice; model fallback policies are documented elsewhere.
Wei Chen: Added clarification + link to fallback policy overview page (existing doc).
Elliot Price: Approved. Please squash the typo fixes into the main docs commit before merge.
Wei Chen: Squashed and rebased; CI green.
source.merge_outcome: Merged via squash (1 commit) into main by Elliot Price

title: Document 429 reason codes, headers, and retry guidance for hot-route capacity protection
preview:
## Summary
This PR updates Redwood docs to reflect the new tier-aware rate limiting / quota enforcement and overload protection behavior being rolled out for hot enterprise routes. It documents:
- The standardized 429 error payload shape used across Hosted API endpoints
- The set of 429 reason codes and how to interpret them (quota vs rate limit vs overload shedding)
- Response headers clients should read (including Retry-After) and recommended retry/backoff strategies
- How enterprise priority works under fleet pressure (what is and isn’t guaranteed)

This is intended to reduce customer confusion during rollout and give Support consistent language to reference.

## Why
We’ve historically overloaded multiple concepts into “429 Too Many Requests” with inconsistent messages across routes and SDKs. With hot-route capacity protection enabled, customers will see more predictable throttling for best-effort traffic, and enterprise traffic will be protected by priority admission control. We need clear docs so developers can:
- Distinguish between quota exhaustion and overload shedding
- Implement correct backoff and avoid synchronized retries
- Understand what “enterprise priority” means (and what it does not mean)

Related incidents/tickets prompting clarity:
- SUP-18421 (enterprise reported a 429 spike after priority routing rollout; unclear which limits applied)
- SUP-18462 (streaming clients saw inconsistent termination semantics on shed)

## What changed
### 1) Standardized 429 payload + reason codes
Added a dedicated section to `docs/api-reference/errors.md` and a new guide at `docs/hosted-api/handling-429s.md`.

Documented reason codes (current set):
- `quota_exceeded` — Account/org/project quota depleted for the current window.
- `rate_limited` — Request rate exceeded configured limit (short window protection).
- `overload_shed` — Request rejected due to fleet pressure / admission control to protect SLOs.
- `tier_capacity_protection` — Request rejected because best-effort capacity was constrained and enterprise budgets were reserved.
- `route_budget_exhausted` — Protected route budget depleted for the tier in the current control interval.

Notes included in docs:
- Not all codes apply to all endpoints.
- Codes are stable identifiers; human-readable messages may change.
- Customers should branch on `code` and/or headers, not string-match messages.

### 2) Response headers + semantics
Documented headers returned on 429 responses:
- `Retry-After`: integer seconds when a bounded retry window is known (best effort; may be omitted)
- `x-redwood-request-id`: request correlation ID for Support/SRE
- `x-redwood-limit-scope`: `org` | `project` | `api_key` (where applicable)
- `x-redwood-rate-limit-policy`: policy identifier (low cardinality; useful for escalation)
- `x-redwood-overload-mode`: `none` | `admission_control` | `shedding` (only present for overload-related 429s)

Docs explicitly call out that customers may receive 429 without `Retry-After` during fast-changing overload, and should apply client-side exponential backoff with jitter.

### 3) Retry/backoff guidance (streaming + non-streaming)
Added guidance with concrete examples:
- Non-streaming: exponential backoff w/ decorrelated jitter, honor `Retry-After` when present.
- Streaming: treat an early-terminated stream with a final 429-equivalent error as retryable only when the reason code indicates rate limiting / overload (not quota exhaustion). The docs include a warning about duplicate side effects for tool-calling workflows.

### 4) Enterprise priority explanation
New page: `docs/hosted-api/enterprise-priority-and-capacity-protection.md`.
It explains:
- Enterprise traffic is assigned a higher priority class on protected routes and should remain within published SLOs during moderate overload conditions.
- Best-effort traffic is throttled earlier and more predictably to preserve fleet health.
- Priority does not mean unlimited capacity; severe regional incidents can still cause 429/5xx.
- How to request temporary exceptions (burst allowances) and what information Support will ask for.

## Examples included in docs
### Example 429 response (quota)
```http
HTTP/1.1 429 Too Many Requests
Content-Type: application/json
Retry-After: 60
x-redwood-request-id: req_01JFK9Q2QY7X1N8B1M6P4A6R9Z
x-redwood-limit-scope: project
x-redwood-rate-limit-policy: hosted-default-quota-v2

{
  "error": {
    "type": "rate_limit_error",
    "code": "quota_exceeded",
    "message": "Quota exceeded for this project. Please retry after the window resets or request a quota increase."
  }
}
```

### Example 429 response (overload shedding)
```http
HTTP/1.1 429 Too Many Requests
Content-Type: application/json
x-redwood-request-id: req_01JFK9T1S8Z7QWQ5C3QXH4D9AD
x-redwood-overload-mode: shedding
x-redwood-rate-limit-policy: hot-route-protection-v1

{
  "error": {
    "type": "rate_limit_error",
    "code": "overload_shed",
    "message": "Request rejected due to temporary capacity pressure. Please retry with backoff."
  }
}
```

## Validation
- Ran docs build locally (`pnpm docs:build`) and link-check.
- Added a small script guard (`scripts/validate-error-codes.ts`) to ensure the reason code list stays in sync with the canonical list in docs (reduces drift during rollout).

## Rollout / timing notes
This PR is safe to merge before GA; it uses wording aligned with the current canary behavior and avoids promising specific numeric limits. The changelog entry frames this as “clarified 429 semantics and new reason codes/headers” without implying a billing change.

## Follow-ups (not in this PR)
- Add SDK-specific retry snippets (Python/TS/Go) once SDK teams land their corresponding helpers.
- Add a Console screenshot once internal UI hooks for viewing tier limits are stable.

Co-authored-by: Dev Patel (Support copy edits)
Reviewed-by: Elliot Price (Docs), Amara Diallo (Gateway semantics), Rafael Mendes (SRE), Nikhil Sharma (Product)

source.title: Internal: add Sales Demo Guide v1 (runbook links + backup mode)
source.author: Elliot Price
source.description: Context: We’re standardizing the Redwood sales demo environment (demo tenant) and need one canonical, internal guide that SEs/AEs can follow without tribal knowledge. This PR adds an internal “Sales Demo Guide (v1)” with a 30-min run-of-show, prep checklist, tenant reset/healthcheck steps, and a “backup mode” section for when live steps fail (quota/model/dashboard/auth issues). The guide links out to the Confluence runbooks and the demo deck/talk track, and is written to be resilient to product UI drift by emphasizing stable URLs/commands and expected outcomes rather than pixel-perfect click paths. Scope: docs only (no product changes). Security note: guide is internal-only and explicitly states that demo data must remain synthetic; includes safe-sharing rules (no exporting audit logs; no copy/pasting API keys into shared docs; do not grant external access to demo org).
commits: docs(internal): add demo guide landing page + nav entry
docs(demo): add run-of-show (30 min) with AE/SE handoffs
docs(demo): document tenant reset + healthcheck flow; link runbooks
docs(demo): add backup mode paths (screenshots/recording/canned outputs)
docs(demo): add troubleshooting (empty dashboards, 429s, RBAC/SSO)
docs: tighten language + add security callouts + glossary
source.content:
Title: Sales Demo Guide (Internal) — v1
Audience: Sales Engineering + AEs (pilot); internal-only
Owner: Elliot Price (Docs) | Technical owner: Markus Klein
Last updated: 2025-11-20

Purpose
This page is the canonical guide for running a consistent Redwood Inference demo using the demo tenant. It is optimized for reliability: it includes a default 30-minute flow, optional branches (Hosted vs Dedicated-style controls vs Private/VPC narrative), and a “backup mode” plan when live steps fail.

What this guide covers
- Pre-demo checklist: tenant health, keys, dashboards, model pins
- Live demo run-of-show (30 minutes) with AE↔SE handoffs
- Exact API calls for: streaming chat, structured output, tool calling, retries
- Console tour: cost/latency breakdowns, errors, rollouts (safe synthetic workload)
- Reliability story: fallback routing + graceful degradation (what to show vs what to say)
- Backup mode: screenshots/recordings + canned outputs + “how to recover in 60 seconds”
- Troubleshooting: 429s, empty dashboards, auth/RBAC, region/streaming issues
- Security and sharing rules (synthetic-only, least privilege, recording guidance)

What this guide does NOT cover
- Customer POC onboarding (separate process; do not reuse demo tenant)
- Deep performance tuning beyond the scripted toggles
- Any non-synthetic dataset usage

Single source of truth (link hub)
- Demo deck (canonical): Confluence → Sales enablement → Pitch decks → “Redwood demo deck v1”
- Talk track script: Confluence → Positioning & messaging → “Demo talk track v1”
- Runbook: Demo tenant reset: Confluence → Eng Platform → Runbooks → “Demo tenant reset runbook”
- Provisioning overview: Confluence → Eng Platform → How-tos → “Demo tenant provisioning overview”
- Demo environment SLOs: Confluence → Eng SRE → SLO and error budgets → “Demo environment SLOs”
- Capacity/quotas runbook: Confluence → Eng Infra → Runbooks → “Demo capacity and quotas”

Pre-demo checklist (T-15 minutes)
1) Confirm you have the correct scoped demo API key
- Use only keys issued for the demo org/project.
- Do not paste keys into shared docs/slides.
- If you suspect a key was shared, rotate (see reset runbook).

2) Run the demo tenant healthcheck
- Goal: verify quota headroom, model pin availability, dashboard ingestion, and that reset is not running.
- Expected: PASS across: auth, quota, model availability, console ingestion freshness.

3) Verify Console dashboards are populated (synthetic traffic)
- Check time range (last 60 minutes).
- Ensure you are looking at the correct demo project.
- If graphs are empty, start the synthetic traffic generator job (see troubleshooting).

4) Confirm model pins for the demo routes
- Demo routes must remain pinned to known-good model versions.
- If a model is unavailable in-region, follow the fallback policy; do not “pick a random model” live.

5) Open backup assets
- Have the backup screenshots folder open.
- Have the 2–3 minute backup recording ready.
- Have the canned outputs file ready for structured output + tool calling.

30-minute default run-of-show
Minute 0–3 (AE)
- Set context: “We help you ship LLM inference with predictable latency/cost/reliability across hosted, dedicated, and private deployments.”
- Ask 1–2 discovery questions: latency SLOs, cost sensitivity, security constraints (VPC/on-prem), regions.

Minute 3–12 (SE) — Core API demo (streaming + structured output + tool calling)
- Show streaming chat completion: emphasize low-latency token streaming and retry behavior.
- Show structured output: “We can enforce JSON schema so downstream code doesn’t break.”
- Show tool calling: “Model calls a tool; we execute; we return result; model finalizes.”
- If customer is sensitive to compatibility: mention SDKs + OpenAI-compatible endpoint as options.

Minute 12–20 (SE) — Production economics + observability
- Toggle batching/caching preset to illustrate latency vs cost tradeoff.
- Open Console: token-level cost and latency breakdown, per-route dashboard.
- Show error analysis panel with synthetic errors (safe to demo).

Minute 20–25 (SE) — Reliability (fallback routing / graceful degradation)
- Explain: routing policy chooses region/model variant based on latency/capacity.
- Show a controlled “degraded” scenario: route fails over to compatible variant; app stays online.
- Emphasize: for demos we use a safe fallback policy aligned to pinned model families.

Minute 25–30 (AE)
- Summarize outcomes: predictable serving economics + unified operations across deployment modes.
- Next step options: POC plan (eval prompts, throughput targets), security review call, Dedicated sizing.

Backup mode (use when live steps are brittle)
Trigger conditions
- You hit 429 throttling during the live call.
- Console dashboards are empty and won’t populate quickly.
- Auth/RBAC blocks access to the demo org/project.
- A pinned model is temporarily unavailable in the chosen region.

Backup mode path A: “Screenshots-only Console tour” (fastest)
- Switch to annotated screenshots (rollouts view, cost breakdown, error analysis).
- Narrate “what you would see live” and the outcome, not the internal implementation.
- Do not claim the screenshot is from the customer’s environment.

Backup mode path B: “Recorded demo clip (2–3 min) + live Q&A”
- Play the clip for: streaming + structured output + tool calling.
- Continue with Q&A and architecture slides.

Backup mode path C: “Canned outputs + explain API contract”
- Paste pre-generated JSON outputs for structured output + tool calling.
- Emphasize: contract stability, schema guarantees, retries.

Troubleshooting quick references
- 429 / throttling: reduce parallelism; confirm quota; switch to hosted region with headroom; follow capacity/quotas runbook.
- Empty dashboards: verify time range and project selection; start synthetic traffic generator; check ingestion freshness; see SUP-4189 notes.
- RBAC/access issue: confirm you’re in the correct IdP role mapping; follow INT-993 guidance; do not request admin role as a workaround.
- Streaming disconnect in a region: switch region; avoid repeated retries live; use backup mode clip; log incident for follow-up.

Security and sharing rules (demo environment)
- Synthetic-only: never use customer data, even “sanitized.”
- Least privilege: use scoped keys; rotate if exposure is suspected.
- Recordings/screensharing: avoid showing API keys, internal tenant IDs, or unredacted audit logs.
- External access: do not grant prospects access to the demo tenant. Use a separate POC tenant when approved.

Appendix: SDK snippets
- Python: streaming + retries + tool calling (stable snippet)
- TypeScript: streaming + structured output validation

Notes for maintainers
- Keep this guide stable across UI churn by linking to durable runbooks and by referencing expected outcomes.
- If a live step fails more than twice in a week, update backup mode guidance and file an issue against the product/runbook.
conversation: Soojin Lee (PMM): Can we avoid the term “Optimize” in the guide? Field feedback is that it confuses customers. Suggest: “cost/performance controls” or “serving optimization toolkit.”
Elliot Price (author): Done—updated all headings to “Cost & performance controls” and added a one-line glossary entry mapping it back to product naming when needed.

Markus Klein (SE): Please make the healthcheck step explicit (command/URL + what PASS means). Also add a note: don’t improvise model selection if pinned model is down—use the fallback policy or backup mode.
Elliot Price (author): Added explicit healthcheck instructions (kept generic, links to runbook for exact command). Added callout box in model pins section + backup mode trigger condition.

Sean Gallagher (SRE): Backup mode is good, but add a guardrail: if dashboards are empty, first check time range and project selection before running the traffic job (we’ve had false alarms). Also link SLO page for expected ingestion freshness.
Elliot Price (author): Added “check time range + project selection” as step 1 and linked demo SLOs page under dashboard freshness.
Alex Martinez (DevEx): Snippets should pin SDK versions and avoid experimental flags. Also suggest including both native Redwood SDK and OpenAI-compatible example, but keep one as primary.
Elliot Price (author): Updated snippet pages to pin versions and marked OpenAI-compatible example as optional appendix; primary path remains native Redwood SDK.

Dylan Brooks (Console): For the Console tour, please avoid referencing the flaky “rollouts” sub-tab name; it’s being renamed. Better: “Deployments → Rollouts (A/B)” and mention it may appear as “Experiments” in some builds.
Elliot Price (author): Adjusted wording to be outcome-based and added a note about possible label changes.
checks: Link checker: PASS (2 warnings: internal Confluence links require VPN)
source.release_notes: Internal-only: Adds a canonical Sales Demo Guide with run-of-show, backup mode, troubleshooting, and links to demo tenant runbooks.

source.title: Docs: clarify public status page components + how to read incident updates
source.author: Elliot Price
source.release_notes:
- Public docs now define each status page component (by product surface + region where applicable)
- Added guidance on incident states (Investigating/Identified/Mitigating/Monitoring/Resolved) and what customers should expect
- Added FAQ + Dedicated/Private visibility notes to avoid confusion
source.body:
## Summary
This PR updates customer-facing docs so the public status page is easier to interpret (what each component represents) and incident updates are more consistent (what each update state means, what info is included/excluded).

This work supports the Status Page Transparency Upgrades initiative by reducing ambiguity around component naming and setting correct expectations during active incidents.

## Why
We’ve had repeated customer feedback that the status page was “too coarse” (e.g., Embeddings failures while API looked healthy, streaming-only degradations not reflected clearly). Support also asked for a canonical reference they can link in tickets when customers dispute accuracy.

Key goals for the copy:
- Explain component boundaries in plain language (without leaking internal service topology)
- Define incident update phases + typical contents
- Avoid over-promising SLAs or update cadence
- Clarify how Dedicated/Private incidents are handled on the public page

## What changed
- Added a new doc page: **“Status page components”** with:
  - Component definitions (Hosted API vs Streaming vs Embeddings vs Reranking vs Console)
  - Region scoping guidance (e.g., US-East vs EU-West components)
  - Common examples of “what is / isn’t included”
- Updated incident comms guidance:
  - What each status value means (Operational / Degraded Performance / Partial Outage / Major Outage)
  - What each incident update state means (Investigating / Identified / Mitigating / Monitoring / Resolved)
  - Explicit note: timelines and scope may change during investigation; we’ll correct component selection if new evidence emerges
- Added FAQ targeted at common customer questions:
  - “Why does my app fail but status shows Operational?”
  - “Do you post latency-only issues?”
  - “How do I subscribe to updates / where are post-incident summaries?”
- Dedicated/Private notes:
  - Dedicated: public page may reflect shared control-plane signals and region-level issues; workload/customer-specific degradations may not be publicly visible
  - Private: customer-managed environments are not represented on the public status page; customers should follow their private support/escalation channel

## Content details (copy excerpts)
**New component definition table (excerpt):**
- *Hosted API – Chat/Completions (US-East)*: Request routing + synchronous generation endpoints in US-East. Does not include Streaming disconnects if non-streaming traffic is unaffected.
- *Hosted API – Streaming (US-East)*: SSE/websocket streaming connection stability and stream delivery. Does not imply non-streaming endpoints are impacted.
- *Hosted API – Embeddings (Global)*: Embeddings endpoints across regions; incidents may be marked per-region when impact is isolated.
- *Console*: Console access, incident/summaries viewing, and operational dashboards; does not affect inference availability directly.

**Incident update state guidance (excerpt):**
- *Investigating*: We’re aware of a potential issue and are validating scope/impact. Components may be adjusted as we confirm evidence.
- *Identified*: Root cause (or likely cause) is known and we’re working on mitigation.
- *Mitigating*: Active mitigation underway (rollback, capacity shift, failover, etc.).
- *Monitoring*: Mitigation is in place; we’re monitoring to confirm recovery and prevent regression.
- *Resolved*: Systems are stable; post-incident summary will be published when available.

## Files changed
- `docs/status-page/components.md` (new)
- `docs/status-page/incident-updates.md` (expanded: definitions + examples)
- `docs/status-page/index.md` (navigation + cross-links)
- `docs/support/incident-escalations.md` (added “link customers here” guidance)
- `docs/glossary.md` (added/normalized terms: Partial Outage, Degraded Performance)
- `docs/_includes/statuspage-component-callout.md` (new callout block reused across pages)
- `docs/assets/statuspage-component-example.png` (small annotated screenshot)
- `docs/README.md` (contribution note: don’t add customer-specific examples)
- `docs/redirects.yml` (redirect old “status” URL to new index)

## Testing / validation
- Ran docs build + link check locally
- Verified redirects do not break existing inbound URLs from Support macros
- Proofread against template language constraints (avoid: exact update interval promises; avoid: confirming customer-specific impact)

CI checks:
- `docs-build` ✅
- `markdownlint` ✅
- `link-check` ✅

## Review notes / discussion
- **Wei Chen**: requested softer language around uncertainty early in incidents; replaced “we will confirm” with “we are validating” and added explicit note that affected components may be updated as evidence changes.
- **Dev Patel (Support)**: requested an FAQ entry + a single canonical URL to share in disputed-status tickets; added “If you believe status is inaccurate” section with what info to include when contacting Support.
- **Paula Mendes**: requested alignment with the new incident template verbs (“Mitigating” vs “Mitigation in progress”); updated wording to match the internal template phases.
- **Michael Grant (Legal)**: requested removal of any implied SLA/cadence language; removed “updates every X minutes” phrasing and replaced with “we aim to provide timely updates as we validate scope.”
- **Naomi Feldman (Security/Compliance)**: requested explicit statement that Dedicated/Private customer-specific incidents may not be publicly disclosed; added “visibility and confidentiality” note.

## Checklist
- [x] Copy aligns with status page taxonomy (component naming)
- [x] No customer-identifying details / no internal topology disclosure
- [x] No SLA / cadence commitments
- [x] Support-friendly canonical link + FAQ

## Follow-ups
- PM-612: add a public post-incident summary format page (separate PR)
- DES-245: ensure status page UI labels match component display names used in docs

Merged via squash after approvals; commit message: `Docs: clarify status page components + incident update guidance`.

title: Docs: clarify public status page components + how to read incident updates
preview:
## Summary
This PR updates customer-facing docs so the public status page is easier to interpret (what each component represents) and incident updates are more consistent (what each update state means, what info is included/excluded).

This work supports the Status Page Transparency Upgrades initiative by reducing ambiguity around component naming and setting correct expectations during active incidents.

## Why
We’ve had repeated customer feedback that the status page was “too coarse” (e.g., Embeddings failures while API looked healthy, streaming-only degradations not reflected clearly). Support also asked for a canonical reference they can link in tickets when customers dispute accuracy.

Key goals for the copy:
- Explain component boundaries in plain language (without leaking internal service topology)
- Define incident update phases + typical contents
- Avoid over-promising SLAs or update cadence
- Clarify how Dedicated/Private incidents are handled on the public page

## What changed
- Added a new doc page: **“Status page components”** with:
  - Component definitions (Hosted API vs Streaming vs Embeddings vs Reranking vs Console)
  - Region scoping guidance (e.g., US-East vs EU-West components)
  - Common examples of “what is / isn’t included”
- Updated incident comms guidance:
  - What each status value means (Operational / Degraded Performance / Partial Outage / Major Outage)
  - What each incident update state means (Investigating / Identified / Mitigating / Monitoring / Resolved)
  - Explicit note: timelines and scope may change during investigation; we’ll correct component selection if new evidence emerges
- Added FAQ targeted at common customer questions:
  - “Why does my app fail but status shows Operational?”
  - “Do you post latency-only issues?”
  - “How do I subscribe to updates / where are post-incident summaries?”
- Dedicated/Private notes:
  - Dedicated: public page may reflect shared control-plane signals and region-level issues; workload/customer-specific degradations may not be publicly visible
  - Private: customer-managed environments are not represented on the public status page; customers should follow their private support/escalation channel

## Content details (copy excerpts)
**New component definition table (excerpt):**
- *Hosted API – Chat/Completions (US-East)*: Request routing + synchronous generation endpoints in US-East. Does not include Streaming disconnects if non-streaming traffic is unaffected.
- *Hosted API – Streaming (US-East)*: SSE/websocket streaming connection stability and stream delivery. Does not imply non-streaming endpoints are impacted.
- *Hosted API – Embeddings (Global)*: Embeddings endpoints across regions; incidents may be marked per-region when impact is isolated.
- *Console*: Console access, incident/summaries viewing, and operational dashboards; does not affect inference availability directly.

**Incident update state guidance (excerpt):**
- *Investigating*: We’re aware of a potential issue and are validating scope/impact. Components may be adjusted as we confirm evidence.
- *Identified*: Root cause (or likely cause) is known and we’re working on mitigation.
- *Mitigating*: Active mitigation underway (rollback, capacity shift, failover, etc.).
- *Monitoring*: Mitigation is in place; we’re monitoring to confirm recovery and prevent regression.
- *Resolved*: Systems are stable; post-incident summary will be published when available.

## Files changed
- `docs/status-page/components.md` (new)
- `docs/status-page/incident-updates.md` (expanded: definitions + examples)
- `docs/status-page/index.md` (navigation + cross-links)
- `docs/support/incident-escalations.md` (added “link customers here” guidance)
- `docs/glossary.md` (added/normalized terms: Partial Outage, Degraded Performance)
- `docs/_includes/statuspage-component-callout.md` (new callout block reused across pages)
- `docs/assets/statuspage-component-example.png` (small annotated screenshot)
- `docs/README.md` (contribution note: don’t add customer-specific examples)
- `docs/redirects.yml` (redirect old “status” URL to new index)

## Testing / validation
- Ran docs build + link check locally
- Verified redirects do not break existing inbound URLs from Support macros
- Proofread against template language constraints (avoid: exact update interval promises; avoid: confirming customer-specific impact)

CI checks:
- `docs-build` ✅
- `markdownlint` ✅
- `link-check` ✅

## Review notes / discussion
- **Wei Chen**: requested softer language around uncertainty early in incidents; replaced “we will confirm” with “we are validating” and added explicit note that affected components may be updated as evidence changes.
- **Dev Patel (Support)**: requested an FAQ entry + a single canonical URL to share in disputed-status tickets; added “If you believe status is inaccurate” section with what info to include when contacting Support.
- **Paula Mendes**: requested alignment with the new incident template verbs (“Mitigating” vs “Mitigation in progress”); updated wording to match the internal template phases.
- **Michael Grant (Legal)**: requested removal of any implied SLA/cadence language; removed “updates every X minutes” phrasing and replaced with “we aim to provide timely updates as we validate scope.”
- **Naomi Feldman (Security/Compliance)**: requested explicit statement that Dedicated/Private customer-specific incidents may not be publicly disclosed; added “visibility and confidentiality” note.

## Checklist
- [x] Copy aligns with status page taxonomy (component naming)
- [x] No customer-identifying details / no internal topology disclosure
- [x] No SLA / cadence commitments
- [x] Support-friendly canonical link + FAQ

## Follow-ups
- PM-612: add a public post-incident summary format page (separate PR)
- DES-245: ensure status page UI labels match component display names used in docs

Merged via squash after approvals; commit message: `Docs: clarify status page components + incident update guidance`.

source.title: Key rotation procedure + audit evidence checklist (KMS/Private)
source.author: Kira Thompson
source.description:
Summary: Adds customer/operator-facing documentation for key rotation in Redwood Private/Dedicated contexts, plus an audit-friendly evidence checklist that maps cleanly to common SOC2/ISO narratives. This PR is intended to reduce back-and-forth during enterprise security reviews by making our rotation steps and evidence artifacts explicit and repeatable.

Context / why: Regulated customers (and prospects) are asking for (a) a clear rotation procedure (including rollback criteria) and (b) the exact evidence they can expect to produce/receive (who approved, what changed, when it happened, what logs prove it). This doc set aligns with the ongoing KMS hardening effort and the rotation strategy in ADR-008.

What’s included: 1) New page: “Key rotation procedure (KMS envelope encryption)” covering prerequisites, roles/responsibilities (Customer vs Redwood), staged rollout, dual-read window, cutover, verification, and rollback. 2) New page: “Rotation evidence checklist” that can be used as a runbook attachment and as audit evidence. Includes templates for a rotation log entry, required screenshots/exports, and pointers to audit log event types. 3) Updates to navigation + cross-links from the Private KMS Operator Guide and Security FAQ sections to reduce discoverability issues.

Audience: Private operators, SRE/on-call, Security/GRC reviewers, Sales/SE support during enterprise security reviews.

Notes on scope: This is documentation-only and does not introduce new technical behavior. It references implementation behavior that is landing in the core repo (dual-read support, audit log events, healthchecks). Where behavior differs between Hosted vs Private, we call it out explicitly.

References: - ADR-008 (Key rotation strategy: dual-read window + cutover criteria) - Linear: ENG-4826 (evidence artifacts), PM-913 (customer expectations), ENG-4821 (initiative epic) - Support: SUP-19405 (customer evidence request / cutover questions)

Content highlights (key decisions captured in docs): - Rotation cadence: documented as customer-driven (typical quarterly/annual), with Redwood providing a repeatable procedure + evidence artifacts; the doc avoids committing to a universal cadence but includes guidance and defaults used in regulated deployments. - Dual-read / dual-decrypt window: documented as the recommended approach to avoid hard cutovers; includes a minimum observation window and explicit cutover criteria (error-rate and decrypt-mismatch signals). - Rollback: explicitly documented; includes triggers (e.g., elevated KMS errors, decrypt mismatches) and the fastest safe rollback path (toggle to prior key version + keep dual-read during investigation). - Evidence: checklist enumerates approvals, change tickets, timestamps, config diffs, audit log exports, and validation outputs from installer preflight (Private).

Screenshots/examples: Redacted examples are included (ARNs, tenant IDs, regions). Please flag anything that looks too close to production formatting.

Test plan: - Local docs build: `pnpm install && pnpm build` - Link check: `pnpm lint:links` - Preview reviewed in Netlify: see CI checks.

Checklist: - [x] Reviewed with Security (terminology + evidence expectations) - [x] Reviewed with SRE (rollback triggers + operational steps) - [x] Reviewed with Product (customer-facing positioning) - [x] Added cross-links from Private KMS operator guide - [x] Added “last reviewed” footer + owners for ongoing maintenance

Follow-ups (not in this PR): - Once PR redwood/pr-18541 lands, update evidence page to include final audit log event names/fields (placeholders included). - Once dashboards ship (observability-pack/pr-844), add a “Verification dashboards” subsection with canonical panel links per environment.
commits: docs(kms): add key rotation procedure page with dual-read and rollback guidance
docs(compliance): add rotation evidence checklist + rotation log template
docs(nav): wire rotation docs into Private deployment navigation and add cross-links
docs(style): align terminology with Security standard (KEK/DEK, envelope encryption) and add owner/last-reviewed footer
chore(ci): fix link lint false positives for generated anchors
source.review_conversation:
Elliot Price (Docs) 2025-11-09: The structure reads well. Two requests: (1) move “Evidence checklist” earlier in the page so operators see it before step-by-step; (2) add a small “Who does what” table (Customer vs Redwood vs Joint) to reduce ambiguity in Private deployments.

Kira Thompson (author) 2025-11-09: Done. Evidence checklist is now linked at the top and the responsibilities table is added under “Prereqs and ownership”.

Marcus Lin (Security Eng) 2025-11-10: Please tighten language around ‘fallback’ during KMS outage so it doesn’t imply we can decrypt without KMS. We can degrade availability and preserve correctness, but we should not suggest bypassing KMS. Also, add a note that key disable/delete is treated as an incident and requires Security escalation.

Kira Thompson 2025-11-10: Updated. The outage section now states: no bypass; behavior is fail-closed for decrypt, with bounded retries/circuit breaker to avoid storms. Added escalation note for key disable/delete.

Olga Petrov (Compliance) 2025-11-11: Evidence checklist looks good. Can we explicitly call out acceptable substitutes when a customer cannot share screenshots (e.g., export from SIEM / audit-log-exporter)? Also add a “Retention” line for what we recommend customers retain.

Kira Thompson 2025-11-11: Added “acceptable substitutes” under each evidence row + a retention recommendation section. Kept it phrased as guidance, not policy.

Sean Gallagher (SRE) 2025-11-12: Rollback criteria: include concrete thresholds placeholders and tell readers to use environment-specific alert thresholds (since we tuned these recently). Also please reference the runbook path for decrypt failures.

Kira Thompson 2025-11-12: Added a “Suggested rollback triggers” callout with placeholders and a note to follow environment thresholds; linked to the decrypt failures runbook and added a short troubleshooting entry.

Naomi Feldman (PM) 2025-11-13: One wording issue: ‘quarterly/annual’ is fine, but add a line that cadence is customer-controlled and may be contractually defined; avoid implying a default SLA.

Kira Thompson 2025-11-13: Updated intro + cadence section to be explicit about customer-controlled cadence and contractual terms.

Elliot Price 2025-11-14: LGTM after last edits. Ship it.

Marcus Lin 2025-11-14: Approved.

Olga Petrov 2025-11-14: Approved.
ci_checks: spellcheck: pass (1 word added to allowlist: 'cutover')
source.release_notes: Docs: Added key rotation procedure and audit evidence checklist for KMS-integrated Private/Dedicated deployments; includes dual-read window, cutover/rollback guidance, and evidence templates for SOC2/ISO reviews.

source.title: Private: publish KMS integration operator guide (setup, validation, troubleshooting)
source.author: Kira Thompson
source.release_notes: Docs: Added Private deployment operator guide for customer-managed KMS integrations, including required permissions, validation/preflight steps, and a troubleshooting matrix for common failure modes (AccessDenied, throttling, timeouts, invalid ciphertext).
source.description:
Context
Regulated customers evaluating Redwood Private are consistently asking for a single, operator-oriented KMS integration guide that explains (1) what Redwood encrypts and where envelope encryption happens, (2) which permissions are required and why, (3) how to validate the integration before turning on production traffic, and (4) what the expected system behavior is during KMS degradation/outage.

This PR publishes the first version of the "Private deployment: Customer KMS integration operator guide" on the docs site, incorporating feedback from Support (SUP-19341 / SUP-19377) and Sales/SE security reviews (SUP-19405). It is written to be evidence-friendly for enterprise security questionnaires and to reduce back-and-forth during onboarding.

What’s included
- New operator guide page for Private deployments covering: prerequisites, supported KMS patterns (AWS/GCP/Azure conceptual flows), required IAM permissions, key/region configuration requirements, and key ID/ARN gotchas.
- A validation/preflight section that mirrors the installer checks (linking out to the installer’s validation report format) and provides copy/paste commands/operators steps to confirm access.
- Troubleshooting matrix with symptoms -> likely root cause -> how to confirm -> remediation, focused on the failure modes we see most often: AccessDenied, throttling, timeouts/network issues, wrong key ID/region, key disabled, and ciphertext mismatch during rotation.
- “Operational behavior” section describing Redwood’s safe defaults: timeouts/retry budget guidance, circuit-breaker/degrade behavior at a high level, and when operators should page Redwood vs investigate their KMS/IAM.
- Cross-links to the rotation procedure page (to be published in a follow-up PR) and internal runbooks for SRE (for Redwood staff only).

What’s not included (intentionally)
- No new cryptographic primitives/algorithms; this is operational documentation only.
- No provider-specific Terraform modules or customer IAM templates embedded inline; we instead link to the Private deployment reference config examples and keep this guide focused on the “what/why/how to validate.”

Information architecture
This guide lives under: Private Deployments -> Security & Compliance -> Key management (KMS).

Screenshots/diagrams
- Included one simple sequence diagram for envelope encryption flow in Private.
- Avoided UI screenshots that will churn; instead used CLI and configuration snippets.

Verification
- Local build: `pnpm i && pnpm build`.
- Link check: `pnpm lint:links`.
- Verified no broken anchors in the new troubleshooting matrix.

Follow-ups
- Rotation procedure + evidence checklist PR (tracked in PM-913 / ENG-4826).
- Add a short "KMS readiness" checklist to the Private install/upgrade guide landing page.

Checklist
- [x] Docs align with Security review checklist terminology (AccessDenied vs Unauthorized; "key disabled" vs "pending deletion")
- [x] Troubleshooting content validated against recent Support tickets (SUP-19341, SUP-19377)
- [x] Added explicit guidance on least-privilege vs break-glass roles (operator action vs Redwood action)
- [x] Included “known pitfalls” callouts: wrong region, alias vs key ID confusion, missing `kms:Decrypt` on runtime role

Notes for reviewers
- Please scrutinize the troubleshooting table for accuracy and whether the remediation steps are actionable.
- SRE: please confirm the “expected behavior during KMS throttling/outage” section matches current alerting and on-call guidance.
- Security: please confirm we are not overpromising (especially around audit logs and rotation).

docs(private): add validation/preflight steps and common permission pitfalls
docs(private): add troubleshooting matrix for KMS failures (AccessDenied/throttling/timeout)
docs(security): clarify envelope encryption terminology + key identifiers (ARN vs alias)
chore: fix broken anchors, run formatter, tighten link lint
changed_areas: site/navigation: add operator guide to Private Deployments > Security & Compliance section
content/private/security: new operator guide markdown page
diagrams: add simple sequence diagram for envelope encryption flow
linking: references to installer validation report and rotation docs placeholder
source.notable_snippet:
Troubleshooting (excerpt)
Symptom: `KMS AccessDenied` when decrypting
Likely cause: runtime principal missing `kms:Decrypt` on the configured key
How to confirm: check KMS audit logs for `AccessDenied` with the runtime role ARN; run preflight validation and inspect the permission check output
Remediation: update the runtime decrypt role policy to include `kms:Decrypt` (+ `kms:DescribeKey`); ensure the key policy trusts the role; re-run preflight

Symptom: elevated 5xx correlated with KMS throttling
Likely cause: request burst to KMS exceeds account or key limits
How to confirm: check KMS throttle metrics; inspect Redwood KMS error taxonomy logs for `Throttling`
Remediation: confirm retry budget defaults; reduce burst concurrency; request KMS quota increase; contact Redwood if sustained after quota changes
review_conversation: Elliot Price (Docs) — 2025-11-04: The structure is solid. Please move the 'Operational behavior' section earlier (right after prerequisites) so operators understand failure semantics before configuration details. Also: add one paragraph on what data is encrypted with DEKs vs what is not in scope (e.g., network traffic is separate).
Kira Thompson (Author) — 2025-11-04: Done. Reordered sections and added an explicit 'What’s encrypted' subsection + an 'Out of scope' note for transport/security layers.

Marcus Lin (Security) — 2025-11-05: A few wording issues: avoid 'guarantees' around outage handling; use 'designed to' and link to on-call escalation guidance. Also, under IAM permissions, please separate runtime decrypt permissions from rotation/admin permissions—auditors care about separation of duties.

Kira Thompson (Author) — 2025-11-06: Updated language to avoid guarantees and added a clear separation between Runtime role vs Rotation/Admin role. Added a callout referencing least-privilege + break-glass workflow (no internal links to restricted systems).

Sean Gallagher (SRE) — 2025-11-07: Troubleshooting matrix is good but needs a crisp 'When to page Redwood' row. Also: in throttling remediation, mention jitter/backoff and that retries are bounded by a configured budget; otherwise operators will try to crank retries up.

Kira Thompson (Author) — 2025-11-08: Added 'When to page Redwood' guidance and expanded throttling remediation to explicitly say: bounded retries, exponential backoff w/ jitter, do not increase retry budget beyond defaults without Redwood guidance (links to config guardrails note).

Vivek Kulkarni (Private Deployments) — 2025-11-09: Please ensure the validation steps mirror what installer preflight prints. Also the guide should remind customers to validate from within the cluster/VPC (not from a laptop) to catch routing/DNS issues.
Kira Thompson (Author) — 2025-11-09: Aligned wording with the installer preflight output format and added a 'Run validation from the same network path' callout + examples.

Bruno Silva (Infra/Installer) — 2025-11-10: Minor: AWS examples should prefer key ARN over alias for clarity; aliases are fine but people misconfigure cross-account. Also add a note that key policies must allow the role in addition to IAM policy.
Kira Thompson (Author) — 2025-11-10: Updated examples to lead with key ARN, moved alias guidance into a 'pitfalls' note, and added the explicit reminder about key policy trust requirements.
Elliot Price (Docs) — 2025-11-12: Final nit: the H2 heading 'Permissions' should be 'Required permissions (least privilege)' to match our docs style. Otherwise LGTM.
Kira Thompson (Author) — 2025-11-12: Updated heading. Ready to merge.
Elliot Price (Docs) — 2025-11-12: Approved.
Marcus Lin (Security) — 2025-11-12: Approved.
Sean Gallagher (SRE) — 2025-11-12: Approved.
source.post_merge_notes: Shared link with Sales/SE and Support; follow-up task opened to add rotation evidence checklist page and link it from this guide (PM-913 / ENG-4826).

title: Private: publish KMS integration operator guide (setup, validation, troubleshooting)
preview:
Context
Regulated customers evaluating Redwood Private are consistently asking for a single, operator-oriented KMS integration guide that explains (1) what Redwood encrypts and where envelope encryption happens, (2) which permissions are required and why, (3) how to validate the integration before turning on production traffic, and (4) what the expected system behavior is during KMS degradation/outage.

This PR publishes the first version of the "Private deployment: Customer KMS integration operator guide" on the docs site, incorporating feedback from Support (SUP-19341 / SUP-19377) and Sales/SE security reviews (SUP-19405). It is written to be evidence-friendly for enterprise security questionnaires and to reduce back-and-forth during onboarding.

What’s included
- New operator guide page for Private deployments covering: prerequisites, supported KMS patterns (AWS/GCP/Azure conceptual flows), required IAM permissions, key/region configuration requirements, and key ID/ARN gotchas.
- A validation/preflight section that mirrors the installer checks (linking out to the installer’s validation report format) and provides copy/paste commands/operators steps to confirm access.
- Troubleshooting matrix with symptoms -> likely root cause -> how to confirm -> remediation, focused on the failure modes we see most often: AccessDenied, throttling, timeouts/network issues, wrong key ID/region, key disabled, and ciphertext mismatch during rotation.
- “Operational behavior” section describing Redwood’s safe defaults: timeouts/retry budget guidance, circuit-breaker/degrade behavior at a high level, and when operators should page Redwood vs investigate their KMS/IAM.
- Cross-links to the rotation procedure page (to be published in a follow-up PR) and internal runbooks for SRE (for Redwood staff only).

What’s not included (intentionally)
- No new cryptographic primitives/algorithms; this is operational documentation only.
- No provider-specific Terraform modules or customer IAM templates embedded inline; we instead link to the Private deployment reference config examples and keep this guide focused on the “what/why/how to validate.”

Information architecture
This guide lives under: Private Deployments -> Security & Compliance -> Key management (KMS).

Screenshots/diagrams
- Included one simple sequence diagram for envelope encryption flow in Private.
- Avoided UI screenshots that will churn; instead used CLI and configuration snippets.

Verification
- Local build: `pnpm i && pnpm build`.
- Link check: `pnpm lint:links`.
- Verified no broken anchors in the new troubleshooting matrix.

Follow-ups
- Rotation procedure + evidence checklist PR (tracked in PM-913 / ENG-4826).
- Add a short "KMS readiness" checklist to the Private install/upgrade guide landing page.

Checklist
- [x] Docs align with Security review checklist terminology (AccessDenied vs Unauthorized; "key disabled" vs "pending deletion")
- [x] Troubleshooting content validated against recent Support tickets (SUP-19341, SUP-19377)
- [x] Added explicit guidance on least-privilege vs break-glass roles (operator action vs Redwood action)
- [x] Included “known pitfalls” callouts: wrong region, alias vs key ID confusion, missing `kms:Decrypt` on runtime role

Notes for reviewers
- Please scrutinize the troubleshooting table for accuracy and whether the remediation steps are actionable.
- SRE: please confirm the “expected behavior during KMS throttling/outage” section matches current alerting and on-call guidance.
- Security: please confirm we are not overpromising (especially around audit logs and rotation).
