source.title: Internal ops docs: Provision-to-ready readiness states, remediation, and dashboards/runbooks
source.author: Talia Benson
source.release_notes: Internal-only documentation update for Dedicated/Fleet operators: readiness state interpretation, safe remediation steps, and links to dashboards/runbooks. No product behavior changes.
source.description:
Motivation: With the provision-to-ready automation rollout, we need a single, up-to-date internal operator guide that explains (a) what each readiness state means, (b) what to do when nodes are stuck or failing validations, and (c) where to look first (dashboards/logs/runbooks) without rediscovering tribal knowledge in Slack threads. This PR updates the internal docs to match ADR-017 readiness semantics and the current control-plane/Fleet Readiness Service behavior, and standardizes remediation guidance (drain/quarantine/reimage order) to reduce risk during on-call.

Summary of changes: Adds an operator-focused "Provision-to-ready operations" page that describes the canonical states (Allocated/Provisioning/Validating/Ready/Quarantined), the authoritative signals for transitions (agent heartbeat + validation artifacts + control-plane writes), and practical triage flows for common failures (kubelet join, driver parity, GPU health, NCCL smoke test, perf canary). Adds a "stuck in Validating" decision tree with time-based thresholds and clear ownership boundaries (Infra/Fleet vs Platform vs SRE). Links the docs to the current dashboards and runbooks, and includes a quickstart section for internal console usage (filters, drill-down, and where to find failure reasons/artifacts). Also documents the image channel rollout + rollback basics (candidate/stable), including the audit event trail for channel changes.

Notable clarifications: (1) Quarantine is a safety state used when failures repeat or indicate hardware/network fabric issues; it is not a generic catch-all for "slow provisioning". (2) Reimage is the preferred first-line remediation for image/driver drift failures; quarantine is preferred for GPU health or NCCL/fabric failures after retry policy is exhausted. (3) Do not manually set readiness state except via the approved operator workflow; state changes are audited.

Docs links added/updated in this PR: Provision-to-ready failures triage runbook; SLO/time-to-ready page; dashboard catalog; gameday plan; ADR-017 reference; Fleet Readiness Service API overview.

Rollout note: These docs assume the staged rollout feature flag is enabled only for us-east A100 pools; wording is careful to avoid implying global availability. References SUP-28433 for the "stuck in Validating" support pattern and INT-1042 for dashboard access/least-privilege.

Screenshots: Included updated internal console screenshots (redacted pool IDs) showing readiness state filters and failure reason drill-down.

Testing: Ran docs build locally and link checker; CI includes markdown lint + internal link validation.
commits: docs: add provision-to-ready operations guide (states, triage, remediation)
docs: add stuck-in-validating decision tree + ownership boundaries
docs: link dashboards + runbooks + SLO page; standardize terminology to ADR-017
docs: add image channel rollout/rollback notes + audit trail references
chore(docs): fix broken anchors; tighten language around quarantine vs reimage
docs: add internal console usage notes + redacted screenshots
source.review_conversation:
Vanessa Ortiz (Infra/Fleet): "This is close. Two asks: (1) please make the remediation order explicit (drain -> quarantine -> reimage) and include the safe exceptions, and (2) the NCCL smoke test section should mention the known flake mode + when to re-run vs quarantine." Author (Talia Benson): "Updated remediation sections with a numbered flow, plus added a callout box for NCCL flake mitigation and the retry/backoff pointer. Also added the 'do not quarantine for slow provisioning' warning." Vanessa Ortiz: "Looks good now. +1."

Aisha Bello (SRE): "Can you add the actual SLI/SLO names we page on, and a link to the dashboard panel for P95 time-to-ready? Also, the 'page vs ticket' guidance should match our rollout tuning (ticket for elevated failure rate unless >X% for 15m)." Author: "Added the metric names used in alerts (time_to_ready_seconds p50/p95 + step_failure_rate) and linked to the dashboard section; updated guidance to match the initial tuning notes."

Ethan Park (Platform): "Minor: In the readiness semantics, please align wording with ADR-017: control plane stores current state; events/validation artifacts are attached but not event-sourced. Also clarify who can mutate state (service accounts vs operator overrides)." Author: "Aligned language, added an 'Authoritative state' paragraph, and added a table for read vs write permissions + audit expectations."

Connor O'Brien (Release Eng): "Please add a short section on rollout comms: where the go/no-go checklist lives and who signs off." Author: "Added a 'Staged rollout' section with checklist link and sign-offs; noted kill switch behavior." 

Fiona McCarthy (SecOps): "Thanks for calling out audit. Please add a line that image channel changes must be done via the approved workflow and are logged; no manual edits in Terraform state." Author: "Added explicit guidance and linked the audit events page."

Approvals: Vanessa Ortiz approved; Aisha Bello approved; Ethan Park approved; Connor O'Brien approved; Fiona McCarthy approved.
source.merge_outcome: Merged to main via squash; no follow-up required for release train. Internal docs published after merge.

source.title: Dedicated: document autoscaling bounds + predictive mode (config, semantics, troubleshooting)
source.author: Elliot Price
release_notes: Docs: Added Dedicated autoscaling policy bounds (min/max) configuration guide + semantics and precedence.
Docs: Added Predictive Mode overview, when to use it, and safety/override behavior.
Docs: Added troubleshooting guidance for bound-hit saturation, queue buildup, and forecast staleness.
source.description: Summary This PR updates customer-facing documentation for Dedicated autoscaling policy bounds and Predictive Mode. It is written to support pilot->GA rollout and reduce support load by clarifying semantics (what is enforced, what is advisory), recommended starting ranges, and what to do when customers hit max bounds or experience queueing during ramps. The new docs cover: configuring min/max replicas (and GPU equivalent guidance), cooldown behavior, Predictive Mode basics, signal arbitration (reactive overrides predictions), and troubleshooting steps with references to the new dashboard panels/metrics. Motivation Recent Dedicated escalations (SUP-28411/SUP-28437) and pilot feedback indicated confusion around (1) whether max is a hard ceiling, (2) why scale-up may stop while queue grows, (3) how Predictive Mode interacts with reactive signals and cooldowns, and (4) what customers should do during region capacity shortages where min cannot be met. This PR consolidates answers into a single doc set and aligns wording with the ADR semantics (hard invariants: never exceed max, never drop below min; deterministic clamping; emit bounded-state signals). Scope / What changed - Added docs page: "Dedicated autoscaling policy" (min/max, cooldowns, predictive toggle, examples for common workloads) - Added section: "Predictive Mode" (what it is, required history, confidence, staleness, fallback to reactive) - Added "Recommended starting points" including baseline min for SLA protection and max as spend guardrail; includes guidance to iterate based on observed queue depth and bound-hit rate - Added troubleshooting playbook: "Max-bound saturation", "Oscillation/jitter", "Forecast stale/unavailable", "Min not met due to capacity shortage" with suggested mitigations and when to contact support - Updated API/Console configuration snippets and terminology to match implementation (field names and UI labels) - Added small FAQ (e.g., "Does Predictive Mode scale to zero?", "Can I exceed max during incidents?" -> no; use contract/CS process) Not in scope - No product behavior changes; this is documentation only - No full runbook content (SRE runbook lives in internal Confluence); we link to customer-facing troubleshooting steps and support contact points Testing - Docs site build + link check - Verified code snippets against Console API endpoint shapes referenced in ENG-22141 (as of 2025-08-21) Rollout notes - Docs are written to be feature-flag aware: clearly marked "Available by request during pilot" and "GA" sections. Where behavior differs between pilot builds and GA target (e.g., confidence indicator display), language is guarded to avoid overpromising. Screenshots are intentionally minimal; we rely on text that matches UX labels in DES-981 to avoid churn during final UI polish. Links - Linear: PM-1842, ENG-22149 - Support references: SUP-28501 (how to set bounds), SUP-28437 (daily ramp), SUP-28479 (oscillation) - Internal semantics: ADR "Autoscaling bounds precedence and safety invariants" (referenced but not linked in public docs) - Observability: references the public-facing metric names that appear in the Dedicated dashboard (bound hit rate, desired vs bounded replicas, queue depth) Changed areas/files (high-level) - docs/dedicated/autoscaling-policy.md (new) - docs/dedicated/predictive-mode.md (new) - docs/dedicated/troubleshooting/autoscaling.md (new) - docs/reference/api/dedicated-scaling-policy.md (updated) - docs/console/dedicated-scaling-policy-ui.md (updated) - docs/shared/glossary.md (updated terms: bound, desired, bounded desired, cooldown) - redirects + nav sidebar updates Example snippet added (from docs) JSON example: { "autoscaling_policy": { "min_replicas": 4, "max_replicas": 24, "cooldown_seconds": 180, "predictive_mode": "ENABLED" } } Notes: - The system will clamp requested desired replicas into [min_replicas, max_replicas]. - If predictive signal is stale/unavailable, the autoscaler falls back to reactive scaling (still respecting bounds).
commits: docs(dedicated): add autoscaling policy bounds page + examples
docs(dedicated): add predictive mode overview + FAQ
docs(troubleshooting): add bounded scaling troubleshooting guide
docs(api): update dedicated scaling policy reference and field names
docs(nav): add Dedicated autoscaling section to sidebar + redirects
source.review_conversation: Kira Thompson (review): The bounds semantics section is close, but we need to explicitly state max is a hard ceiling and cannot be exceeded even during incident mitigation. Also add a short paragraph on what happens if customers set min > available regional capacity. Elliot Price (author): Updated: added "Hard invariants" callout (never exceed max; never drop below min) and a new "When min cannot be met" section explaining degraded state + retry and to contact support for capacity planning. Lauren Bishop (review): Please add a "Recommended starting points" section. Customers keep asking "what should my min be for SLA". It should be framed as guidance, not guarantees. Elliot Price (author): Added recommended ranges with disclaimers + a simple method: set min to cover typical baseline + ensure p95 during low traffic; set max as budget ceiling; iterate based on queue depth and bound-hit metrics. Included example values for steady workloads vs daily ramp workloads. Rafael Mendes (review): Troubleshooting needs to be careful not to tell customers to disable predictive mode without context; also include what to collect before opening a ticket (deployment_id, region, timeframe, bound-hit rate, queue depth). Elliot Price (author): Adjusted: added "Before contacting support" checklist and a decision tree: confirm bound saturation vs prediction staleness vs cooldown effects; disable predictive only if oscillation suspected and reactive scaling is stable. Allison Grant (review): Metric names: please use the exact public metric labels shown in Console dashboard (Desired Replicas, Bounded Desired Replicas, Max Bound Hit Rate, Queue Depth). Avoid adding internal Prometheus names into customer docs. Elliot Price (author): Done. Replaced internal metric identifiers with dashboard label names and added a note: "Your dashboard labels may vary slightly by region during pilot." Tessa Morgan (review): UX wording: Console uses "Predictive Mode" and a separate "Fallback to Reactive" toggle is not exposed; don't mention it as a customer setting. Also ensure validation messages match DES-981 (min/max errors). Elliot Price (author): Cleaned up: predictive fallback described as system behavior; removed references to a toggle. Updated validation examples to match current UI copy. Approvals: Kira Thompson approved, Lauren Bishop approved, Rafael Mendes approved after edits, Allison Grant approved, Tessa Morgan approved.
source.merge_outcome: Merged to main; published with next docs deploy window (2025-08-23 18:00 UTC).

It is written to support pilot->GA rollout and reduce support load by clarifying semantics (what is enforced, what is advisory), recommended starting ranges, and what to do when customers hit max bounds or experience queueing during ramps. The new docs cover: configuring min/max replicas (and GPU equivalent guidance), cooldown behavior, Predictive Mode basics, signal arbitration (reactive overrides predictions), and troubleshooting steps with references to the new dashboard panels/metrics. Motivation Recent Dedicated escalations (SUP-28411/SUP-28437) and pilot feedback indicated confusion around (1) whether max is a hard ceiling, (2) why scale-up may stop while queue grows, (3) how Predictive Mode interacts with reactive signals and cooldowns, and (4) what customers should do during region capacity shortages where min cannot be met. This PR consolidates answers into a single doc set and aligns wording with the ADR semantics (hard invariants: never exceed max, never drop below min; deterministic clamping; emit bounded-state signals). Scope / What changed - Added docs page: "Dedicated autoscaling policy" (min/max, cooldowns, predictive toggle, examples for common workloads) - Added section: "Predictive Mode" (what it is, required history, confidence, staleness, fallback to reactive) - Added "Recommended starting points" including baseline min for SLA protection and max as spend guardrail; includes guidance to iterate based on observed queue depth and bound-hit rate - Added troubleshooting playbook: "Max-bound saturation", "Oscillation/jitter", "Forecast stale/unavailable", "Min not met due to capacity shortage" with suggested mitigations and when to contact support - Updated API/Console configuration snippets and terminology to match implementation (field names and UI labels) - Added small FAQ (e.g., "Does Predictive Mode scale to zero?", "Can I exceed max during incidents?" -> no; use contract/CS process) Not in scope - No product behavior changes; this is documentation only - No full runbook content (SRE runbook lives in internal Confluence); we link to customer-facing troubleshooting steps and support contact points Testing - Docs site build + link check - Verified code snippets against Console API endpoint shapes referenced in ENG-22141 (as of 2025-08-21) Rollout notes - Docs are written to be feature-flag aware: clearly marked "Available by request during pilot" and "GA" sections. Where behavior differs between pilot builds and GA target (e.g., confidence indicator display), language is guarded to avoid overpromising. Screenshots are intentionally minimal; we rely on text that matches UX labels in DES-981 to avoid churn during final UI polish. Links - Linear: PM-1842, ENG-22149 - Support references: SUP-28501 (how to set bounds), SUP-28437 (daily ramp), SUP-28479 (oscillation) - Internal semantics: ADR "Autoscaling bounds precedence and safety invariants" (referenced but not linked in public docs) - Observability: references the public-facing metric names that appear in the Dedicated dashboard (bound hit rate, desired vs bounded replicas, queue depth) Changed areas/files (high-level) - docs/dedicated/autoscaling-policy.md (new) - docs/dedicated/predictive-mode.md (new) - docs/dedicated/troubleshooting/autoscaling.md (new) - docs/reference/api/dedicated-scaling-policy.md (updated) - docs/console/dedicated-scaling-policy-ui.md (updated) - docs/shared/glossary.md (updated terms: bound, desired, bounded desired, cooldown) - redirects + nav sidebar updates Example snippet added (from docs) JSON example: { "autoscaling_policy": { "min_replicas": 4, "max_replicas": 24, "cooldown_seconds": 180, "predictive_mode": "ENABLED" } } Notes: - The system will clamp requested desired replicas into [min_replicas, max_replicas]. - If predictive signal is stale/unavailable, the autoscaler falls back to reactive scaling (still respecting bounds).

source.title: Docs: Private observability minimum pack (install, dashboards, alerts, log export examples)
source.author: Elliot Price
source.release_notes: Documentation only. Adds the supported “Private observability minimum pack” guide (install + validation), dashboard catalog with panel intent, alert meaning + first-response steps, and log export configuration examples for Loki and Splunk HEC. Includes security guidance on redaction and retention defaults.
source.description:
Context: This PR documents the v1 “Private observability minimum pack” shipping with Redwood Private (VPC and on-prem). The goal is to make installs supportable without bespoke enablement by providing (1) clear install/upgrade steps, (2) a stable dashboard catalog, (3) actionable alert descriptions with runbook links, and (4) supported log export examples suitable for private environments.

What’s included: Adds a new doc set under /private/observability/minimum-pack covering prerequisites (Prometheus/Grafana/Loki optional), Helm values required for the observability-pack integration, and post-install validation steps that match installer checks. Documents the dashboard catalog (Runtime Overview, Control Plane Health, GPU/Node Saturation, Queueing/Batching, and Prometheus Health) with “what to look at” notes and ownership. Adds an alert reference section mapping each baseline alert to severity, likely causes, and first actions (including how to silence/tune safely). Adds log pipeline/export guidance: recommended structured log fields, redaction rules, retention defaults, and tested sink examples for Loki and Splunk HEC (plus “bring-your-own” notes for ELK/OTLP).

Not included / non-goals: No premium/federated observability features (multi-cluster aggregation, long retention sizing, full tracing sampling policy UI). This doc intentionally focuses on what Support/SRE will treat as “supported minimum.”

Why now: Support cases SUP-49210 and SUP-49238 showed gaps in “what should I see after install/upgrade?” and “which alerts are expected/noisy?”. This doc set provides a single canonical reference and standardizes runbook links embedded in alert annotations.

Notes: The configuration examples are written to be air-gap friendly (no external chart repos required at runtime) and avoid advising customers to increase metric label cardinality. Security guidance explicitly calls out that request/response payload logging is disabled by default and should remain off for most customers.

Validation: Verified docs against current Helm values from redwood-helm-charts wiring and smoke test harness outputs; cross-checked alert names and dashboard UIDs with observability-pack bundle version v0.9.3.

Checklist: [x] Install steps include both Helm-only and installer-managed flows [x] Includes post-install verification and common failure modes (scrapes, datasource auth) [x] Runbook links included in each alert meaning section [x] Security review notes for redaction/retention [x] Support enablement section updated (what info to request in an escalation)

Example snippet (Splunk HEC values): helm upgrade --install redwood-private redwood/private -f values.yaml --set observability.logs.enabled=true --set observability.logs.sinks.splunkHec.enabled=true --set observability.logs.sinks.splunkHec.endpoint=https://splunk.example:8088/services/collector --set observability.logs.sinks.splunkHec.tokenFromSecret.name=splunk-hec --set observability.logs.sinks.splunkHec.tokenFromSecret.key=token

Example snippet (Loki values): observability: { logs: { enabled: true, sinks: { loki: { enabled: true, endpoint: "http://loki.monitoring.svc:3100" } } } }
commits: docs(private/observability): add minimum pack overview and install guide
docs(private/observability): add dashboard catalog + screenshots placeholders and UID stability note
docs(private/observability): add alert reference (severity, meaning, first actions, runbook links)
docs(private/logging): add log pipeline guidance and Splunk HEC + Loki config examples
docs(security): add redaction + retention guidance and audit log interaction notes
chore(docs): fix nav, add redirects, lint markdown
source.conversation:
Vivek Kulkarni (Product) review: “This is close. Two requests: (1) call out what ‘supported’ means explicitly (Support will ask for these dashboards/alerts before escalating), and (2) add a quick table of ‘minimum pack components’ vs ‘customer-managed’ (Prometheus/Grafana) so it’s unambiguous.” Elliot Price (author): “Added a ‘Supported scope’ box in overview + a components responsibility table in install-and-verify.”

Neha Kapoor (SRE) review: “Alert reference looks good but please add concrete first-response steps and include the exact runbook link format we’re using. Also ensure the latency burn alerts mention ‘check scrape freshness’ before assuming runtime regressions.” Elliot: “Updated each alert entry to include ‘What to do first’ bullets; added a preflight section on scrape freshness and datasource auth; standardized runbook link anchors.”

Logan Wright (Telemetry) review: “Be careful advising users to enable high-cardinality labels. The logging doc currently suggests adding model + route labels to metrics in one example; that’s not supported for Private.” Elliot: “Removed the metrics label example; replaced with guidance to use dashboards’ aggregated views and to avoid per-tenant/model breakdowns unless explicitly instructed by Redwood.”

Marcus Lin (Security) review: “Please make it explicit that request/response payloads must not be logged by default; redact Authorization, cookies, and any user-provided prompt text. Also add retention guidance aligned with audit logging doc.” Elliot: “Added ‘Do not log payloads’ callout, explicit redaction list, and a retention defaults section (ops logs vs audit logs) with pointers.”

Dev Patel (Support) review: “Can we add a section: ‘What to collect for Support’ (grafana snapshot, /metrics scrape status, collector buffer stats, installer version) and link to SUP-49210 workaround?” Elliot: “Added escalation data checklist and a small known issues callout (dashboards empty after upgrade) with workaround steps.”

Final approvals: Vivek approved after scope language update; Neha approved after alert runbook links; Marcus approved after redaction callouts; Logan approved after cardinality note fix; Dev approved after support checklist addition.
source.merge_outcome: Merged to main; included in docs site deploy 2025-08-19. No product behavior changes.

source.title: Update Private upgrade docs: new installer commands, troubleshooting, rollback guidance
source.author: Elliot Price
source.description: Summary: Align Private (VPC + on-prem/air-gapped) upgrade/rollback documentation with the new installer workflow: plan -> prechecks -> backup -> apply -> verify -> rollback (if needed). This updates the operator-facing steps, introduces troubleshooting playbooks for common precheck and upgrade failures, and clarifies rollback guarantees/limitations (esp. around schema migrations and partial rollouts). Motivation: Support escalations and design partner feedback indicated the previous docs were ambiguous about (a) which checks are blocking vs warning, (b) what gets backed up and how to validate restore readiness, and (c) when rollback is safe vs when manual intervention is required. This PR is the docs companion to private-installer changes (upgrade plan generator, preflight framework, backup orchestrator, rollback state machine) and Helm ordering changes (control plane before data plane). Audience/visibility: Internal runbooks + external-ish docs pages that are shared with Private customers under guidance. What changed (high level): 1) Rewrote the main upgrade procedure to use new commands and phase naming: Generate plan, Run prechecks, Create backup, Apply upgrade (control plane then data plane), Post-upgrade verification, Capture artifacts/support bundle. 2) Added a troubleshooting index for: precheck failures (storage headroom, DB connectivity, OIDC reachability, TLS/DNS), migration lock timeouts, stuck Helm hooks/jobs, artifact/bundle verification errors for air-gapped upgrades, and rollback failures. 3) Added explicit rollback guidance: rollback entry points, expected time, health checks, and a decision tree for 'rollback vs fix-forward' based on stage. 4) Updated on-prem/air-gapped section with offline bundle verification expectations (checksums/signatures), mirror endpoints, and common operator mistakes. 5) Added an 'Upgrade artifacts for auditability' section describing what the installer persists (plan JSON, precheck results, backup IDs, execution log, audit events) and what to attach to support tickets. Notes: This PR intentionally does not introduce net-new policy; it documents the gating/checklist already being formalized elsewhere. Where behavior is still being finalized, wording is 'as of Private installer >= 1.9.0' and points to release notes. Checklist: - [x] Steps match installer CLI (plan/apply/rollback) - [x] Troubleshooting covers top 5 support cases (SUP-18421, SUP-18488, SUP-18510, SUP-18577) - [x] On-prem/air-gapped guidance includes bundle verification and offline registries - [x] Security reviewed language around backups/secrets (no plaintext exports, KMS/HSM expectations) - [x] Link check + docs build + Vale pass
commits: docs(private): rewrite upgrade procedure around plan/prechecks/backup/apply
docs(private): add rollback decision tree and post-rollback verification
docs(private): troubleshooting for precheck categories + remediation steps
docs(onprem): air-gapped bundle verification and offline mirror guidance
docs: add auditability artifacts section + support bundle pointers
chore: fix links, consolidate duplicated content, update TOCs
notable_snippets: Installer command flow (excerpt): redwood-private upgrade plan --target 1.12.0 --output /var/lib/redwood/upgrade/plan.json ; redwood-private upgrade precheck run --plan /var/lib/redwood/upgrade/plan.json ; redwood-private upgrade backup create --plan /var/lib/redwood/upgrade/plan.json ; redwood-private upgrade apply --plan /var/lib/redwood/upgrade/plan.json ; redwood-private upgrade verify --plan /var/lib/redwood/upgrade/plan.json

Rollback decision point (excerpt): If failure occurs before control-plane migrations complete, prefer rollback. If failure occurs after irreversible schema migration (plan marks step as non-reversible), stop and contact Redwood Support for manual intervention steps; do not repeatedly toggle apply/rollback.
source.review_conversation: Kira Thompson (requested changes): The procedure still mentions the old 'upgrade run' command in two places; please replace with plan/apply and ensure rollback references the stored upgrade state path. Also add a short 'what success looks like' section with concrete verification commands. Elliot Price (author): Fixed the remaining 'upgrade run' references, added a 'Success criteria' block under Post-upgrade verification (pods ready, API /healthz, control plane connectivity, sample inference request), and clarified the default state directory plus flag overrides. Connor O'Brien: Please call out that upgrades are supported N-1 -> N for GA, and that skipping minors is 'unsupported unless explicitly approved'. Also add a sentence about upgrade windows and release gating. Elliot Price: Added 'Supported upgrade paths' section and pointed to the release gating page; included language that multi-hop is not automated in v1. Rafael Mendes: Troubleshooting for migration lock timeouts should include the mitigation we used in staging (check for long-running transactions + retry after backoff), and the strong warning about not deleting migration jobs blindly. Elliot Price: Added a dedicated 'Migration lock timeout' page with safe mitigations and a 'do not' list. Fatima Noor: Air-gapped docs should include the exact failure mode we see: checksum mismatch due to proxy rewriting or partial downloads; and instructions to re-verify with sha256sum and re-sync bundle. Elliot Price: Added that scenario + remediation, plus a note about using the provided manifest file as the source of truth. Naomi Feldman (security): Please ensure we do not imply secrets are exported during backup; keep the language at 'references/handles' + KMS expectations. Elliot Price: Updated backup language to avoid suggesting plaintext secret export; added a short callout that secrets remain in K8s/secret store and backups capture refs/config + DB snapshots only. Approvals: Kira Thompson (approved), Connor O'Brien (approved), Rafael Mendes (approved), Fatima Noor (approved), Naomi Feldman (approved).
source.release_notes: Docs-only: Updated Redwood Private upgrade/rollback procedures to match new installer workflow; added troubleshooting for prechecks/migrations/artifacts; clarified supported upgrade paths (N-1 -> N) and rollback constraints.

source.title: Document Dedicated Burst: behavior, limits, denial reasons, and client retry guidance
source.author: Kira Thompson
release_notes: Adds customer-facing docs for Dedicated Burst (how it works, guardrails, and expected behaviors).
Documents denial reason codes and recommended retry/backpressure strategies.
Adds FAQ + runbook/dashboard pointers for Support/CS readiness.
source.description: Summary: This PR adds customer-facing documentation for the new Dedicated “Burst Capacity” option: what it is (borrowing temporary capacity from Redwood-managed burst pools), how limits/guardrails work, how to configure it (what’s controlled by Redwood vs what customers can tune), and what clients should do when burst is denied. Motivation: During beta we saw repeated support questions around (a) why burst was denied even when baseline looked “underused”, (b) what HTTP status / reason codes mean, and (c) how retries can accidentally amplify load or trigger double-counting concerns. This doc set aims to make behavior predictable and reduce time-to-resolution for incidents. Scope: Documented (1) baseline vs burst semantics, (2) per-tenant caps (max burst ratio, absolute cap), cooldown windows, and fleet safety caps at a high level, (3) denial reasons and what the platform does (fallback vs immediate backpressure), and (4) recommended client retry patterns with Retry-After handling + jitter and idempotency guidance. Non-goals: This does not document internal pool sizing policy, exact threshold numbers (these are contract/config dependent), or any cross-region bursting (not supported in initial GA). Notable behavior notes included in docs: Burst is best-effort and admission-controlled; baseline Dedicated capacity is not reduced by enabling burst. Burst is constrained to the same region and hardware class as the Dedicated baseline. When burst is denied, the platform returns a deterministic denial reason code and (when applicable) a Retry-After value; clients should not spin in tight loops. When the fleet safety circuit breaker is active, burst admissions may be broadly denied to protect non-burst SLOs. Customer guidance highlights: Prefer app-level smoothing (queues) for batch jobs; avoid synchronized retries across workers; use exponential backoff with full jitter; honor Retry-After; set an overall deadline; and ensure request idempotency keys are stable across retries. Checklists: Docs reviewed by Product + SRE; includes Support-facing “how to explain denials” section; includes example curl/SDK snippets; links to dashboards and runbook placeholders for CS enablement. Screenshots: None added in this PR (Console UI landing in a separate change); docs reference the Console location and API fields customers should expect to see. Testing: Docs build + link check in CI; manual preview in staging docs environment.
commits: docs(dedicated): add burst capacity overview + limits page
docs(api): document burst denial reason codes + HTTP behaviors
docs(guides): add retry/backpressure best practices and examples
docs(support): add FAQ + troubleshooting section
chore(docs): add redirects + nav updates
conversation: 2025-06-04 10:13 Kira Thompson (author): Added first pass docs for Dedicated Burst ahead of GA. Would love review on denial reason naming + retry guidance; tried to keep it consistent with what Routing returns today. Also included Support FAQ section based on SUP-28503 and recent enablement questions.

2025-06-04 12:02 Lauren Bishop: Looks good overall. Request: in the “Limits” section, please avoid implying specific numeric defaults (some customers have negotiated caps). Can we phrase as “configurable per contract” and list the categories (ratio, absolute cap, cooldown) instead?

2025-06-04 12:25 Kira Thompson (author): Yep, updated language to remove explicit numbers and added “configured per tenant/contract” callouts. Also added a note that Sales/CS can confirm caps in the order form / Console.

2025-06-05 09:18 Sean Gallagher: Retry guidance is strong. Two nits: (1) emphasize honoring Retry-After when present (we’ve added it in the gateway), and (2) include explicit anti-pattern callout for “retry immediately with same concurrency” because it worsens neighbor impact.
2025-06-05 09:44 Kira Thompson (author): Done. Added a highlighted anti-pattern box and a small snippet showing exponential backoff with jitter + Retry-After precedence.

2025-06-05 11:07 Mina Haddad: On denial semantics: the docs currently say “may queue” in one paragraph. My understanding is that for GA we’re doing immediate admission decision (no server-side queue) and clients handle backpressure. Please update to avoid implying an internal queue, and mention that streaming requests don’t get special treatment for admission (it’s per request).

2025-06-05 11:38 Kira Thompson (author): Great catch — removed “queue” wording, now says “immediate deny/backpressure” with client-side queuing recommended. Added note that admission is evaluated per request (including streaming) and customers should expect the same denial behavior.

title: Document Dedicated Burst: behavior, limits, denial reasons, and client retry guidance
preview: Summary: This PR adds customer-facing documentation for the new Dedicated “Burst Capacity” option: what it is (borrowing temporary capacity from Redwood-managed burst pools), how limits/guardrails work, how to configure it (what’s controlled by Redwood vs what customers can tune), and what clients should do when burst is denied. Motivation: During beta we saw repeated support questions around (a) why burst was denied even when baseline looked “underused”, (b) what HTTP status / reason codes mean, and (c) how retries can accidentally amplify load or trigger double-counting concerns. This doc set aims to make behavior predictable and reduce time-to-resolution for incidents. Scope: Documented (1) baseline vs burst semantics, (2) per-tenant caps (max burst ratio, absolute cap), cooldown windows, and fleet safety caps at a high level, (3) denial reasons and what the platform does (fallback vs immediate backpressure), and (4) recommended client retry patterns with Retry-After handling + jitter and idempotency guidance. Non-goals: This does not document internal pool sizing policy, exact threshold numbers (these are contract/config dependent), or any cross-region bursting (not supported in initial GA). Notable behavior notes included in docs: Burst is best-effort and admission-controlled; baseline Dedicated capacity is not reduced by enabling burst. Burst is constrained to the same region and hardware class as the Dedicated baseline. When burst is denied, the platform returns a deterministic denial reason code and (when applicable) a Retry-After value; clients should not spin in tight loops. When the fleet safety circuit breaker is active, burst admissions may be broadly denied to protect non-burst SLOs. Customer guidance highlights: Prefer app-level smoothing (queues) for batch jobs; avoid synchronized retries across workers; use exponential backoff with full jitter; honor Retry-After; set an overall deadline; and ensure request idempotency keys are stable across retries. Checklists: Docs reviewed by Product + SRE; includes Support-facing “how to explain denials” section; includes example curl/SDK snippets; links to dashboards and runbook placeholders for CS enablement. Screenshots: None added in this PR (Console UI landing in a separate change); docs reference the Console location and API fields customers should expect to see. Testing: Docs build + link check in CI; manual preview in staging docs environment.

source.title: Console docs: Hosted canary rollouts GA (concepts, workflow, API, troubleshooting)
source.author: Wei Chen
source.description: Summary: This PR refreshes and GA-izes our documentation for Hosted canary rollouts in Redwood Console. It consolidates beta-era notes into a single GA narrative, adds a step-by-step UI walkthrough (wizard + rollout details view), documents the relevant API surfaces at a conceptual level (with request/response snippets), and adds troubleshooting guidance aligned with the current orchestrator + policy guardrails + auto-stop behavior. Motivation: Support and design partners repeatedly asked for a single source-of-truth doc that explains (1) what a Hosted canary rollout is, (2) how percentage splits behave, (3) what guardrails/approvals exist, (4) what happens on SLO breach / auto-pause / rollback, and (5) how to debug split mismatches and “stuck” steps. This PR is intended to be the doc companion to the Hosted canary workflow GA effort. What’s included: (1) New docs page: “Hosted canary rollouts (GA)” covering core concepts (baseline vs canary, cohorting, step schedule, soak time, stop conditions), limitations, and FAQs; (2) Console walkthrough: Create rollout → Review & validate → Start → Progress step → Pause/Resume → Rollback, with screenshots callouts and copy consistent with the wizard; (3) Guardrails and approvals: explains max step size / minimum soak time / approval gates at a user-facing level, with examples of policy validation errors; (4) Observability: how to interpret baseline vs canary cohort metrics (latency p50/p95, error rate, timeouts) and how auto-stop decisions are made; (5) Troubleshooting: common issues (split mismatch, unexpected auto-pause, rollout cannot progress due to soak/approval) and what to collect for support (rollout_id, route, region, timestamps, activity log events); (6) Security/governance notes: audit log event types and what fields customers can expect to see; (7) Updated docs navigation + redirects from the beta page. Not included: Dedicated/Private rollout parity docs, automated quality-gate setup, and deep policy language reference (those remain follow-ups / internal docs). Screenshots: redacted tenant names + routes; updated to current Console build as of 2025-03-26. Testing: link checker, docs build, and snippet lint pass. Rollout: will publish with Console GA announcement; support macros will link to the troubleshooting section; release notes draft updated separately.
commits: docs(console/rollouts): add GA concepts page for Hosted canary rollouts
docs(console/rollouts): add wizard walkthrough + rollout details timeline section
docs(api): document rollout-related fields and query parameters (Hosted only)
docs(troubleshooting): add playbook for auto-pause/rollback + split mismatch
docs(nav): add sidebar entries + redirect beta canary docs to GA page
chore: screenshot refresh + alt text + copy alignment with Console strings
source.review_conversation: Elliot Price (review): The concepts page looks solid. Two nits: (1) we should be explicit that cohorting is deterministic but not guaranteed to be per end-user unless they provide a stable key; (2) “auto-rollback” vs “auto-pause” terminology is inconsistent across sections. Also, can we add a short “What data do we log?” callout in troubleshooting to reduce back-and-forth with support? Wei Chen (author): Updated the cohorting section to call out the default stickiness key (request hash inputs) and added guidance for providing a stable user/session identifier (when available) to get more consistent end-user experience. Also normalized language to “auto-stop” with outcomes “auto-pause” or “auto-rollback” depending on policy. Added a “Collect this for support” block with rollout_id, route, region, and timestamps + activity log export. Marissa Cole (review): The UI walkthrough matches the wizard flow, but the warning callouts should match current copy (esp. the rollback confirmation). Also can we mention where approvals show up (timeline vs modal) to set expectations? Wei Chen (author): Synced warning copy and updated screenshots from latest build. Added a note in the Approvals section: approval requests appear as a gated action in the step progression dialog and are recorded in the rollout activity timeline with actor + timestamp. Dylan Brooks (review): API page: please avoid implying these endpoints are public GA for everyone; we have tenant gating during staged rollout. Suggest phrasing: “available for eligible tenants; see enablement” and link to Console availability note. Also add a small disclaimer about region behavior (split is applied per-request in region after smart routing). Wei Chen (author): Updated wording to “available for eligible tenants” and added an Availability block. Added a “How splits are applied” note: route selection happens first, then canary split is enforced in the selected region using deterministic cohorting. Sean Gallagher (review): Troubleshooting: add guidance for interpreting short-term latency spikes (warm cache / cold start) and advise minimum soak times before escalating. Also include the default stop-condition signals we page on (5xx, timeouts, p95 regression) without giving exact thresholds (those vary). Wei Chen (author): Added a “Before you rollback” subsection describing warm-up effects and suggesting a minimum observation window aligned with configured soak time. Added a list of common stop-condition signals without embedding exact thresholds. Marcus Lin (review): Audit logging section: please state that audit log event fields vary by plan/retention and include an example event payload, but redact tenant IDs. Also make sure we don’t claim SIEM export in GA docs unless it’s actually enabled for Hosted tenants. Wei Chen (author): Added a redacted example event and adjusted wording to “audit logs can be exported via supported integrations where enabled” (no blanket SIEM claim). Also added a note about retention depending on plan.
source.merge_outcome: Merged via squash into main by Elliot Price. Post-merge: publish job scheduled with Console GA announcement bundle.
source.release_notes: Docs: Added GA documentation for Hosted canary rollouts including concepts, Console walkthrough, baseline vs canary metrics interpretation, audit logging overview, and troubleshooting for auto-pause/rollback and split mismatch scenarios.

source.title: Route experiments: add customer guide + stickiness key and metrics interpretation
source.author: Dylan Brooks
source.description: This PR adds the initial customer-facing documentation for Route experiments (route-level A/B/n testing) in Redwood Console Rollouts. It focuses on: (1) what route experiments are and when to use them, (2) how deterministic assignment works and how to choose/pass a stickiness key to avoid skew, (3) how to interpret the built-in metrics (latency/cost/error/throughput + optional quality signals) and guardrails in Console, and (4) troubleshooting common beta issues (missing attribution on streaming retries, propagation lag/stale results, skew when sticky key is low entropy). The guide is intentionally light on statistical significance claims; it documents basic deltas and guardrail thresholds only (per GA scope). It also documents headers and SDK helpers surfaced during beta so users can self-debug variant assignment. Includes updates to the navigation, terminology glossary, and a small FAQ entry on audit logs and RBAC. Related: ENG-9032 (docs), PM-482 (GA requirements). Support context: SUP-24877 (streaming attribution), SUP-24910 (stale results), SUP-24944 (audit export fields). Checklist: [x] New guide page + screenshots/placeholders [x] Stickiness key guidance + examples (Python/TS) [x] Metrics definitions (p50/p95/p99, cost attribution, error rate) [x] Troubleshooting + known pitfalls [x] Link to runbooks/Console UI concepts without exposing internal-only endpoints [x] Doc site nav + lint/link checks
commits: docs(route-experiments): add customer guide skeleton and nav entry
docs(route-experiments): add stickiness key guidance + SDK examples
docs(route-experiments): document metrics, guardrails, and common failure modes
docs: fix linkcheck failures + align header names with Console
changed_files: site/docs/glossary.mdx (add/adjust terms: stickiness key, attribution, variant)
ci_checks: spellcheck: PASS (2 words added to dictionary: 'stickiness', 'bucketing')
review_conversation: Marissa Cole (Product): "Thanks for getting this in before beta expansion. Two requests: (1) make it explicit that we do not provide statistical significance in GA; use 'directional deltas' wording. (2) In the 'Guardrails' section, clarify soft-stop vs optional hard-stop and the presence of a kill switch (without naming internal flags)." Dylan Brooks (Author): "Updated 'How to read results' to say 'directional deltas' and added a callout that significance testing is not included in GA. Also reworked Guardrails to describe default soft-stop + notification and that some orgs can enable automatic stop with admin controls; removed internal flag names."

Logan Wright (Telemetry): "Metrics section: please don’t imply cost is exact per-request. We should describe cost attribution as aggregated from token usage + pricing model, and mention that results can lag due to aggregation windows. Also: the header names in the examples don’t match what we emit; we use `x-redwood-experiment-id` and `x-redwood-variant-id` (lowercase in docs is fine), plus `x-redwood-sticky-key` is request-side only." Dylan Brooks (Author): "Fixed header names, added a 'Cost attribution' subsection (aggregated, windowed, may lag). Added a 'Results freshness' callout: typical delay 5–15 minutes depending on dataset + region."

Jordan Lee (Policy Engine): "Stickiness key guidance is good, but please add a warning about low-entropy defaults (e.g., missing user id causing many requests to share the same key). Also clarify precedence: explicit sticky key > customer_id > request_id (not stable)." Dylan Brooks (Author): "Added 'Anti-patterns' section: IP address, request_id, empty string. Added precedence text and a recommendation to always provide an end-user or session identifier."

Tessa Morgan (Design): "The screenshots are placeholders; can we add a note that UI labels may differ slightly by release channel? Also please keep the table widths reasonable on mobile (the metrics table is huge)." Dylan Brooks (Author): "Added a note under screenshots about minor UI label differences during phased rollout. Reworked the metrics table into smaller tables per metric family + collapsible sections."

Kaitlyn Nguyen (RBAC/Authz): "In RBAC section, avoid suggesting that 'any admin can stop experiments'—in some orgs it’s restricted. Please link to RBAC permissions doc and list the specific permissions we expect." Dylan Brooks (Author): "Updated to reference the RBAC doc and list permissions at a high level (view/create/update/start/stop/delete). Removed the 'any admin' phrasing."
Docs CI (bot): "link-check failed: /rollouts/route-experiments#variant-headers returned 404" Dylan Brooks (Author): "Fixed anchor mismatch + reran CI."
Marissa Cole: "LGTM after wording changes."
Logan Wright: "Approved once cost/lag caveats were added."
Jordan Lee: "Approved."
Tessa Morgan: "Approved."
Kaitlyn Nguyen: "Approved."

source.title: Incident Bot v2 operator guide + troubleshooting (deploy/config diff/saturation cards)
source.author: Dylan Brooks
source.description: This PR updates the operator-facing documentation for Incident Bot v2 diagnostics (GA-ready). The goal is to make the bot usable in the first 5 minutes of an incident by standardizing what each context card means, how to validate it, and what to do when it’s missing/empty due to permissions or upstream timeouts. The guide also documents degraded-mode behavior (cached context + links) and the security model for tenant-safe config diffs (redaction + audit logging + break-glass). Scope: docs only (no product/runtime changes). Motivation: recent incident retros and the beta rotation feedback showed that responders could see the cards but weren’t sure what thresholds/links were authoritative, and we repeatedly re-taught the same troubleshooting steps in #help. This adds a single canonical “Operator guide” that Support/SRE/On-call can link during active incidents. Contents: (1) Overview of v2 cards (Recent deploys / Config diff / Saturation signals / Error budget burn links). (2) Interpretation + common pitfalls: staged rollouts, partial propagation, canary vs full, tenant pinning, routing policy interactions, and why GPU% alone is not actionable without queue depth and admission/backpressure. (3) Troubleshooting playbook: empty cards, permission denied, redaction warnings, telemetry query timeouts, and how to switch to link-only mode. (4) Deep link standards into Console (service/region/time window/tenant safe params) and how to widen/narrow time ranges. (5) Security notes: what the bot is allowed to show in Slack, how to request elevated access, and what audit events are emitted. (6) Rollout notes: feature-flag cohorts, GA announcement template, and rollback criteria (docs references only; implementation lives elsewhere).
commits: docs(incident-bot): add Incident Bot v2 operator guide and card reference
docs(incident-response): add troubleshooting for empty cards, timeouts, and degraded mode
docs(security): document config diff redaction, break-glass, and audit events
docs(console): standardize incident deep links and query parameters
chore: tighten terminology and add glossary (tenant/service/route/pool)
source.release_notes: Operator documentation for Incident Bot v2 diagnostics is now available (internal + customer-safe guidance). Includes troubleshooting for empty cards, telemetry timeouts, degraded mode, and permissions/redaction behavior.
review_conversation: Rafael Mendes: Please add a very explicit “what to do when the bot is slow” section. During incidents we saw folks hammer the slash command which made query load worse. Also call out the ack/retry behavior so people don’t assume it’s stuck.

Dylan Brooks (author): Added a “Bot is slow / timing out” subsection with: don’t spam the command, wait for retry, and use deep links + cached context. Also added a note that the bot will post partial cards as sources return, then update the message.

Allison Grant: On saturation signals, can we avoid suggesting absolute GPU% thresholds? We’ve seen GPU pinned without impact when queue depth is low. Recommend leading with queue depth + admission/backpressure, then GPU/KV cache as supporting signals.
Dylan Brooks (author): Updated ordering: queue depth + admission/backpressure first, then GPU util, then KV cache pressure. Also added “GPU high but queue low” as a non-incident example.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: Please be very careful with the config diff section. We should explicitly state that Slack output is customer/tenant-safe and that full diffs require elevated access (and are audited). Also add the error message responders will see when scope is missing so they can self-diagnose.

Dylan Brooks (author): Added explicit language on redaction guarantees + limitations, plus the exact error strings for missing scopes and the break-glass request path. Included a reminder that screenshots of privileged views should not be posted in shared channels.

Chloe Martin: Deep links: can we standardize on time window defaults and include an example link template? Also call out that the Console link should land on the service+region preselected, not global.

Dylan Brooks (author): Added link templates and default windows (T-30m..now for incident start; T-2h..now for saturation). Clarified required parameters (service, region, env, route optional) and that tenant is only included for tenant-safe views.

Miles Thornton: Minor: add a quick “card glossary” explaining terms used in the bot output (rollout phase, canary health, KV cache pressure). Also add a note on how to interpret redaction markers so people don’t assume data corruption.
Dylan Brooks (author): Added glossary and a short section on redaction markers (REDACTED, HASHED, OMITTED) and why they occur.
Rafael Mendes: Approved once the “degraded mode” section clearly states that it’s expected under partial outages and that links are the primary tool when telemetry is unhealthy.
Dylan Brooks (author): Done; degraded mode now has a banner callout and a short decision tree.
Allison Grant: LGTM.
Kaitlyn Nguyen: LGTM with the final wording changes.
Miles Thornton: LGTM.
source.notes_for_rollout: Docs are written to match GA behavior behind the incident-bot v2 feature flag. If GA is delayed, we can keep the nav entry labeled “Beta” by reverting the sidebar change only. The troubleshooting guide includes the current known error strings from prod; if those change, update docs in the same PR as message changes to avoid drift.

This adds a single canonical “Operator guide” that Support/SRE/On-call can link during active incidents. Contents: (1) Overview of v2 cards (Recent deploys / Config diff / Saturation signals / Error budget burn links). (2) Interpretation + common pitfalls: staged rollouts, partial propagation, canary vs full, tenant pinning, routing policy interactions, and why GPU% alone is not actionable without queue depth and admission/backpressure. (3) Troubleshooting playbook: empty cards, permission denied, redaction warnings, telemetry query timeouts, and how to switch to link-only mode. (4) Deep link standards into Console (service/region/time window/tenant safe params) and how to widen/narrow time ranges. (5) Security notes: what the bot is allowed to show in Slack, how to request elevated access, and what audit events are emitted. (6) Rollout notes: feature-flag cohorts, GA announcement template, and rollback criteria (docs references only; implementation lives elsewhere).

source.title: Private: publish backup/restore guide + DR checklists + troubleshooting
source.author: Elliot Price
source.release_notes: Docs-only: Added customer-facing Private backup/restore guide, DR checklists (VPC + on-prem/air-gapped), and troubleshooting/FAQ. Clarified encryption model (envelope encryption), secret handling (no plaintext secrets), and validation/smoke test steps.
source.description:
This PR publishes customer-consumable documentation for Redwood Private backup/restore and disaster recovery (DR), aligned with the Day-2 operations toolkit shipping in the private installer and Helm charts. The goal is to give enterprise operators a repeatable path to: (1) configure backup storage securely, (2) take consistent control-plane backups, (3) perform restores into a clean or existing cluster, and (4) validate DR readiness via checklists and periodic restore drills.

What’s included (new/updated docs pages):
- Private Backup & Restore Guide (end-to-end): prerequisites, supported storage backends, encryption/key management, backup create/verify flow, retention, and operational recommendations.
- DR checklists by archetype:
  - VPC single-region restore into clean cluster (same region): DNS/TLS considerations, restore ordering, post-restore validation.
  - On-prem / air-gapped DR: offline bundle expectations, checksum/signature verification, and restore flow without outbound dependencies.
- Troubleshooting + FAQ: common failure modes (RBAC, missing CRDs, slow object stores/NFS timeouts, clock skew), what is and isn’t backed up, and support boundaries.

Key clarifications (based on SRE + Security review):
- Secrets: backups must not contain plaintext secret material. Documentation explicitly states we back up references/metadata (and required config state) and rely on customer-managed secret stores/KMS for secret material. Where a secret must exist (e.g., restore job credentials), the docs call out operator action and validation steps.
- Encryption: artifacts are encrypted at rest; envelope encryption is required when KMS/HSM is available. The guide describes key ownership, rotation expectations, and what to do if KMS is temporarily unavailable.
- Scope boundaries: the guide focuses on control-plane state + critical configuration for deterministic redeploy. Data plane is treated as stateless; we document what config must be captured to redeploy deterministically (values files, model routing policies, etc.) and explicitly call out what is out of scope.

Operator flows documented (commands are examples; exact flags may vary by version):
- Create backup: rwi backup create --storage s3://<bucket>/<prefix> --encryption kms --manifest-out <path>
- Verify backup integrity: rwi backup verify --manifest <path>
- Restore: rwi restore apply --manifest <path> --target-cluster <context> --validate

Cross-links / references:
- Links to the internal runbooks (for Redwood SRE/Solutions) and to the public-facing pages as the source of truth for customers.
- Links to Velero integration guidance (resource include/exclude philosophy and coordination with Redwood snapshots), with explicit warnings to avoid duplicative/unsafe secret capture.

Why now:
- Support escalation SUP-18421 requested an official customer-facing runbook.
- On-prem customer restore issue SUP-18477 highlighted missing CRD/ordering guidance and pre-flight checks.

Follow-ups (not blocking this PR):
- Add an “RPO/RTO matrix” page once Product finalizes default targets per archetype.
- Add a short video walkthrough for restore validation and DR drills.

Screenshots:
- Included in docs preview (Netlify) as embedded images for the DR checklist pages.

Docs preview:
- Netlify deploy posted by CI (see checks).

docs(private): add restore procedure + validation checklist and rollback criteria

docs(private): add troubleshooting matrix (RBAC, CRDs, storage latency, clock skew)
docs(security): clarify secret handling and envelope encryption terminology
chore(docs): fix broken links, add redirects, unify terminology (control plane vs data plane)
review_conversation: Kira Thompson (review): Restore steps look solid, but the validation section needs a tighter, enumerated checklist (what to check in UI/API, expected statuses, and when to abort). Also add a callout that operators should snapshot Helm values/manifest pins used for the control plane.

Elliot Price (author): Added a dedicated “Validate restore” page with step-by-step checks (control plane API health, tenant config presence, routing policy sanity, and a minimal inference smoke test) and added a callout to store Helm values + installer config alongside the backup manifest.

Sean Gallagher (review): For on-call readiness, please add clear paging thresholds guidance (e.g., missed backup window, restore drill failure) but keep it customer-friendly. Also please add a troubleshooting entry for RBAC denial on CronJob service account (we hit this in staging).

Elliot Price (author): Added “Operational recommendations” and “Restore drill cadence” sections (no paging policy specifics, but recommended alert conditions). Added RBAC denial troubleshooting with common verbs/resources and a note to compare against the Helm chart RBAC template.

Marcus Lin (review): Strongly recommend we avoid implying that backups ever include secrets. Current wording in FAQ is ambiguous (“secrets references”). Please explicitly state: no plaintext secret material is included; only references/metadata are captured; customer must ensure secret stores/KMS are available at restore time. Also add a short threat model note on artifact access control.

Elliot Price (author): Updated FAQ + overview to explicitly say no plaintext secret material is included. Added an “Access controls” section (least privilege, separate backup credentials, and audit logging recommendations).

Hanae Suzuki (review): In encryption section, call out KMS downtime behavior: backup should fail closed (no unencrypted artifact). Also mention key rotation expectations (keep old keys accessible for restore until retention window expires).
Elliot Price (author): Added “KMS availability” and “Key rotation” subsections, explicitly stating fail-closed behavior and rotation guidance.

Markus Klein (review): Add a customer-facing “Support boundaries” note: Support can help interpret errors/validation output, but won’t manage customer KMS/IAM policies. Also include a simple pre-flight checklist we can point customers to in tickets.
Elliot Price (author): Added “Support boundaries” callout and added a short “Pre-flight checklist” at the start of create-backup and restore pages.

Fatima Noor (review): Air-gapped checklist needs explicit offline artifact requirements: signed bundle, checksum manifest, and no external calls. Also highlight the common ‘missing CRDs’ issue (SUP-18477) and how to verify required CRDs exist before restore.
Elliot Price (author): Added offline bundle/signature verification steps and a CRD pre-flight section with an example command and failure message patterns.
Vivek Kulkarni (approval): Approved with the updated scope language. Please ensure we don’t hard-commit to specific RPO/RTO numbers until the matrix is published.
Elliot Price (author): Removed any numeric RPO/RTO claims; replaced with “customer-defined targets” and pointers to upcoming matrix page.
ci_checks: spellcheck: pass (2 words added to dictionary: 'air-gapped', 'Velero')
source.merge_outcome: Merged to main; Netlify production deploy completed successfully. Redirects applied for prior internal-only backup/restore draft URLs.

source.title: Document retention controls + compliance reporting pack request flow (incl. FAQ updates)
source.author: Elliot Price
source.release_notes: Docs-only: Adds public-facing retention controls overview, limitations/FAQ, and instructions to request the baseline compliance reporting pack (report contents + turnaround expectations).
source.description:
Summary: This PR publishes initial external documentation for the Retention controls + compliance reporting pack workstream. It adds a retention controls overview (supported data classes, tier defaults, and how to configure), documents limitations and operational semantics (TTL vs purge, change history, verification), and updates the Security FAQ with customer-ready answers for security questionnaires.

Motivation: Enterprise security reviews (Dedicated/Private) are consistently asking for (a) configurable retention by data class and (b) a standardized evidence package for audit logs + retention configuration. Prior docs were a mix of internal Confluence and ad-hoc SE answers; this makes the baseline story consistent and reduces back-and-forth.

What’s included:
- New docs page: “Retention controls” covering supported data classes (request/response logs where enabled, audit logs, traces/metrics retention, and artifacts where applicable), what Redwood does by default by deployment tier, and how customers change retention (Console + API + Terraform for Private where supported).
- New docs page: “Retention controls limitations & semantics” clarifying: TTL enforcement is asynchronous; deletes are idempotent; retention controls apply to stored telemetry/logging data (not model weights); and short retention may reduce debugging history unless a documented break-glass exception is approved.
- Updated Security FAQ entries: “Do you support configurable retention?”, “Can you provide an audit log export?”, “What is included in the compliance reporting pack?”, “How do you handle legal hold / incident holds?”
- New section: “Requesting the compliance reporting pack” describing how to request the baseline package (via Support ticket or AE/SE), expected content (audit-log coverage summary, retention configuration export, data residency/region summary, RBAC/access report, evidence checklist), and typical turnaround for a specified time window.

Notes / wording constraints:
- Avoids making certification claims (no “SOC 2 certified” language); uses “aligned to SOC 2 / ISO-style controls” and “customer evidence pack” phrasing.
- Legal hold wording intentionally scoped: describes the concept and that holds are tracked/audited, but does not publish internal approval matrix details.
- Includes explicit caveats for Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private (e.g., some stores may be customer-managed in Private; retention enforcement depends on configured backing services and installed purge jobs).

Follow-ups (not in scope for this PR):
- Add screenshot annotations once the Console retention settings page is GA.
- Add a dedicated “Report pack format/versioning” page once the export format ADR lands in public docs.

Review checklist:
- [x] Security reviewed customer-facing terminology (audit logs vs request logs)
- [x] GRC reviewed evidence pack section headings
- [x] CS validated request flow and expectations
- [x] Docs build/link check green

docs(security): add retention semantics/limitations (ttl vs purge, async behavior)
docs(faq): update security faq entries for retention + audit logs + compliance pack
docs(compliance): add 'requesting the compliance reporting pack' section
chore(docs): fix broken anchors, add redirects, run formatter
changed_files: site/docs/security/audit-logging.mdx (updated: cross-links to report pack + export guidance)
site/docs/_redirects (updated: add legacy retention link redirects)
review_thread: Naomi Feldman (2025-02-25): Please add an explicit statement that retention controls apply to stored telemetry/log data and not to model weights, and clarify Hosted vs Dedicated differences for request/response logs (since logging can be disabled).
Elliot Price (2025-02-25): Added a “Scope” callout in the overview + a Hosted/Dedicated/Private table noting request/response logs are only retained when enabled by tenant logging settings.
Marcus Lin (2025-02-26): Watch the phrasing on legal hold. Don’t publish the internal approval matrix; keep it high-level and emphasize auditability. Also: avoid “purge is immediate” language.

Elliot Price (2025-02-26): Updated language to “asynchronous deletion” and “time-bounded enforcement”; replaced legal-hold section with a brief explanation that holds are tracked and audited and may override deletion within permitted policy boundaries.

Olga Petrov (2025-02-27): The compliance pack section should match the baseline contents we’ve been using in reviews: include retention configuration export, audit-log coverage summary, data residency summary, RBAC/access control report, and an evidence checklist. Also add a short note on specifying the desired time window for exports.
Elliot Price (2025-02-27): Aligned the bullet list, added a “What we need from you” subsection (tenant/org, region(s), time window, and point of contact).

Rachel Kim (2025-02-28): Request flow: please route self-serve customers to Support first and enterprise customers via AE/SE is fine. Also add an expectation on turnaround time (e.g., 3–5 business days) with caveat depending on scope.
Elliot Price (2025-02-28): Added a request options table (Support vs AE/SE) and a typical timeline statement + caveat for multi-region / long time windows.

Kira Thompson (2025-03-01): Nit: there’s a claim that reports are available “on-demand.” We should say “available upon request” until the generator service is GA. Also a couple of broken anchors in the sidebar.
Elliot Price (2025-03-01): Changed “on-demand” to “upon request” everywhere; fixed sidebar anchors and added redirects for the old internal link slug.
Marcus Lin (2025-03-02): Final pass looks good. Please add one line that short retention may impact incident debugging and that break-glass exceptions may be available per contract/policy.
Elliot Price (2025-03-02): Added a short “Operational considerations” note in limitations/semantics with break-glass wording reviewed by Security.
Naomi Feldman (2025-03-03): Approved after wording changes. Ship it.

source.title: Docs: add observability hooks reference for quickstart templates
source.author: Elliot Price
source.release_notes: Add a reference page documenting the required tracing/metrics emitted by Quickstart Templates and how they map to Redwood Console dashboards (including cardinality/redaction guidance).
source.description:
Title: Docs: add observability hooks reference for quickstart templates

Context
We’re shipping production-minded RAG + agent quickstart templates that emit standardized OpenTelemetry signals plus Redwood-specific conventions. Support/SRE feedback has been consistent: we need a canonical reference page (1) defining the required spans/attributes/metrics/log fields, and (2) mapping those to Redwood Console dashboards so users can verify instrumentation quickly and understand what they’re looking at.

What this PR does
- Adds a new docs reference page: "Observability hooks (reference)" for Quickstart Templates.
- Documents required spans + span attributes (including what is REQUIRED vs RECOMMENDED).
- Documents required metrics (names, types, units, and allowed dimensions) and includes a “cardinality guardrails” section aligned with Observability/SRE guidance.
- Adds a “Mapping to Redwood Console” section that calls out which dashboards/panels use which signals (Templates Overview, RAG Latency Breakdown, Tool Failures, Retrieval Quality, Token Economics).
- Adds a small “How to validate locally” checklist (view trace tree, confirm metrics are flowing, confirm request-id correlation).

Non-goals
- This does not introduce new instrumentation in templates/SDKs; it’s documentation only.
- This does not document every possible OTel semantic convention; we focus on the minimum set needed to make templates supportable and dashboards functional.

Docs IA / links
- Linked from the Quickstart Templates landing page and the RAG+Agent walkthrough.
- Cross-linked from the Troubleshooting page ("No traces in Console?" and "Missing tool spans?").

New/updated content (high level)
1) Tracing
- Required span hierarchy (expected parent/child relationships):
  - http.server (or equivalent framework span)
    - redwood.llm.request
      - redwood.rag.retrieve (optional for non-RAG)
      - redwood.agent.iteration (0..N)
        - redwood.tool.<tool_name> (0..N)
- Required span attributes (REQUIRED):
  - redwood.request_id (from API response header when available; used for support correlation)
  - llm.system = "redwood" (or llm.provider = "redwood"; chosen term documented for consistency)
  - llm.model (pinned model identifier)
  - llm.operation ("chat" | "responses" | "embeddings" | "rerank")
  - http.response.status_code (for the client call span)
  - error.type (normalized error taxonomy keys)
- Recommended attributes (RECOMMENDED) with cardinality cautions:
  - redwood.deployment_mode ("hosted" | "dedicated" | "private")
  - redwood.region
  - redwood.template.name / redwood.template.version (for template-originated traffic tagging)
  - tool.name (low-cardinality) and tool.result (DO NOT log raw output)
- Redaction rules:
  - Explicitly prohibit recording prompt/user content as span attributes.
  - Recommend hashing document IDs if they must be included.
  - Provide examples of safe logging fields.

2) Metrics
- Required metrics (names, types, and units):
  - redwood.llm.latency_ms (histogram)
  - redwood.llm.tokens.input (counter)
  - redwood.llm.tokens.output (counter)
  - redwood.llm.requests (counter)
  - redwood.llm.errors (counter)
  - redwood.rag.retrieval.latency_ms (histogram)
  - redwood.rag.retrieval.results_count (histogram)
  - redwood.tool.calls (counter)
  - redwood.tool.errors (counter)
- Required metric dimensions (allowed list):
  - llm.model, llm.operation, redwood.template.name, redwood.template.version
- Forbidden/high-risk dimensions (explicit):
  - user_id, session_id, request_id, full URL paths, document titles, arbitrary tool args

3) Logs
- Minimal structured log fields to align with support workflows:
  - level, message, timestamp
  - redwood.request_id
  - error.type, error.message (redacted)
  - llm.model, llm.operation
  - tool.name (if relevant)

4) Mapping to Redwood Console
- Dashboard: Templates Overview
  - Uses redwood.template.name/version + redwood.llm.requests + redwood.llm.latency_ms
- Dashboard: Token Economics
  - Uses redwood.llm.tokens.input/output + latency histograms for cost/latency analysis
- Dashboard: Tool Failures
  - Uses redwood.tool.errors + tool spans with error.type/tool.name
- Dashboard: RAG Latency Breakdown
  - Uses redwood.rag.retrieval.latency_ms span timing + llm request span timing

Why this matters
- Reduces "is it instrumented correctly?" support load.
- Makes template dashboards immediately actionable.
- Prevents accidental high-cardinality tags and prompt/PII leakage.

Implementation notes
- Content is aligned with internal standards discussed with Allison/Logan (span naming + attribute set) and SRE feedback (cardinality guardrails, redaction language).
- Added a small admonition block where we previously saw proxy issues with opt-in telemetry headers; the reference clarifies that template tagging is optional and must not include user data.

Screenshots
- N/A (docs-only). Included example trace tree and metric table inline.

Testing
- `pnpm lint`
- `pnpm test:links`
- `pnpm build`

Follow-ups
- After merge, we should link this page from the SDK instrumentation guide (Wei is tracking separately) and ensure the observability pack dashboard names match the final docs strings.


Commits
1) docs(quickstarts): add observability hooks reference page
2) docs(quickstarts): link reference from templates landing + walkthrough
3) docs(observability): add metric tables + cardinality guidance
